title: 'Best X' Listicle Writer
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a helpful 'best tools/products' listicle that ranks and converts.
prompt:
Role: You are an affiliate/SEO content writer.

Task: Write a 'Best [CATEGORY] for [AUDIENCE/USE]' listicle targeting [KEYWORD], featuring [ITEMS or 'suggest 7–10']. Give honest mini-reviews with real differentiation, not filler.

Format: Title + meta; intro with a quick top-3 verdict box; for each item — what it is, best for, pros/cons, pricing note; a comparison table; how we picked; FAQ; conclusion with top pick.

title: 'X vs Y' Comparison Page Writer
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a fair, conversion-aware comparison page targeting 'vs' keywords.
prompt:
Role: You are a BOFU content writer who converts comparison traffic.

Task: Write a comparison page for [MY PRODUCT] vs [COMPETITOR] targeting the keyword [X vs Y]. Be genuinely fair — credible beats biased — while showing where we win for [IDEAL USER].

Format: SEO title + meta; intro with the quick verdict; a comparison table; section-by-section breakdown; 'choose us if / choose them if'; FAQ (3 Qs); CTA.

title: 1:1 Meeting Agenda & Questions
author: PromptDeck
description: Structures recurring 1:1s that surface problems early and grow people.
prompt:
Role: You are a leadership coach.

Task: Design my recurring 1:1 with [REPORT/ROLE], [CADENCE], current context [SITUATION: new hire, high performer plateauing, struggling, etc.]. The 1:1 is their meeting — I mostly listen.

Format: A standing agenda (their topics first); 10 rotating questions grouped by purpose (unblock, engagement, growth, feedback both ways); early-warning questions that surface problems; what to record after each session; and 3 anti-patterns to avoid.

title: 30-Day Social Content Calendar
author: PromptDeck
description: Builds a full month of platform-specific social posts with hooks, formats, and CTAs.
prompt:
Role: You are a social media manager and content strategist.

Task: Create a 30-day content calendar for [BRAND/NICHE] on [PLATFORM] with the goal [GROW FOLLOWERS/DRIVE SALES] in brand voice [VOICE]. Rotate 4 pillars — educational, entertaining, promotional, community — and front-load the strongest hooks.

Format: A markdown table — Day | Pillar | Post Format | Hook | Caption Idea | CTA | Hashtags. After the table, suggest 3 recurring series to build consistency.

title: 30-Second Audio Ad Script
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a 30-second radio/podcast ad optimized for the ear.
prompt:
Role: You are an audio advertising copywriter.

Task: Write a 30-second ad (~75 words) for [PRODUCT] airing on [PODCAST GENRE/STATION] for [AUDIENCE], with offer [OFFER/URL/PROMO CODE]. Write for the ear: simple words, rhythm, repetition of the memorable bit.

Format: The script with [PAUSE]/[EMPHASIS] cues; the URL/code repeated twice naturally; plus a 15-second cutdown and a host-read talking-points version.

title: 5-Email Welcome / Nurture Sequence
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a full automated email sequence that builds trust toward a purchase.
prompt:
Role: You are an email marketing strategist.

Task: Write a 5-email welcome sequence for new subscribers of [BUSINESS] who joined via [LEAD MAGNET], leading to [OFFER]. Human and warm, not salesy.

Format: For each email — purpose, subject line (+1 alt), preview text, body (≤200 words, one CTA). Arc: (1) welcome + value; (2) story/relatability; (3) teach + soft mention; (4) social proof + objection; (5) direct offer with a reason to act now.

title: 60-Second Elevator Pitch
author: PromptDeck
description: Crafts a spoken elevator pitch plus 10-second and tweet-length versions.
prompt:
Role: You are a pitch coach.

Task: Write an elevator pitch for [PERSON/COMPANY/PRODUCT] that does [WHAT] for [WHO], solving [PROBLEM], differentiated by [DIFFERENTIATOR], with pitch goal [INVESTMENT/CUSTOMERS/JOB]. Written to be said out loud.

Format: Three versions — a 10-second one-liner; a tweet-length version; a 60-second spoken pitch (hook, problem, solution, proof, clear ask).

title: 60-Second Explainer Video Script
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a tight explainer script that makes a product instantly clear.
prompt:
Role: You are an explainer-video scriptwriter.

Task: Write a ~60-second script for [PRODUCT] that makes it instantly clear to [AUDIENCE]: the problem, the solution, how it works, and why it's better.

Format: A two-column script — Voiceover | On-screen/visual — timed in short beats, ending on a clear CTA. Keep the voiceover conversational and jargon-free (~150 words).

title: 8-Touch Outbound Sequence
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs a full multi-channel outbound sequence over 3 weeks.
prompt:
Role: You are an outbound sequence designer.

Task: Design an 8-touch, ~3-week sequence for [PRODUCT] targeting [ICP ROLE] with pain [PAIN]. Mix email, call, LinkedIn; every touch adds new value or angle — never 'just bumping this'.

Format: A table — Day | Channel | Angle | Message (full copy for emails/LinkedIn, talk track for calls). Include one pattern-interrupt touch and a clean break-up. Note the reply-handling branch for 'interested' and 'not now'.

title: 90-Second VSL / Promo Video Script
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a short video sales script with hook, story, proof, and CTA.
prompt:
Role: You are a video sales letter writer.

Task: Write a ~90-second VSL script for [PRODUCT] for [AUDIENCE] with problem [PROBLEM]. Spoken language, short sentences, momentum throughout.

Format: Two columns — Voiceover | Visual cue — through: pattern-interrupt hook (0–5s), problem empathy, the shift/mechanism, proof, offer, CTA with reason to act now. ~220 words of VO max.

title: A/B Test Design Variants
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs meaningfully different variants for a UI A/B test.
prompt:
Role: You are a CRO designer.

Task: Design variants to test on [PAGE/ELEMENT] where the metric is [METRIC] and the hypothesis is [WHY IT UNDERPERFORMS]. Variants must differ meaningfully — button-color tests waste traffic.

Format: The control described; 2–3 variants each with the hypothesis it tests, what changes, and the psychology behind it; the success metric + guardrail metrics; rough traffic/duration sanity check; and what we learn from each possible outcome (including a flat result).

title: Accessibility (a11y) Review
author: PromptDeck
description: Reviews a UI description or markup for accessibility issues with fixes.
prompt:
Role: You are an accessibility specialist versed in WCAG 2.2.

Task: Review the UI described or the markup below for accessibility issues (contrast, keyboard nav, focus, labels, semantics, alt text, ARIA misuse) and prioritize fixes.

Format: A table — Issue | WCAG criterion | Severity | Fix. Then the top 3 to fix first. Input: [PASTE UI DESCRIPTION OR MARKUP].

title: Accessible & Inclusive Support Replies
author: PromptDeck
description: Adapts support communication for accessibility and language barriers.
prompt:
Role: You are an inclusive-communication specialist.

Task: Adapt our support approach for customers who [SITUATION: non-native speakers, screen-reader users, cognitive load, elderly users]. Rewrite this reply as an example: [PASTE REPLY].

Format: The rewritten reply (plain language, one instruction per sentence, no idioms, numbered steps); the general guidelines list for this audience; format alternatives to offer (video/screenshots/call); and the phrases to ban because they don't translate or read poorly.

title: Active-Recall Study Quiz
author: PromptDeck
description: Turns notes or a topic into a spaced, active-recall quiz with explanations.
prompt:
Role: You are a learning scientist who designs retrieval practice.

Task: Create an active-recall quiz on [TOPIC or paste notes] for [LEVEL], ordered easy to hard.

Format: 12 questions — 5 multiple-choice, 4 short-answer, 3 'explain why'. Put answers with brief explanations below a divider. End with the 3 concepts to review again. Notes/topic: [PASTE HERE].

title: Ad Copy Variations (Meta / Google)
author: PromptDeck
description: Produces multiple ad-copy variations with different angles for A/B testing.
prompt:
Role: You are a performance marketing copywriter.

Task: Write ad copy for [PRODUCT/SERVICE] targeting [AUDIENCE] on [PLATFORM] with goal [CONVERSIONS/CLICKS/LEADS], key benefit [BENEFIT], offer [OFFER]. Create 5 variations with different angles: pain, desire, social proof, curiosity, urgency.

Format: For each variation — primary text (≤125 chars where relevant), headline (≤40 chars), description. Add 3 CTA button ideas. Note which angle to test first and why.

title: Ad Jingle / Brand Song Lyrics
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes catchy, original jingle lyrics for a brand or campaign.
prompt:
Role: You are a jingle writer for advertising.

Task: Write original, catchy jingle lyrics for [BRAND] promoting [PRODUCT/MESSAGE] to [AUDIENCE], with a [MOOD] mood. Work the brand name and one key benefit into a hook that's easy to remember. Keep it original (do not imitate any existing song).

Format: A short jingle — a repeatable hook/chorus plus 1–2 short verses, with a note on suggested tempo and feel.

title: Adapt One Prompt Across AI Models
author: PromptDeck
description: Tunes a prompt for the quirks of different AI models.
prompt:
Role: You are a prompt engineer who works across model families.

Task: Adapt my prompt for different AI models: [PASTE PROMPT]. Explain where model behaviors diverge (instruction-following strictness, verbosity, formatting habits, refusal sensitivity) and tune per model family.

Format: The base prompt (model-agnostic best version); per-family tweaks (reasoning models: less hand-holding; chat models: more structure) with the changed lines highlighted; a quick test to compare outputs; and which parts of prompting are universal.

title: Add Clear Comments & Docstrings
author: PromptDeck
description: Adds helpful comments and docstrings to code without changing logic.
prompt:
Role: You are an engineer who writes comments that explain 'why', not 'what'.

Task: Add comments and docstrings to the code below in [LANGUAGE], following [DOC STYLE, e.g. JSDoc/Google]. Explain intent and non-obvious decisions; don't state the obvious or change logic.

Format: The fully commented code, then a one-line note on any part that looks confusing and might deserve refactoring. Code: [PASTE CODE].

title: Add Step-by-Step Reasoning to a Prompt
author: PromptDeck
description: Restructures a task so the model reasons step by step for more accurate answers.
prompt:
Role: You are a prompt engineer specializing in reasoning tasks.

Task: Take the task [TASK] and restructure it so the model works through it step by step before answering, reducing errors. Define the reasoning steps explicitly.

Format: (1) a rewritten prompt that instructs an explicit step-by-step method with a final answer section; (2) the reasoning checklist it should follow; (3) a note on when to hide vs show the reasoning.

title: Advisor Recruitment Outreach
author: PromptDeck
description: Recruits an advisor with a clear, respectful, scoped ask.
prompt:
Role: You are a founder coach.

Task: Write outreach to [TARGET ADVISOR] asking them to advise [COMPANY] on [SPECIFIC AREA]. Why them specifically: [REASON]. Offer: [EQUITY/COMP/SCOPE, e.g. monthly call]. Scope it tightly — vague advisor asks get ignored.

Format: The outreach ≤120 words — personal reason it's them, the specific scoped ask, the offer, easy next step; plus a one-paragraph advisor agreement summary (scope, time, comp, term) and a graceful follow-up.

title: Agency Case Study & Portfolio Piece
author: PromptDeck
description: Turns a client project into a results-driven case study that wins similar work.
prompt:
Role: You are an agency marketer.

Task: Write a case study from my project: client [CLIENT/INDUSTRY — anonymize if needed], the challenge [PROBLEM], what we did [WORK], results [METRICS/OUTCOMES]. Target reader: a prospect with the same problem.

Format: A results-first headline; the client context; the challenge in the prospect's language; our approach (showing judgment, not just tasks); results with numbers (and honest context); a client quote placeholder; the 'facing something similar?' CTA. Plus a 3-line version for the portfolio grid.

title: Agency Pricing Model Design
author: PromptDeck
description: Chooses between hourly, project, retainer, and value pricing for your agency.
prompt:
Role: You are an agency pricing consultant.

Task: Design the pricing model for my [SERVICE] agency: team [SIZE], costs [MONTHLY COSTS], current model [CURRENT], typical client value delivered [VALUE EXAMPLES]. Match the model to how clients experience value.

Format: The model recommendation per service line (hourly/project/retainer/value — with why); the margin math (minimum viable price per model); how to present prices (options, anchoring); the transition plan from the current model; and the utilization trap to avoid.

title: Agile User Stories & Acceptance Criteria
author: PromptDeck
description: Turns a feature idea into well-formed user stories with acceptance criteria.
prompt:
Role: You are a product manager.

Task: For the feature [FEATURE] in [PRODUCT], write small, independently shippable user stories with acceptance criteria and edge cases.

Format: 3–5 stories as 'As a [user], I want [goal] so that [benefit].' Each with Given/When/Then acceptance criteria and noted edge cases. Then 2 out-of-scope items and any open questions.

title: AI Agent Instructions & Tools Spec
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes clear instructions and a tool-use policy for an autonomous AI agent.
prompt:
Role: You are an AI agent architect.

Task: Write instructions for an agent whose job is [GOAL], with access to tools [TOOLS]. Define how it plans, when to use each tool, and when to ask the user or stop.

Format: Role & objective; operating loop (plan→act→observe→reflect); tool-use rules per tool; guardrails & stop conditions; output format for the final answer; 2 example task walk-throughs.

title: AI Image Prompt (DALL·E / SD)
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes detailed image-generation prompts for DALL·E or Stable Diffusion with a negative prompt.
prompt:
Role: You are an AI image prompt specialist.

Task: Write image prompts for [CONCEPT] for tool [DALL·E/STABLE DIFFUSION], use case [LOGO/HERO/ILLUSTRATION]. Each specifies subject, composition, style/medium, lighting, color, mood, and detail level.

Format: 3 ready-to-paste variations. For Stable Diffusion also include a negative prompt and suggested settings.

title: AI Search (SGE/Chatbot) Optimization
author: PromptDeck
description: Optimizes content to be cited by AI search engines and chatbots.
prompt:
Role: You are a search strategist tracking the shift to AI answers.

Task: Optimize my content about [TOPIC] to be cited by AI search experiences and chatbots. Current page: [PASTE/DESCRIBE CONTENT]. AI answers favor clear claims, structured facts, definitions, and cited data.

Format: The extractability rewrite (direct answer blocks, definition sentences, stat formatting with sources); entity and terminology consistency fixes; the schema additions that help; the 'quotable sentence' audit (5 sentences an AI could lift verbatim with attribution); and what NOT to change for traditional rankings.

title: AIDA Framework Copy Generator
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes copy for any offer using the Attention–Interest–Desire–Action framework.
prompt:
Role: You are a direct-response copywriter.

Task: Write copy for [PRODUCT/OFFER] aimed at [AUDIENCE] using AIDA. Channel: [EMAIL/AD/PAGE]. Keep each stage doing one job — attention that stops, interest that's relevant, desire built on benefits/proof, one clear action.

Format: The copy with the four stages labeled, then an unlabeled clean version ready to use, plus 2 alternative attention hooks.

title: All-Hands / Team Announcement
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes an internal announcement that lands well, even with hard news.
prompt:
Role: You are an internal communications lead.

Task: Write an announcement to [TEAM/COMPANY] about [CHANGE: reorg, pivot, policy, milestone]. Tone: honest and human. Anticipate the questions people will actually have: [LIKELY CONCERNS].

Format: The message — what's changing, why (real reasons), what it means for people (specifically), what's not changing, timeline, where to ask questions; plus a leader's speaking-notes version and 5 anticipated Q&As.

title: Amazon PPC Campaign Structure
author: PromptDeck
description: Structures Amazon ad campaigns with the right match types and negatives.
prompt:
Role: You are an Amazon PPC specialist.

Task: Structure ad campaigns for my product [PRODUCT/ASIN CONTEXT] with target ACOS [TARGET] and budget [BUDGET/DAY]. Keywords so far: [KEYWORDS if any].

Format: The campaign structure (auto discovery → exact performance ladder); match-type strategy; the negative-keyword harvesting routine; bid starting points and adjustment rules; the weekly optimization checklist; and the 3 budget-wasting mistakes to avoid.

title: Amazon Product Listing Optimizer
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a keyword-rich Amazon title, bullets, and description that convert.
prompt:
Role: You are an Amazon listing and SEO specialist.

Task: Optimize the listing for [PRODUCT] with features [FEATURES], target buyer [BUYER], and keywords [KEYWORDS]. Follow Amazon style: front-load key terms, be benefit-led, stay within character norms, and avoid prohibited claims.

Format: (1) Title (≤200 chars); (2) 5 benefit-led bullet points; (3) a product description paragraph; (4) 10 backend search-term keywords. Keep it scannable and persuasive.

title: Annual Life Review & Goal Setting
author: PromptDeck
description: Guides a structured year review and meaningful goal-setting session.
prompt:
Role: You are a thoughtful life coach.

Task: Guide me through an annual review of [YEAR]. Ask me one question at a time, wait for my answer, and go deeper based on what I say. Cover: what energized/drained me, relationships, health, work, money, and what I avoided.

Format: Conversational, one question at a time. After the review phase, help me set 3 meaningful goals for next year — each with the underlying 'why', a keystone habit, and a quarterly check. End with a one-page summary of the whole session.

title: Annual Plan on One Page
author: PromptDeck
description: Compresses next year's strategy into one alignment page.
prompt:
Role: You are a strategic planning facilitator.

Task: Compress the annual plan for [COMPANY/TEAM] onto one page. Inputs: mission [MISSION], this year's results [RESULTS], next year's big bets [BETS], constraints [CONSTRAINTS].

Format: One page — the yearly theme (one sentence); 3 strategic priorities each with its metric and owner; what we're explicitly NOT doing; key risks; quarterly milestones per priority. Then 3 questions to stress-test alignment with the team.

title: API Reference Documentation
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes clear API reference docs with examples for every endpoint.
prompt:
Role: You are an API documentation writer.

Task: Document these endpoints for [API/PRODUCT]: [ENDPOINT DETAILS: method, path, params, responses]. Developers copy-paste first, read second — every endpoint needs a working example.

Format: Per endpoint — description (one line), the request (method, path, auth, params table with types/required), a curl example, the success response with realistic JSON, error responses table, and rate-limit notes; plus the getting-started/auth section and the changelog stub.

title: API Versioning & Deprecation Policy
author: PromptDeck
description: Defines how to evolve an API without breaking existing clients.
prompt:
Role: You are an API platform engineer.

Task: Define versioning and deprecation for [API] with consumers [WHO USES IT]. Choose a versioning scheme (URL/header/none-with-additive-changes) for this context and justify it.

Format: The scheme + reasoning; rules for what's breaking vs additive; the deprecation playbook (announce → headers → sunset timeline); a changelog/communication template; how to instrument who's on old versions; the compatibility test to add to CI.

title: App Store Listing (ASO) Optimizer
author: PromptDeck
description: Optimizes an app's title, subtitle, keywords, and description for the stores.
prompt:
Role: You are an ASO specialist.

Task: Optimize the store listing for [APP], which does [WHAT] for [AUDIENCE]. Target keywords: [KEYWORDS]. Platform: [iOS/ANDROID/BOTH].

Format: Title (≤30 chars) options ×3; subtitle/short description (≤30/80 chars) ×3; keyword field suggestions (iOS); a benefit-led long description with scannable feature bullets; screenshot caption ideas (5); what to A/B test first.

title: Ask a Happy Customer for a Referral
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a natural message to request a referral or introduction from a happy client.
prompt:
Role: You are a relationship-driven sales pro.

Task: Write a referral request to a happy customer of [PRODUCT/SERVICE] who recently got [RESULT], asking for an intro to [IDEAL REFERRAL PROFILE]. Make it easy and low-pressure; make them feel appreciated, not used.

Format: A short message that thanks them for a specific win, makes a clear and easy ask (with a forwardable blurb they can copy), and gives an out. Include the ready-to-forward blurb.

title: Authentication Flow Design
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs a secure auth flow (sessions/JWT/OAuth) for your app.
prompt:
Role: You are an application security engineer.

Task: Design authentication for [APP TYPE] on [STACK] needing [REQUIREMENTS: email/password, OAuth, magic links, 2FA]. Recommend session vs JWT for this case with reasoning. Prefer a managed provider when it fits: note the tradeoff.

Format: The recommended flow with a sequence diagram in text; token/session storage decisions (and why not localStorage if so); password handling rules; the endpoints/middleware needed; top 5 implementation mistakes to avoid; what to log for security events.

title: B2B LinkedIn Ads Copy & Targeting
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes LinkedIn ad copy and targeting logic for B2B offers.
prompt:
Role: You are a B2B paid-social specialist.

Task: Create LinkedIn ads for [OFFER: demo, guide, event] from [COMPANY] targeting [ICP: role, industry, size]. LinkedIn CPMs are brutal — the offer must match the buying stage; cold audiences don't book demos.

Format: The offer-to-stage check (and the fix if mismatched); 3 ad variations — intro text (≤150 chars before the fold), headline, and the angle each tests; targeting recipe (job functions + seniority + firmographics, with the too-narrow warning); the retargeting layer plan; and the metric expectations to set.

title: Backlink / Guest Post Outreach Email
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a personalized outreach email to earn a backlink or guest post.
prompt:
Role: You are a link-building specialist.

Task: Write outreach to [SITE/EDITOR] to earn a [BACKLINK / GUEST POST] related to [TOPIC], offering genuine value [WHAT YOU OFFER]. Personalized and non-spammy.

Format: A short email (≤120 words) with a specific personalized opener [PLACEHOLDER], why it helps their readers, the concrete ask, and an easy yes. Plus 3 subject lines and a polite follow-up.

title: Batch Content Generation System
author: PromptDeck
description: Sets up templates to generate consistent content variations at scale.
prompt:
Role: You are a content-operations engineer.

Task: Build a batch-generation system for [CONTENT TYPE: product descriptions, social variants, meta tags] at [VOLUME]. Consistency + variation: same structure, fresh language each time.

Format: The master template with [VARIABLES] and locked style rules; the anti-repetition instructions (vary openings, ban recycled phrases); a 5-item sample batch to verify; the QA checklist per batch (spot-check %, brand-word scan); and the input-spreadsheet format to run this at scale.

title: Beginner Garden Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Plans a manageable garden matched to your space, light, and climate.
prompt:
Role: You are a patient gardening mentor.

Task: Plan a garden for me: space [BALCONY/YARD/WINDOWSILL + SIZE], sunlight [HOURS/DIRECTION], climate/region [REGION], goal [FOOD/FLOWERS/LOW-EFFORT GREEN], experience [NONE/SOME]. Start small enough to succeed.

Format: The starter plant list (5–7, forgiving varieties for my light/climate) with why each; container/bed setup and soil basics; the planting calendar for my region; a 15-minute weekly care routine; the 3 beginner killers (overwatering etc.) and how to avoid them; expansion path for season 2.

title: Beginner-Friendly Workout Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs a weekly workout routine for your goal, level, and equipment (general fitness guidance).
prompt:
Role: You are a fitness coach. (General fitness guidance, not medical advice — consult a professional before starting.)

Task: Design a weekly plan for goal [STRENGTH/FAT LOSS/GENERAL], level [BEGINNER/INTERMEDIATE], [N] days/week, equipment [HOME/GYM/NONE], [MINUTES]/session.

Format: A day-by-day split with exercises, sets/reps, and rest; a warm-up and cool-down; weekly progression; form cues for main lifts; and swap options for limited mobility.

title: Better Sleep Routine Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Builds an evidence-informed wind-down routine to improve sleep (general wellness guidance).
prompt:
Role: You are a sleep-hygiene coach. (General wellness guidance, not medical advice.)

Task: Build a sleep routine given my issues [FALLING ASLEEP / WAKING / SCHEDULE], bedtime goal [TIME], and constraints [SHIFT WORK / KIDS / etc.].

Format: An evening wind-down timeline (from ~2 hours before bed); daytime habits that help; environment tweaks; a plan for racing thoughts; and 3 things to stop doing. Note to see a doctor for persistent issues.

title: Billboard / Outdoor Ad Copy
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes 7-words-or-fewer outdoor copy that lands at 100 km/h.
prompt:
Role: You are an out-of-home advertising copywriter.

Task: Write billboard copy for [BRAND/PRODUCT] near [LOCATION CONTEXT], for [AUDIENCE]. Max 7 words per line concept — readable in 3 seconds. Location-aware wit welcome.

Format: 8 concepts — each with the headline, an optional 2–4 word support line, and a one-line visual suggestion. Star the boldest and the safest.

title: Billing Dispute & Chargeback Response
author: PromptDeck
description: Handles billing complaints and chargeback threats fairly and safely.
prompt:
Role: You are a billing-support specialist.

Task: Respond to this billing dispute for [PRODUCT]: [PASTE COMPLAINT: unexpected charge, double billing, forgot subscription, chargeback threat]. Our records show: [WHAT ACTUALLY HAPPENED]. Resolve fairly fast — a chargeback costs more than most refunds.

Format: The reply — acknowledge without admitting fault prematurely, the factual explanation with dates, the resolution offer matched to the case, and the make-it-right close; the internal decision rule (refund vs hold firm); and the chargeback-prevention flag for this account type.

title: Black Friday / Sale Campaign Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Plans a high-stakes sale campaign: offer, timeline, and all key copy.
prompt:
Role: You are a promotions marketer.

Task: Plan a [BLACK FRIDAY/SEASONAL] campaign for [PRODUCT/STORE]. Design the offer [OFFER or 'recommend one'] to feel genuinely special without wrecking margins or training discount-waiting.

Format: The offer + fine print; a timeline (teaser → early access → live → last call); email subjects + bodies for each phase; 2 social posts; a site banner line; post-sale full-price rationale.

title: Blog Intro & Conclusion Writer
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a hooking intro and action-driving conclusion for a post.
prompt:
Role: You are an editor who fixes weak intros and endings.

Task: Write an intro and conclusion for a post titled [TITLE] targeting [KEYWORD] for [AUDIENCE]. Intro: hook in 2 sentences, establish relevance, promise the payoff, include the keyword naturally. Conclusion: summarize the takeaway and drive one action [CTA].

Format: 2 intro options (≤120 words each) and 1 conclusion (≤100 words).

title: Book Notes & Key Ideas Extractor
author: PromptDeck
description: Distills a book or long article into actionable notes you'll actually use.
prompt:
Role: You are a knowledge distiller.

Task: From the book/article [TITLE or paste excerpts], extract what's actually useful for my goal [WHY I'M READING IT]. Prioritize ideas I can apply, not summary for its own sake.

Format: (1) the core argument in 2 sentences; (2) 7 key ideas, each with a one-line 'how to apply'; (3) 3 quotes or passages worth remembering (paraphrased); (4) one action to take this week. Input: [PASTE EXCERPTS/NOTES].

title: Brand Color Palette & Rationale
author: PromptDeck
description: Suggests a cohesive brand color palette with hex codes, roles, and psychology.
prompt:
Role: You are a brand and UI designer.

Task: Suggest a color palette for [BRAND] (sells [WHAT], wants to feel [ADJECTIVES], audience [WHO]). Keep it cohesive and modern; avoid clashing hues.

Format: Primary, secondary, and 2 accent colors with hex; neutral/background and text colors with hex; the role and 'when to use' per color; a one-line psychology rationale each; a WCAG contrast note for text pairings.

title: Brand Partnership / Collab Pitch
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a win-win partnership pitch to another brand or newsletter.
prompt:
Role: You are a partnerships manager.

Task: Pitch [TARGET BRAND/NEWSLETTER] on a collaboration with [MY BRAND]. Overlapping audience: [OVERLAP]. Proposed formats: [IDEAS or 'suggest 3']. Lead with what's in it for them.

Format: A pitch email ≤150 words — personalized opener, the audience overlap logic, 2–3 concrete collab formats with their benefit first, an easy next step. Plus 3 subject lines.

title: Brand Refresh (Not Rebrand) Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Modernizes a brand's look while keeping its recognition equity.
prompt:
Role: You are a brand designer who protects equity.

Task: Plan a refresh for [BRAND], which feels [CURRENT PROBLEM: dated, inconsistent] but has recognition in [EQUITY ELEMENTS: logo, color, name]. Keep what's recognized; modernize what's dated.

Format: The equity audit (keep/evolve/retire per element); the refresh scope; before/after direction per element described; rollout order across touchpoints; how to brief stakeholders who fear change; the litmus test that we've refreshed, not rebranded.

title: Brand Voice & Messaging Guide
author: PromptDeck
description: Defines a brand's voice, tone, vocabulary, and do/don't rules for consistent content.
prompt:
Role: You are a brand strategist and voice designer.

Task: Create a brand voice guide for [BRAND], selling [WHAT] to [AUDIENCE], wanting to feel [3 ADJECTIVES].

Format: (1) 3 core voice traits, each with a definition and a 'we sound like / we don't sound like' pair; (2) tone shifts by context (sales vs support vs social); (3) vocabulary we use / avoid; (4) 2 sample paragraphs of the same message in-voice; (5) a 5-point pre-publish checklist.

title: Brief a Coding AI Agent Effectively
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes task briefs that get coding agents to ship what you actually want.
prompt:
Role: You are an expert at delegating to AI coding assistants.

Task: Turn my rough request into an effective coding-agent brief: [WHAT I WANT BUILT/FIXED]. Codebase context: [STACK/CONVENTIONS]. Vague briefs produce confident wrong code — pin down behavior, constraints, and 'done'.

Format: The structured brief — goal (user-visible behavior), constraints (stack, patterns to follow, what NOT to touch), acceptance criteria (testable), edge cases I care about, and the verification step to run; plus the 3 details I left ambiguous that will bite me.

title: Brochure / One-Pager Copy
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes structured brochure or leave-behind one-pager copy.
prompt:
Role: You are a B2B collateral copywriter.

Task: Write copy for a [TRI-FOLD BROCHURE / ONE-PAGER] for [PRODUCT/SERVICE] handed to [AUDIENCE] at [CONTEXT: trade show/meeting]. It must work standalone when read later.

Format: Panel-by-panel (or section-by-section) copy — cover hook; problem/solution; benefits with icons note; proof/logos placeholder; how it works; offer + contact CTA. Keep each block ≤40 words.

title: Browse Abandonment & Retargeting Copy
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes recovery emails/ads for shoppers who browsed but never carted.
prompt:
Role: You are a lifecycle marketer for e-commerce.

Task: Write browse-abandonment recovery for [STORE] shoppers who viewed [PRODUCT/CATEGORY] without adding to cart. Softer than cart abandonment — they were curious, not committed.

Format: (1) an email — subject + body ≤100 words, curiosity-framed ('still thinking about…'), value-add angle (guide/reviews), no discount; (2) a retargeting ad set — 3 variations (social proof, objection, category-benefit); (3) frequency caps and when to stop.

title: Bug Report Triage & Response
author: PromptDeck
description: Turns a messy customer bug report into a clean triage summary plus a reply to the customer.
prompt:
Role: You are a technical support engineer.

Task: From the customer bug report below for [PRODUCT], produce an internal triage summary and a customer-facing reply. If reproduction details are missing, list the exact questions to ask.

Format: (1) Internal — issue in one line; suspected area; severity (P1–P4) with reasoning; repro steps as understood; missing info. (2) Customer reply — acknowledge, set expectations, ask only the necessary questions. Report: [PASTE REPORT].

title: Build a Portfolio With No Clients Yet
author: PromptDeck
description: Creates credible portfolio pieces before you have paying clients.
prompt:
Role: You are a mentor for new freelancers.

Task: Help me build a portfolio for [SERVICE] with zero clients. My skill level: [LEVEL]. Spec work must look like real work — full process, real constraints, honest labeling.

Format: 3 portfolio project concepts (realistic fake briefs for recognizable-type businesses, labeled as concept work); the case-study structure for each (brief → decisions → outcome reasoning); one strategic freebie/discount project approach with scope limits; where to publish; and what beginners wrongly include.

title: Build a Social Life in a New City
author: PromptDeck
description: Builds friendships from zero after moving somewhere new.
prompt:
Role: You are a social-connection coach.

Task: Help me build a social life in [CITY] where I know [NOBODY/A FEW PEOPLE]. My interests: [INTERESTS], social style [INTROVERT/EXTROVERT/AMBIVERT], time [AVAILABILITY]. Friendship needs repetition + vulnerability — one-off meetups rarely convert.

Format: The repetition-based venue list for my interests (recurring > one-off events); the acquaintance→friend conversion plays (the first invite script, the follow-up timing); a weekly social quota that fits my energy; the 90-day expectation reset (it takes longer than movies suggest); and the loneliness-spike protocol for hard weeks.

title: Build Your Promotion Case
author: PromptDeck
description: Builds an evidence-based case for a promotion and the conversation script.
prompt:
Role: You are a career advancement coach.

Task: Build my promotion case from [CURRENT ROLE] to [TARGET ROLE]. My evidence: [ACCOMPLISHMENTS/SCOPE GROWTH]. Company process: [PROCESS/TIMELINE if known]. Frame it as already operating at the next level, not wanting a reward.

Format: The case document — next-level expectations mapped to my evidence; impact quantified; gaps and my plan for them; the manager conversation script (the ask, timeline question, and response to 'not yet'); and the 6-month plan B if declined.

title: Burnout Check & Recovery Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Assesses burnout signals and builds a realistic recovery approach.
prompt:
Role: You are an occupational-wellbeing coach. (Supportive guidance — a doctor or therapist for clinical concerns.)

Task: Check my burnout signals: what I'm experiencing [SYMPTOMS: exhaustion, cynicism, detachment, sleep, dread], for how long [DURATION], the work context [WORKLOAD/CONTROL/RECOGNITION SITUATION]. Burnout is a workplace-conditions problem, not a personal weakness.

Format: The honest signal assessment (early strain vs deep burnout indicators); the triage — what to stop this week (with the scripts to push back on workload); the recovery basics that actually move the needle vs the bubble-bath myths; the conditions conversation with my manager (or the calculation if the conditions won't change); and the professional-help threshold stated plainly.

title: Business Event / Meetup Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Plans a small business event or meetup that people actually enjoy.
prompt:
Role: You are an event producer.

Task: Plan a [EVENT TYPE: meetup/workshop/dinner] for [AUDIENCE], goal [GOAL: community, pipeline, brand], budget [BUDGET], size [N]. Design for connection, not lectures.

Format: Concept & name ideas; run-of-show with timings; the connection mechanic (structured intros/activity); invite copy; a pre-event reminder that cuts no-shows; post-event follow-up; budget breakdown; success metrics.

title: Business Risk Assessment
author: PromptDeck
description: Identifies and prioritizes business risks with likelihood, impact, and mitigations.
prompt:
Role: You are a risk management consultant.

Task: Assess the key risks for [BUSINESS/PROJECT] pursuing [GOAL] in [CONTEXT]. Cover operational, financial, market, legal/compliance, and reputational risks.

Format: A table — Risk | Category | Likelihood (H/M/L) | Impact (H/M/L) | Mitigation | Owner. Then the top 3 risks to address now and an early-warning signal for each.

title: Business SWOT & Next Steps
author: PromptDeck
description: Runs a structured SWOT and turns it into prioritized, actionable next steps.
prompt:
Role: You are a management consultant.

Task: Perform a SWOT for [BUSINESS/IDEA/PRODUCT] in [MARKET], referencing my specifics rather than textbook filler.

Format: 4–6 specific points under each of Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats. Then synthesize — 3 strategic priorities (strengths × opportunities), 2 risks to mitigate now, and a 30-60-90 day action list.

title: Buy vs Build Analysis
author: PromptDeck
description: Compares building in-house vs buying a solution with total-cost honesty.
prompt:
Role: You are a pragmatic CTO-advisor.

Task: Analyze buy vs build for [CAPABILITY] at [COMPANY]. Build estimate: [ENGINEERING GUESS — I'll help you sanity-check it]. Buy options: [VENDORS/PRICING]. Include the costs people forget: maintenance, opportunity cost, key-person risk, vendor lock-in.

Format: True cost comparison over 3 years (table); strategic fit (is this our differentiator?); risk profile of each path; the hybrid option if one exists; recommendation with the condition that would flip it.

title: Campaign Concept & Big Idea
author: PromptDeck
description: Generates creative campaign concepts with a big idea, tagline, and channel plan.
prompt:
Role: You are a creative director.

Task: Develop 3 campaign concepts for [BRAND] to achieve [GOAL] with [AUDIENCE], budget [BUDGET]. Each needs a distinct 'big idea', not just tactics.

Format: For each concept — a name, the big idea in one sentence, the core insight it taps, a tagline, 3 channel activations, and one hero asset idea. End by recommending the strongest and why.

title: Cancellation / Save Flow Message
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a respectful save-offer message when a customer tries to cancel.
prompt:
Role: You are a retention specialist who respects the customer's choice.

Task: Write a save message for a customer of [PRODUCT] cancelling because [REASON], with possible offer [OFFER/ALTERNATIVE]. Never dark-pattern; make leaving easy if they want.

Format: Acknowledge their reason; address it honestly; present one relevant alternative or offer; and clearly allow them to proceed with cancelling. Keep it short and human.

title: Capsule Wardrobe Builder
author: PromptDeck
description: Builds a mix-and-match wardrobe that simplifies getting dressed.
prompt:
Role: You are a practical personal stylist.

Task: Build a capsule wardrobe for me: lifestyle [WORK DRESS CODE/LIFESTYLE], climate [CLIMATE], style feel [ADJECTIVES], budget [BUDGET], body/fit notes [PREFERENCES]. Everything must mix with almost everything.

Format: The capsule list (~25–35 pieces) grouped by category with the color logic explained; 10 example outfits from the capsule; what to declutter from a typical closet; the buy-order by priority within budget; and the one-in-one-out maintenance rule.

title: Car Buying Decision & Negotiation Prep
author: PromptDeck
description: Structures a car purchase decision and dealership negotiation.
prompt:
Role: You are a consumer advocate for car buyers. (General guidance — verify current prices and terms locally.)

Task: Help me buy a car: needs [NEEDS: commute, family, cargo], budget [BUDGET], new/used preference [PREFERENCE], financing situation [CASH/LOAN]. Separate the decision from the negotiation.

Format: The needs-to-shortlist logic (3 vehicle types/models to research); total-cost-of-ownership comparison framework; the used-car inspection/history checklist; negotiation prep (out-the-door price focus, common dealer tactics and counters, walk-away discipline); and financing red flags.

title: Career Development Roadmap
author: PromptDeck
description: Maps a realistic path from your current role to a target role with skills and milestones.
prompt:
Role: You are a career coach.

Task: Build a roadmap from my current role [CURRENT] to target role [TARGET] over [TIMEFRAME], given my strengths [STRENGTHS] and gaps [GAPS].

Format: The skills/experiences to acquire; a phased milestone plan (e.g. 3/6/12 months); projects or roles to seek; people/networks to build; and 3 signals that show I'm ready for the next step.

title: Cart Abandonment Email Series
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a 3-email cart recovery series that rescues lost sales.
prompt:
Role: You are an e-commerce email copywriter.

Task: Write a 3-email cart abandonment series for [STORE/PRODUCT TYPE]. Assume common hesitations: [SHIPPING COST/TRUST/PRICE]. Only include a discount in email 3 if [DISCOUNT AVAILABLE].

Format: Email 1 (1h): helpful reminder + item benefits. Email 2 (24h): objection handling + social proof. Email 3 (48–72h): urgency or incentive. Each — subject + preview + body ≤120 words + CTA.

title: Champion Enablement Email
author: PromptDeck
description: Arms your internal champion to sell for you in meetings you're not in.
prompt:
Role: You are an enterprise seller who wins through champions.

Task: Write an email arming my champion [NAME/ROLE] at [PROSPECT] to pitch [PRODUCT] internally to [WHO THEY MUST CONVINCE]. Make their job effortless — they get the credit.

Format: A short note to the champion; then a forwardable section — the 3-sentence pitch in their company's language, 3 anticipated internal objections with answers, the business case numbers, and the suggested internal ask.

title: Channel / Referral Partner Pitch
author: PromptDeck
description: Pitches an agency or consultant on referring you their clients.
prompt:
Role: You are a partnerships lead.

Task: Pitch [PARTNER TYPE: agencies/consultants/complementary vendors] on referring clients to [MY PRODUCT]. Their clients need us when [TRIGGER]. Offer: [COMMISSION/RECIPROCAL VALUE].

Format: The outreach email ≤130 words — their client's problem first, why we make the partner look good, the concrete offer, easy next step; plus a one-paragraph 'how it works' they can say yes to, and 3 partner-fit qualifying questions for the call.

title: Churn Survey & Exit Interview
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs a cancellation survey that gets honest reasons and saves some customers.
prompt:
Role: You are a retention researcher.

Task: Design a cancellation flow survey for [PRODUCT]. Get the honest primary reason with minimal friction, and branch to a relevant save-offer only where it makes sense.

Format: The reason list (7 options, mutually exclusive); one conditional follow-up per reason; which reasons trigger which save offer; the survey UI copy; and how to report results monthly.

title: Churned Customer Win-Back Email
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a win-back email to re-engage a lapsed or churned customer.
prompt:
Role: You are a retention marketer.

Task: Write a win-back email to a churned customer of [PRODUCT] who left [REASON, if known], offering [INCENTIVE, if any]. Be warm and low-pressure; acknowledge the gap honestly.

Format: A short email with 2 subject lines — a friendly re-opener, a reminder of the value/what's new, an easy reason to return, and a single CTA. Then a one-line SMS version.

title: CI/CD Pipeline Setup
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs a CI/CD pipeline with the right stages for your stack.
prompt:
Role: You are a DevOps engineer.

Task: Design a CI/CD pipeline for a [STACK] project on [GITHUB ACTIONS/GITLAB/etc.], deploying to [TARGET]. Requirements: [TESTS/LINT/PREVIEW DEPLOYS/etc.]. Fast feedback first; expensive jobs later.

Format: (1) the pipeline config file, commented; (2) stage-by-stage explanation with rough timing; (3) caching strategy; (4) secrets handling notes; (5) 3 common failure modes and their fixes.

title: Classroom Management Reset Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Rebuilds classroom norms and routines when things have slipped.
prompt:
Role: You are a classroom management mentor.

Task: Help me reset my [GRADE/CLASS] where [WHAT'S HAPPENING: talking over, transitions chaos, phone use]. What I've tried: [TRIED]. Build systems, not punishments.

Format: The reset conversation script (owning the reset, co-creating norms); 3 routines to install with exact teaching steps; the consistent response ladder for violations; positive reinforcement mechanics; week-by-week rollout; how to hold the line on day 3 when it's tested.

title: Clear Standup / Status Update
author: PromptDeck
description: Turns your raw progress notes into a crisp standup or status update.
prompt:
Role: You are a communication-savvy teammate.

Task: Turn my notes below into a concise status update for [STANDUP / MANAGER / STAKEHOLDERS]. Highlight progress, blockers, and what's next; surface risks early.

Format: Yesterday/Done; Today/Next; Blockers (with what you need); plus a one-line 'overall status' (On track / At risk / Off track). Notes: [PASTE NOTES].

title: CLI Tool Design & UX
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs a command-line tool with great flags, output, and error UX.
prompt:
Role: You are a developer-tools engineer who sweats CLI ergonomics.

Task: Design a CLI tool that [PURPOSE] in [LANGUAGE]. Users: [WHO]. Follow CLI conventions — good defaults, composability, helpful errors.

Format: Command/subcommand structure with flags; example invocations for the 5 main use cases; output design (human vs --json); error message style with examples; the help text; config file vs flags decisions; packaging/distribution note.

title: Clickable Blog Title & Hook Ideas
author: PromptDeck
description: Generates 15 high-CTR, SEO-aware blog titles plus intro hooks across proven formulas.
prompt:
Role: You are a headline strategist who blends CTR psychology with SEO.

Task: Generate 15 blog title ideas for [TOPIC] targeting [KEYWORD], using a mix of formulas (how-to, listicle, question, mistakes-to-avoid, ultimate guide, contrarian, result-driven). Keep titles under 60 characters where possible and front-load the keyword when natural.

Format: A table — Title | Formula | Why it works. Then write 2 opening-hook variations for the single best title.

title: Client Discovery Call Script
author: PromptDeck
description: Runs a discovery call that qualifies the client and sets up the proposal.
prompt:
Role: You are a consultative-selling coach for freelancers.

Task: Script my discovery call for [SERVICE] prospects. I need to leave knowing: their real goal, budget reality, decision process, and whether they're a good client. Red flags I've been burned by: [PAST RED FLAGS].

Format: The call structure with timings — opening frame (I'm assessing fit too), goal questions, the budget conversation (3 ways to raise it comfortably), decision/timeline questions, my red-flag detectors phrased naturally, and the close (next steps + when they'll hear from me). Plus the post-call fit scorecard.

title: Client Onboarding System
author: PromptDeck
description: Builds a smooth onboarding that sets expectations and prevents chaos later.
prompt:
Role: You are an operations consultant for service businesses.

Task: Build a client onboarding system for my [SERVICE] business. Recurring problems to prevent: [PAST PROBLEMS: slow feedback, missing assets, ghost clients, surprise stakeholders].

Format: The onboarding sequence — welcome email (sets communication norms + response expectations), the kickoff questionnaire (questions that surface problems early), asset-collection checklist with deadline, the kickoff-call agenda, and the project-home setup; plus the 'what happens if you're unresponsive' clause phrased kindly.

title: Client Proposal / Quote Writer
author: PromptDeck
description: Drafts a persuasive, well-structured client proposal that closes deals.
prompt:
Role: You are a proposal writer for a services business.

Task: Write a proposal for [CLIENT] to deliver [SERVICE/SCOPE] solving their goal [GOAL], with pricing [PRICING] and timeline [TIMELINE]. Lead with their outcome, not your process.

Format: Sections — Executive summary (their goal & outcome); Understanding of their situation; Proposed solution & scope; Deliverables & timeline; Investment (pricing options); Why us / proof; Clear next step & acceptance. Keep it confident and skimmable.

title: Client Red Flag Detector
author: PromptDeck
description: Evaluates a prospective client conversation for warning signs before you commit.
prompt:
Role: You are a battle-scarred freelancer who has seen every bad client type.

Task: Evaluate this prospective client for red flags. Our interaction so far: [PASTE CONVERSATION/DESCRIBE BEHAVIOR]. Budget signals: [SIGNALS]. My gut says: [GUT FEELING].

Format: Red flags detected (each with the future problem it predicts); yellow flags (proceed with protections); the protections to add if proceeding (deposit %, kill fee, scope language); the polite decline script if warranted; and the pattern-check: which of MY behaviors attract this client type.

title: Client Testimonial Collection Flow
author: PromptDeck
description: Collects strong client testimonials at the moment of peak satisfaction.
prompt:
Role: You are a service-business marketer.

Task: Build my testimonial collection flow for [SERVICE]. The peak moment: [WHEN CLIENTS ARE HAPPIEST: delivery day, first result]. Make it effortless and get specifics, not adjectives.

Format: The ask email at the peak moment (with 4 guided questions that extract the story: before-state, why me, the result, who they'd recommend); the LinkedIn-recommendation variant; the 'can I edit this for length?' permission line; where each testimonial gets used; and the follow-up if they agree but never send.

title: Closed-Lost Re-Engagement
author: PromptDeck
description: Re-opens deals lost 6–12 months ago with a changed-context angle.
prompt:
Role: You are a pipeline recovery specialist.

Task: Re-engage a closed-lost prospect [PROSPECT] who chose [COMPETITOR/NOTHING] [TIMEFRAME] ago because [LOSS REASON]. What's changed on our side: [WHAT'S NEW]. No 'I told you so' energy; lead with the change that voids their old objection.

Format: A re-engagement email ≤110 words + 2 subject lines; a LinkedIn version; and the follow-up if they reply 'we're happy with our choice'.

title: COD Order Confirmation & RTO Reduction
author: PromptDeck
description: Confirms cash-on-delivery orders and cuts return-to-origin losses.
prompt:
Role: You are an e-commerce operations consultant for COD-heavy markets.

Task: Reduce RTO (return-to-origin) for my COD orders at [STORE] with RTO rate [RATE]. Confirm intent without adding friction for genuine buyers.

Format: The order-confirmation flow (WhatsApp/SMS/call script with timing); the address-quality checklist and auto-flags; the pre-dispatch reconfirmation for high-risk orders; incentives to prepay (small discount framing); the delivery-day heads-up message; and RTO-pattern tracking to block repeat offenders fairly.

title: Codebase Onboarding Guide for New Devs
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes the guide that gets a new developer productive in days, not weeks.
prompt:
Role: You are a staff engineer who onboards developers well.

Task: Write an onboarding guide for [CODEBASE/PRODUCT] on [STACK]. Key areas: [MAIN MODULES/SERVICES]. The unwritten knowledge: [TRIBAL KNOWLEDGE: weird build steps, fragile areas, naming quirks].

Format: Day-1 setup (exact commands, common failures); the mental model (how the pieces fit, in prose + a text diagram); the code tour (5 files to read first, in order, with why); the tribal-knowledge section written down at last; the first-task suggestion (small, touches the core path); and who-to-ask-what.

title: Cold Call Openers & Talk Track
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes permission-based cold call openers and a 30-second talk track.
prompt:
Role: You are a cold-calling coach.

Task: Write call openers for selling [PRODUCT] to [ROLE] at [COMPANY TYPE]. Respect their time, earn 30 seconds, no fake familiarity.

Format: 5 openers (permission-based, pattern-interrupt, referral-mention, trigger-event, honest-direct); a 30-second value talk track for when they say 'go ahead'; and responses to 'not interested' and 'send me an email' that keep the door open.

title: Cold LinkedIn DM That Gets Replies
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes cold outreach to people you admire without being ignored.
prompt:
Role: You are a networking-outreach coach.

Task: Write a cold DM to [PERSON/ROLE] about [WHY THEM: their work, path, company]. What I actually want: [ADVICE/REFERRAL PATH/CONVERSATION]. Respect their time mathematically — specific question, easy to answer, no 'pick your brain for 30 minutes'.

Format: The DM (≤90 words: the specific thing of theirs that prompted this, the one focused question, the easy-out); the connection-request note version (≤300 chars); the thank-you-plus-result follow-up later (the update that makes helping feel worthwhile); and the ask-escalation ladder (advice before referral, always).

title: Cold List Re-Engagement Email
author: PromptDeck
description: Wakes up a cold email list before you prune it.
prompt:
Role: You are an email deliverability-conscious marketer.

Task: Write a re-engagement campaign for subscribers of [LIST/BRAND] who haven't opened in [PERIOD]. Give a real reason to come back and an honest goodbye if not.

Format: 3 emails — (1) 'we miss you' + best-of value; (2) preference/frequency options; (3) last-chance with clear unsubscribe framing. Subjects + bodies ≤120 words. End with prune criteria.

title: Commit Message & PR Description Writer
author: PromptDeck
description: Turns a diff into a conventional-commit message and a reviewer-friendly PR description.
prompt:
Role: You are a disciplined senior engineer.

Task: From the change summary or diff below, write a Conventional Commits message and a PR description that's skimmable for a busy reviewer.

Format: (1) commit — type(scope): subject (≤72 chars, imperative) + a short body of what & why; (2) PR description — Summary, What changed, Why, How to test, and a checklist. Changes: [PASTE DIFF OR SUMMARY].

title: Community Building Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Builds a 90-day plan to start and grow a brand community.
prompt:
Role: You are a community strategist.

Task: Create a 90-day plan to launch a community for [BRAND] serving [AUDIENCE] on [PLATFORM: Discord/Slack/Circle/etc.]. Define the value members get that content alone can't give.

Format: The community's core promise; channel/space structure; onboarding flow for new members; a weekly programming calendar (rituals, events, threads); moderation guidelines summary; 30/60/90-day goals with metrics.

title: Community Moderation Responses
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes fair, de-escalating moderator responses for community incidents.
prompt:
Role: You are a community moderator who keeps spaces healthy without heavy-handedness.

Task: Write moderation responses for [COMMUNITY/PLATFORM] incident: [WHAT HAPPENED: heated thread, rule violation, misinformation, spam]. Rules context: [RELEVANT RULES]. Address behavior, never attack the person; de-escalate publicly, discipline privately.

Format: The public thread response (calm, rule-anchored); the private message to the member involved; the escalation criteria if it continues; and a note on whether the rule itself needs clarifying.

title: Compare & Contrast Anything
author: PromptDeck
description: Produces a clear, balanced comparison of options with a recommendation.
prompt:
Role: You are an unbiased analyst.

Task: Compare [OPTION A] and [OPTION B] (and [C]) for someone who cares about [PRIORITIES / USE CASE]. Be balanced and flag any assumption.

Format: A comparison table on the dimensions that matter; pros/cons of each; who each is best for; and a clear recommendation with the one factor that would change it.

title: Comparison Table Copy (Us vs Them)
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes the rows and framing for an honest us-vs-competitors table.
prompt:
Role: You are a product marketer.

Task: Create an us-vs-them comparison table for [MY PRODUCT] vs [COMPETITORS] for [AUDIENCE]. Pick rows we honestly win or tie; phrase row labels neutrally (buyers smell rigged tables).

Format: The table (Row | Us | Them…) with checkmark/text cells; a headline + intro line above it; 2 footnote clarifications for honesty; and one row we lose gracefully included for credibility.

title: Compelling About Page / Brand Story
author: PromptDeck
description: Turns your background into a warm, trust-building About page that still sells.
prompt:
Role: You are a brand storyteller.

Task: Write an About page for [BRAND/PERSON], who does [WHAT] for [AUDIENCE], founded because [ORIGIN/MISSION]. Make it human and trust-building while gently guiding the reader to [DESIRED ACTION]. Voice: [VOICE].

Format: A hook that isn't 'we were founded in…'; the origin story; what you believe and do differently; proof/credibility; who you help; a warm CTA. Keep paragraphs short and skimmable.

title: Competitive Battlecard
author: PromptDeck
description: Builds a battlecard for selling against a specific competitor.
prompt:
Role: You are a competitive-enablement manager.

Task: Build a battlecard for selling [MY PRODUCT] against [COMPETITOR]. What I know about them: [THEIR STRENGTHS/WEAKNESSES/PRICING]. Be honest about where they win so reps keep credibility.

Format: Quick profile; where we win (with proof points); where they win (and the counter-frame); landmine questions to plant early; objection responses ('they're cheaper', 'they have X'); a trap-check: signs we should walk away.

title: Competitor Comparison Matrix
author: PromptDeck
description: Builds an honest comparison of your product against competitors on buyer criteria.
prompt:
Role: You are a competitive intelligence analyst.

Task: Compare [MY PRODUCT] vs [COMPETITOR 1], [COMPETITOR 2], [COMPETITOR 3] in the [MARKET]. Infer the 6–8 dimensions buyers actually compare on; be honest, not just flattering.

Format: A markdown table scoring each player on those dimensions (High/Med/Low or short notes; mark speculation). Then a summary of where I win, where I'm weak, and 3 positioning angles.

title: Competitor Content Gap Analysis
author: PromptDeck
description: Finds topics competitors rank for that you don't, and prioritizes them.
prompt:
Role: You are an SEO strategist.

Task: Given my site's topics [MY TOPICS/PAGES] and competitors [COMPETITOR SITES/TOPICS], identify content gaps — themes they cover that I don't — and prioritize by intent and difficulty.

Format: A table — Gap Topic | Likely Keyword | Intent | Priority (H/M/L) | Content Type. Then the top 5 to create first with a working title each.

title: Complete Book / Ebook Outline
author: PromptDeck
description: Produces a full book structure — titles, chapters, and per-chapter beats.
prompt:
Role: You are a professional book editor.

Task: Create an outline for a [FICTION/NON-FICTION] book about [TOPIC/PREMISE] for [TARGET READER].

Format: 5 title options + a one-line hook; a 10–15 chapter list each with a one-sentence purpose; the first 3 chapters expanded into 4–6 bullet beats; suggested word count per chapter and total. Then ask which chapter to draft first.

title: Complete Lesson Plan Generator
author: PromptDeck
description: Builds a structured lesson plan with objectives, activities, timing, and assessment.
prompt:
Role: You are an experienced teacher and curriculum designer.

Task: Create a [DURATION] lesson plan on [TOPIC] for [GRADE/LEVEL], aligned to [CURRICULUM/STANDARD if any]. Keep it practical.

Format: 3 measurable objectives; a hook/warm-up; a time-boxed activity sequence (teacher & student actions); one group and one individual task; differentiation for struggling and advanced learners; a formative assessment + exit ticket; required materials.

title: Complex Form UX Design
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs a long or complex form people actually finish.
prompt:
Role: You are a UX designer specializing in forms.

Task: Design the UX for a form collecting [FIELDS/DATA] for [PURPOSE]. Reduce abandonment: question order, chunking, and progressive disclosure.

Format: The field order with grouping into steps/sections (and why); what to make optional or infer; input types per field; inline validation timing; progress indication; save-and-resume approach; the 3 fields most likely to cause drop-off with mitigation.

title: Concept Map / Topic Breakdown
author: PromptDeck
description: Breaks a big topic into a structured concept map showing how ideas connect.
prompt:
Role: You are an instructional designer.

Task: Break down [TOPIC] for [LEVEL] into a concept map showing main branches, sub-concepts, and how they connect.

Format: An indented outline (main topic → branches → sub-concepts), then a short list of the key relationships between branches, and the recommended order to learn them.

title: Conference / Event Lead Follow-Up
author: PromptDeck
description: Follows up with event contacts while the conversation is still warm.
prompt:
Role: You are a field marketer turned seller.

Task: Write follow-ups for leads from [EVENT]. Context per lead: [WHAT WE DISCUSSED / BOOTH SCAN / TALK ATTENDED]. Reference the actual moment; don't pitch cold.

Format: 3 templates — (1) had a real conversation: recall detail + next step; (2) badge scan only: value-first, low ask; (3) attended our talk: content follow-on + soft CTA. Each ≤110 words with subject line, sent within [TIMEFRAME].

title: Conference Networking Game Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Works a conference deliberately: targets, conversations, and follow-up.
prompt:
Role: You are a strategic-networking coach.

Task: Plan my networking for [EVENT/CONFERENCE], my goal [GOAL: job leads, clients, learning, industry friends], my style [NETWORKING COMFORT LEVEL]. Three real conversations beat thirty business cards.

Format: The pre-event target list logic (who to seek, how to research attendees/speakers); conversation openers beyond 'so what do you do'; the graceful exit lines; the same-night note system (where I met them, what we discussed, the promised follow-up); the 48-hour follow-up messages; and the introvert pacing plan (recharge slots, quality over coverage).

title: Consistent AI Image Series (Same Style/Character)
author: PromptDeck
description: Keeps characters and style consistent across a series of generated images.
prompt:
Role: You are an AI image workflow specialist.

Task: Help me generate a consistent series of [N] images featuring [CHARACTER/PRODUCT/STYLE] across scenes [SCENES]. Tool: [TOOL]. Consistency comes from locked descriptors, not luck.

Format: The locked style block (exact recurring descriptors: appearance, palette, rendering style, lighting) to paste into every prompt; per-scene prompts (locked block + scene variables); seed/reference-image tactics for my tool; the drift-check ritual; and what to fix in editing vs regeneration.

title: Content Cold-Open Hooks (First 5 Seconds)
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes first-5-second cold opens that stop the scroll for any video/post.
prompt:
Role: You are a hook specialist for short-form content.

Task: Write cold-open hooks for content about [TOPIC] for [AUDIENCE] on [PLATFORM]. Each must create an information gap or stakes within 5 seconds, and be deliverable on camera in one breath.

Format: 12 hooks grouped by type (result-first, mistake, contrarian, story mid-action, question, challenge); for the top 3, add the natural second line that keeps momentum.

title: Contract Review Question List (Pre-Lawyer)
author: PromptDeck
description: Prepares the right questions and red flags before a lawyer reviews your contract.
prompt:
Role: You are a business advisor who preps founders for legal review. (Not legal advice — a licensed lawyer must review the actual contract.)

Task: For a [CONTRACT TYPE: vendor/client/partnership/employment] between [PARTIES] for [PURPOSE], list what to check and ask before/during legal review.

Format: The 10 business terms to verify (payment, termination, liability, IP, exclusivity…); red-flag clauses to search for in this contract type; questions to ask my lawyer; and the 3 terms most worth negotiating.

title: Conversion-Aware FAQ Page
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes an FAQ page that removes doubts and nudges toward purchase.
prompt:
Role: You are a copywriter who treats FAQs as sales copy.

Task: Write an FAQ page for [PRODUCT/SERVICE] using real doubts: [KNOWN QUESTIONS or 'infer 10 likely ones']. Answer clearly first, then add confidence — every answer should reduce anxiety, not create legalese.

Format: 10–12 Q&As grouped (Product / Billing / Getting started / Trust); answers 2–4 sentences with a soft next-step link where natural; suggest the 3 FAQs to also show on the pricing page.

title: Convert a Project Client to Retainer
author: PromptDeck
description: Pitches ongoing retainer work to a client after a successful project.
prompt:
Role: You are a recurring-revenue strategist for freelancers.

Task: Pitch a retainer to [CLIENT] after finishing [PROJECT] with result [OUTCOME]. The ongoing need I see: [RECURRING NEED]. Frame the retainer as protecting/compounding their result, not as me seeking stability.

Format: The pitch email — the result recap, the 'what happens without maintenance' insight (honest), the retainer offer (scope, cadence, price, priority-access perk), and an easy trial framing (3 months); the retainer scope-boundary list; and the response if they say 'let's just do ad-hoc'.

title: Convert Code Between Languages
author: PromptDeck
description: Translates code from one language/framework to another idiomatically.
prompt:
Role: You are a polyglot senior engineer.

Task: Convert the code below from [SOURCE LANGUAGE] to [TARGET LANGUAGE/FRAMEWORK], writing it the idiomatic way in the target — not a literal line-by-line port. Preserve behavior.

Format: (1) the converted code; (2) notes on idiomatic changes you made; (3) any library equivalents used; (4) anything that behaves differently to watch for. Code: [PASTE CODE].

title: Corporate Training Workshop Design
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs an engaging workplace training session that changes behavior.
prompt:
Role: You are a corporate learning designer.

Task: Design a [DURATION] training on [SKILL/TOPIC] for [EMPLOYEES/ROLE] whose behavior change goal is [WHAT THEY DO DIFFERENTLY AFTER]. Adults learn by doing and connecting to their real work.

Format: Learning objectives as observable behaviors; the session arc (hook → their current pain → practice cycles with real scenarios → commitment); activities with timings; the job-aid/cheat-sheet they leave with; the 30-day reinforcement plan; how to measure behavior change (not just satisfaction).

title: Couples Money Conversation Guide
author: PromptDeck
description: Structures a calm, blame-free money conversation with a partner.
prompt:
Role: You are a financial-communication counselor. (Relationship guidance — a financial advisor handles the numbers themselves.)

Task: Help me talk with my partner about [MONEY TOPIC: spending gap, debt reveal, merging finances, savings goals]. Our dynamic: [HOW MONEY TALKS GO NOW]. Money fights are rarely about money — find the values underneath.

Format: The setup (timing, framing the invitation non-ambush); the opening script (curiosity, not audit); the questions that surface each person's money story; the structure for the actual numbers part; the de-escalation move when it gets tense; and the recurring money-date format so this stops being a crisis conversation.

title: Course / Curriculum Outline
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs a full course outline with modules, outcomes, and assessments.
prompt:
Role: You are a curriculum architect.

Task: Design a course on [SUBJECT] for [AUDIENCE/LEVEL], lasting [DURATION], with the end goal that learners can [OUTCOME].

Format: Course description & learning outcomes; a module-by-module outline (module → topics → outcome → activity); assessments and a capstone; required resources; and a suggested pacing schedule.

title: Course Completion Nudge Emails
author: PromptDeck
description: Re-engages students who stalled mid-course.
prompt:
Role: You are a learning-retention specialist.

Task: Write nudge emails for students of [COURSE] who stalled at [WHERE THEY STOP: module 2, after week 1]. Shame doesn't work — lower the re-entry barrier and rebuild momentum.

Format: 3 nudges — (1) the 'life happens' normalizer with a 10-minute re-entry task; (2) the goal-reminder connecting back to why they enrolled; (3) the fresh-start offer (restart from a checkpoint). Each with subject + ≤100-word body; plus the in-course change that would reduce this drop-off point.

title: Creative / Design Brief Generator
author: PromptDeck
description: Produces a clear design brief so a designer or AI tool knows exactly what to create.
prompt:
Role: You are a design lead writing briefs.

Task: Write a design brief for [WHAT NEEDS DESIGNING] for brand [BRAND], goal [GOAL], audience [WHO], desired feeling [ADJECTIVES]. Make it clear enough to start without a meeting.

Format: Objective; audience; key message; deliverables & specs; mandatory elements; style/tone references to look for; what to avoid; timeline placeholder; how success is judged.

title: Crisis Communication Response
author: PromptDeck
description: Drafts a measured public and internal response to a company crisis.
prompt:
Role: You are a crisis communications advisor.

Task: Draft our response to [SITUATION: data issue, service failure, public criticism]. Facts confirmed so far: [FACTS]. Unknowns: [UNKNOWNS]. Never speculate beyond confirmed facts; commit only to what we'll actually do.

Format: A holding statement (if facts are incomplete); the full public statement; the internal note to employees; customer-facing FAQ (3 Qs); what NOT to say; and the update cadence commitment.

title: CSAT / Feedback Survey Builder
author: PromptDeck
description: Creates a short, unbiased customer satisfaction survey that gets responses.
prompt:
Role: You are a CX researcher.

Task: Build a short post-interaction survey for [PRODUCT/SERVICE] to measure satisfaction and surface improvement areas. Keep questions unbiased and quick.

Format: A rating question (CSAT or CES); 1–2 targeted follow-ups (one conditional on a low score); one open-ended question; and a friendly intro + thank-you. Add 3 tips to maximize response rate.

title: Custom GPT / Assistant System Prompt
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs a robust system prompt for a custom GPT, chatbot, or AI assistant.
prompt:
Role: You are an AI systems designer.

Task: Write a system prompt for an assistant that does [PURPOSE] for users [WHO], with personality [TONE]. It must always [KEY BEHAVIORS] and never [GUARDRAILS].

Format: A complete system prompt covering role & goal, tone, step-by-step behavior, output format, handling of unknowns/out-of-scope, and safety guardrails. Then 3 test messages to verify it behaves correctly.

title: Customer Case Study / Success Story
author: PromptDeck
description: Turns raw results into a persuasive challenge-solution-results case study.
prompt:
Role: You are a B2B content writer specializing in case studies.

Task: Write a 400–500 word case study for customer [NAME/INDUSTRY], whose challenge was [PROBLEM], who used our product for [USE], achieving [METRICS/OUTCOMES]. Keep it credible and specific — story first, sales second.

Format: A results-led headline; a short customer intro; the Challenge; the Solution; the Results with metrics highlighted; a realistic pull-quote; a soft CTA.

title: Customer Interview Question Guide
author: PromptDeck
description: Builds a non-leading interview guide to uncover real buying motivations.
prompt:
Role: You are a jobs-to-be-done researcher.

Task: Create an interview guide to learn why customers [BOUGHT/CHURNED/SWITCHED] regarding [PRODUCT]. Questions must be about their actual past behavior, never hypotheticals, never leading.

Format: A warm-up; a timeline walk-through section (first thought → search → decision); 12 core questions with follow-up probes; what to listen for; and a note-taking template.

title: Dashboard UI Layout Design
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs an information hierarchy and layout for a data dashboard.
prompt:
Role: You are a product designer specializing in data-heavy UIs.

Task: Design the layout for a dashboard showing [DATA/METRICS] for user [ROLE] whose top question is [KEY QUESTION]. Hierarchy: answer the top question in 5 seconds, details on demand.

Format: The layout described zone by zone (top-level KPIs, trends, breakdowns, actions); which chart type per metric and why; empty/loading/error state handling; interaction patterns (filters, drill-down); the 3 clutter traps to avoid.

title: Data Pipeline / ETL Design
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs a resilient data pipeline from sources to a queryable store.
prompt:
Role: You are a data engineer.

Task: Design a pipeline moving data from [SOURCES] to [DESTINATION] for [USE CASE: analytics/ML/reporting], volume [VOLUME], freshness needed [LATENCY]. Batch vs streaming: decide and justify.

Format: Architecture (stages + tools fitting my stack [STACK]); the schema/contract at each boundary; idempotency & backfill strategy; failure handling per stage; data-quality checks; the simplest version to ship first.

title: Database Schema Design
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs a normalized schema with indexes and migration-safe decisions.
prompt:
Role: You are a database architect.

Task: Design a schema for [APPLICATION/FEATURE] in [POSTGRES/MYSQL/etc.] with entities [ENTITIES] and access patterns [MAIN QUERIES]. Normalize sensibly; denormalize only with a stated reason.

Format: The CREATE TABLE statements with comments; an ER summary (entity → relationships); indexes with the query each serves; 3 future-proofing decisions made; and one thing NOT to add yet.

title: Deal Risk Review (Red Flags)
author: PromptDeck
description: Stress-tests a deal for the risks that kill late-stage opportunities.
prompt:
Role: You are a skeptical sales director doing deal inspection.

Task: Stress-test this deal: [DEAL NOTES: stage, contacts, timeline, competition, next steps]. Find the risks I'm rationalizing away — single-threading, no economic buyer, vague timeline, 'verbal yes' comfort.

Format: Red flags found (ranked by kill-probability); for each — why it kills deals and the de-risking action this week; a realistic close-probability estimate with reasoning; the one question I'm afraid to ask the prospect (and should).

title: Debate Prep: Both Sides
author: PromptDeck
description: Prepares you to argue a motion by mastering both sides of it.
prompt:
Role: You are a debate coach.

Task: Prepare me to debate [MOTION/TOPIC] arguing [MY SIDE/BOTH]. Format: [FORMAT if known]. Winners know the opposing case better than their opponents do.

Format: The 3 strongest arguments per side (steelmanned, with evidence types); the clash points where the debate will actually be decided; rebuttal lines against their best arguments; my opening framing; the trap questions to expect; and one rhetorical technique fit for this motion.

title: Debug This Error
author: PromptDeck
description: Diagnoses an error with ranked likely causes and concrete fixes.
prompt:
Role: You are a senior engineer debugging a production issue.

Task: Diagnose the error below given context [WHAT I WAS DOING], environment [LANGUAGE/FRAMEWORK/VERSION], and code [PASTE CODE].

Format: (1) the 3 most likely root causes, ranked with why; (2) the fastest way to confirm each; (3) a concrete fix for the most likely one; (4) how to prevent this class of bug. Error: [PASTE ERROR/STACK TRACE].

title: Decision Journal Template & Review
author: PromptDeck
description: Logs big decisions so you can improve your judgment over time.
prompt:
Role: You are a decision-quality coach.

Task: Set up a decision journal for me and log my current decision: [DECISION I'M MAKING]. Judgment improves by comparing what I expected against what happened — memory rewrites history without a record.

Format: The journal template (situation, options, what I expect to happen + confidence %, what would change my mind, emotional state); my current decision logged in it; the review ritual (when to revisit, what to compare); and the 2 bias-checks most relevant to this decision type.

title: Decision-Making Framework
author: PromptDeck
description: Structures a tough decision with weighted criteria, pros/cons, and a recommendation.
prompt:
Role: You are a decision coach.

Task: Help me choose between [OPTION A], [OPTION B], (and [OPTION C]) given priorities [CONTEXT]. Be direct — don't sit on the fence.

Format: (1) 5–6 weighted criteria from my priorities; (2) a scoring table; (3) the strongest argument for each option; (4) a risk/bias I might overlook; (5) a clear recommendation and the one thing that would change it.

title: Deep-Work Focus Sprint Planner
author: PromptDeck
description: Structures a distraction-proof deep-work sprint for one important task.
prompt:
Role: You are a focus coach.

Task: Plan a deep-work sprint to finish [TASK] within [TIME AVAILABLE]. My usual distractions: [DISTRACTIONS]. Break the work into concrete sub-outputs, not vague chunks.

Format: A sprint plan — pre-sprint setup checklist (environment, tools open, distractions blocked); timed blocks with a named deliverable per block and short breaks; a definition of done; and a 2-minute end-of-sprint review ritual.

title: Define Your Content Pillars
author: PromptDeck
description: Defines 4–5 content pillars that balance reach, trust, and conversion.
prompt:
Role: You are a content strategist.
