Task: Define content pillars for [BRAND/CREATOR] serving [AUDIENCE] with business goal [GOAL]. Balance discovery content, trust/authority content, and conversion content.

Format: 4–5 pillars — each with name, purpose in the funnel, 5 example topics, best formats, and rough posting share (%). Then 3 rules for when a topic doesn't fit any pillar.

title: Delegation Brief for a Task
author: PromptDeck
description: Turns 'I'll just do it myself' into a clean delegation brief.
prompt:
Role: You are a management coach.

Task: Help me delegate [TASK/RESPONSIBILITY] to [PERSON/ROLE]. My worry: [CONCERN: quality/speed/it's faster myself]. Define the outcome, not the method — leave room for their judgment.

Format: The delegation brief — outcome & definition of done; context/why it matters; constraints & guardrails; decision authority (what they decide vs escalate); check-in points (minimal); how I'll give feedback. Plus the handoff message to send them.

title: Design → Developer Handoff Package
author: PromptDeck
description: Prepares a design handoff developers won't need five meetings to decode.
prompt:
Role: You are a design-engineering bridge.

Task: Prepare the handoff for [FEATURE/SCREENS] going to developers. Design state: [WHAT EXISTS]. Cover what mocks never show: states, edge cases, and behavior.

Format: The handoff doc — screen inventory with links placeholder; per screen: interactive states (hover/focus/disabled/loading/error), responsive behavior, motion specs, content rules (truncation, max lengths, empty states); the tokens/values table; open questions flagged honestly; and the walkthrough-meeting agenda (30 min max).

title: Design Consistency Audit
author: PromptDeck
description: Audits a product's UI for inconsistencies and drift from the system.
prompt:
Role: You are a design systems auditor.

Task: Audit UI consistency for [PRODUCT]: [DESCRIBE/PASTE the variations you've noticed: buttons, spacing, colors, copy patterns]. Find the drift, judge severity, and prescribe consolidation.

Format: An inconsistency inventory table — Element | Variants found | User-facing harm (real or cosmetic) | Canonical choice recommended; the consolidation order (user-facing harm first); the process fix so drift stops recurring; and what variation is actually fine to keep.

title: Design Critique & Improvements
author: PromptDeck
description: Gives structured, constructive critique of a design with concrete improvements.
prompt:
Role: You are a seasoned design critic who is kind but honest.

Task: Critique the design described below (goal: [GOAL], audience: [WHO]) across hierarchy, spacing, typography, color, and clarity of the primary action.

Format: What's working; the top 5 issues ranked by impact, each with a specific fix; and the single highest-leverage change to make first. Design: [DESCRIBE OR PASTE DETAILS].

title: Design Portfolio Review
author: PromptDeck
description: Reviews a design portfolio's story, case studies, and presentation.
prompt:
Role: You are a design hiring manager who reviews hundreds of portfolios.

Task: Review my portfolio: [DESCRIBE PROJECTS/STRUCTURE or paste case study text]. Target role: [ROLE]. Judge it the way reviewers do — 30 seconds to hook, process over pixels.

Format: First-impression verdict; per project — does it show process, decisions, and impact (gaps flagged); which project to lead with and which to cut; the storytelling fixes; 3 changes with the highest hiring impact.

title: Design System Tokens & Scale
author: PromptDeck
description: Defines foundational design tokens (type, spacing, color, radius) for consistency.
prompt:
Role: You are a design systems lead.

Task: Define foundational design tokens for [PRODUCT/BRAND] with vibe [ADJECTIVES]. Keep scales consistent and accessible.

Format: Token sets with values — color (semantic + palette, hex); typography scale (sizes/weights/line-heights); spacing scale; border-radius; shadows. Present as name/value pairs ready to drop into CSS variables or a tokens file.

title: Detailed Buyer Persona Generator
author: PromptDeck
description: Generates a rich buyer persona with goals, pains, objections, and messaging angles.
prompt:
Role: You are a marketing strategist and customer researcher.

Task: Create a detailed buyer persona for a business selling [PRODUCT/SERVICE] to [TARGET MARKET]. Base it on realistic assumptions and label anything speculative.

Format: A structured profile — Name, age, role, one-line bio; Demographics/firmographics; Goals & motivations; Pains & frustrations; Buying triggers & decision criteria; Common objections (with counters); Preferred channels; 3 messaging angles each with a sample one-liner.

title: Differentiate a Lesson (Mixed Abilities)
author: PromptDeck
description: Adapts one lesson for struggling, on-level, and advanced learners.
prompt:
Role: You are a differentiation specialist.

Task: Differentiate my lesson on [TOPIC] for a class with [CLASS PROFILE: struggling readers, ELL students, advanced cluster]. Core objective everyone must reach: [OBJECTIVE].

Format: The common core task; scaffolds for struggling learners (specific supports, not easier goals); extensions for advanced learners (deeper, not more); ELL supports; how to run all tiers simultaneously (logistics); one formative check that works across tiers.

title: Difficult Performance Feedback Script
author: PromptDeck
description: Prepares a clear, fair conversation about underperformance.
prompt:
Role: You are an HR-savvy leadership coach.

Task: Prepare me to give performance feedback to [ROLE] about [SPECIFIC ISSUE with EXAMPLES]. Prior conversations: [HISTORY]. Be direct and kind — vague feedback is unkind; describe behavior and impact, not character.

Format: The opening lines (no feedback-sandwich); situation-behavior-impact phrasing for each example; the expectations going forward with timeline; how to invite their perspective genuinely; responses if they get defensive/emotional/surprised; the follow-up note template.

title: Digital Declutter & Screen Time Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Reduces digital noise: apps, notifications, subscriptions, and doomscrolling.
prompt:
Role: You are a digital wellness coach.

Task: Help me declutter my digital life. Problem areas: [DOOMSCROLLING/NOTIFICATIONS/EMAIL/SUBSCRIPTIONS]. Phone use: [HOURS/DAY, worst apps]. Reduce friction to good uses, add friction to bad ones — willpower isn't the plan.

Format: The notification audit (what stays); phone home-screen redesign; app-specific friction tactics; an email/subscription purge process; the replacement behavior for each scrolling trigger; a realistic screen-time target with weekly review.

title: Digital PR / HARO Pitch
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes an expert-source pitch that earns press mentions and links.
prompt:
Role: You are a digital PR specialist.

Task: Write a source pitch responding to a journalist query about [QUERY TOPIC], positioning [ME/EXPERT: credentials] as the source. Lead with the quotable insight, not the bio.

Format: Subject line; a 2–3 sentence quotable answer with a specific stat/example; one-line credentials; offer for more. Under 150 words total, skimmable.

title: Direct Mail Sales Letter
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a classic direct-mail letter with envelope teaser and PS strategy.
prompt:
Role: You are a direct-mail copywriter.

Task: Write a sales letter mailed to [AUDIENCE] for [OFFER], from [SENDER/BUSINESS]. Personal, letter-like tone — this must not read like a brochure.

Format: Envelope teaser line; the letter (Johnson-box hook, personal opening, problem→solution, offer, guarantee, clear response mechanism [CALL/QR/URL]); a PS that restates the offer + deadline; a PPS with a bonus.

title: Discovery / Sales Call Script
author: PromptDeck
description: Builds a structured call script from opener to next-step close.
prompt:
Role: You are a sales enablement coach.

Task: Write a call script for selling [PRODUCT] to [BUYER ROLE], goal [BOOK NEXT STEP]. Consultative, not pushy, letting the buyer talk most.

Format: Sections with sample lines — Opener & rapport; Agenda-setting; Discovery questions; Value framing tied to their pain; Handling the top objection; Trial close; Clear next-step ask. Add a 3-bullet pre-call prep checklist.

title: Dockerfile & Container Setup
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes an optimized, secure Dockerfile for your app with an explanation.
prompt:
Role: You are a DevOps engineer.

Task: Write a production-ready Dockerfile for a [STACK] app that [BUILD/RUN DETAILS]. Optimize for small image size, layer caching, and security (non-root, minimal base).

Format: (1) the Dockerfile with brief inline comments; (2) an explanation of key choices; (3) a matching .dockerignore; (4) example build & run commands. Details: [PASTE DETAILS].

title: Doctor Appointment Prep & Questions
author: PromptDeck
description: Prepares you to get the most from a short medical appointment.
prompt:
Role: You are a patient-advocacy educator. (Preparation help — your clinician makes the medical calls.)

Task: Prepare me for my appointment about [CONCERN/SYMPTOMS/CHECKUP]. History relevant: [RELEVANT HISTORY/MEDS]. Appointments are short — organized patients get better care.

Format: The symptom summary to say in 60 seconds (what, when it started, what changes it, what I've tried); my question list ranked (top 3 first, in case time runs out); what to write down during the visit; the 'what happens next' questions before leaving; and the follow-up-if-worse criteria to ask for explicitly.

title: E-commerce Category Page SEO Copy
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes category-page copy that ranks without burying products.
prompt:
Role: You are an e-commerce SEO copywriter.

Task: Write copy for the category page [CATEGORY] at [STORE] targeting [KEYWORD]. Short intro above products; deeper buying guidance below the grid.

Format: (1) title + meta; (2) a 50–80 word intro; (3) a below-grid section: how to choose, key features to compare, FAQ (3 Qs); (4) internal link suggestions to subcategories/guides.

title: E-commerce Product Description
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a benefit-driven, SEO-aware product description with a scannable feature list.
prompt:
Role: You are an e-commerce conversion copywriter.

Task: Write a description for [PRODUCT NAME] with features [FEATURE 1; FEATURE 2; FEATURE 3], for buyer [BUYER], in brand voice [VOICE], including the keyword [KEYWORD] naturally. Translate features into benefits. Keep it under 180 words.

Format: (1) a benefit-led headline; (2) a 2–3 sentence hook; (3) a short benefit paragraph; (4) a scannable feature/spec bullet list; (5) a confident closing CTA.

title: E-E-A-T Trust Signals Audit
author: PromptDeck
description: Audits a page/site for experience, expertise, authority, and trust signals.
prompt:
Role: You are an SEO quality rater expert.

Task: Audit [PAGE/SITE DESCRIPTION] in the [NICHE] niche for E-E-A-T. Identify missing trust signals — author credentials, first-hand experience proof, citations, about/contact, reviews, editorial standards.

Format: A table — Signal | Present? | Fix. Then the 5 highest-impact additions for this niche and a sample author-bio block.

title: Email Newsletter Issue
author: PromptDeck
description: Drafts a complete, engaging newsletter issue with a hook and a single CTA.
prompt:
Role: You are an email newsletter writer with a strong personal voice.

Task: Write a newsletter issue for [BRAND/NEWSLETTER], audience [WHO], about [THEME], in voice [VOICE], around 400–600 words with no fluff.

Format: (1) 3 subject line options + preview text; (2) a personal-feeling intro hook; (3) 2–3 short value sections with subheads; (4) one curated 'worth checking out' placeholder; (5) a single clear CTA.

title: Email Subject Line Pack (50)
author: PromptDeck
description: Generates 50 subject lines across proven styles for one campaign.
prompt:
Role: You are an email copywriter obsessed with open rates.

Task: Write 50 subject lines for a campaign about [CAMPAIGN/OFFER] to [AUDIENCE]. Cover styles: curiosity, benefit, number, question, urgency (honest), personal, contrarian, emoji-light. Keep most ≤45 chars; no spam-trigger words.

Format: Grouped by style with a matching preview-text suggestion for the best 10. Star your predicted top 3.

title: Empathetic Customer Support Reply
author: PromptDeck
description: Drafts a warm, on-brand support response that solves the issue and protects the relationship.
prompt:
Role: You are a senior customer support specialist for [COMPANY/PRODUCT], voice [VOICE].

Task: Reply to the customer message below — acknowledge feelings, take ownership, give a clear solution or next step, and set a timing expectation. If information is missing, ask one specific question. Human, not robotic; no over-apologizing.

Format: A ready-to-send reply. Customer message: [PASTE MESSAGE].

title: Empathy Map → Message Angles
author: PromptDeck
description: Builds an empathy map for your buyer and converts it into copy angles.
prompt:
Role: You are a copy strategist who starts with the customer's head.

Task: Build an empathy map for [BUYER] considering [PRODUCT/CATEGORY] — what they See, Hear, Think & Feel, Say & Do, their Pains and Gains — then convert it into message angles.

Format: The empathy map in sections; then 6 copy angles each tied to a specific quadrant, with a sample headline; finally the one dominant emotion to build the campaign on.

title: Employee → Freelance Transition Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Plans a de-risked move from a job to freelancing.
prompt:
Role: You are a freelance business coach.

Task: Plan my transition from [CURRENT JOB] to freelancing as [SERVICE], with runway [SAVINGS/MONTHS], current side income [AMOUNT], obligations [OBLIGATIONS]. De-risk it — decide with numbers, not vibes.

Format: The financial threshold to quit safely (calculated); the build-while-employed phase plan (check employment contract for conflicts); first-3-clients acquisition plan; pricing floor math; the go/no-go checklist; and the 90-day post-quit plan.

title: Environment & Config Management
author: PromptDeck
description: Sets up clean config/secrets handling across dev, staging, and prod.
prompt:
Role: You are a platform engineer.

Task: Design configuration management for a [STACK] app across dev/staging/prod. Current pain: [PAIN: secrets in code, config drift, onboarding friction].

Format: The config hierarchy (defaults → env → secrets) with file layout; a .env.example template; secrets storage recommendation per environment; validation-at-boot code snippet; onboarding steps for a new dev; 3 config smells to eliminate.

title: Error Handling Strategy for a Codebase
author: PromptDeck
description: Defines consistent error handling: what to catch, wrap, retry, and surface.
prompt:
Role: You are a staff engineer standardizing error handling.

Task: Define an error-handling strategy for a [LANGUAGE/STACK] codebase doing [DOMAIN: API server, CLI, frontend]. Cover: expected vs unexpected errors, retry policy, user-facing messages, and logging.

Format: The error taxonomy (types + when to use); code patterns for each (catch/wrap/propagate) with snippets; retry rules (what's safe to retry, backoff); what the user sees per class; the 5 anti-patterns to ban in review.

title: Essay / Writing Feedback Coach
author: PromptDeck
description: Gives structured feedback on an essay covering thesis, structure, evidence, and style.
prompt:
Role: You are a writing tutor.

Task: Give feedback on the essay below written for [ASSIGNMENT/LEVEL]. Be encouraging but specific; don't rewrite it for me — coach me to improve it.

Format: (1) 2 strengths; (2) feedback on thesis, structure, evidence, and style (each with one concrete suggestion); (3) 3 priority fixes; (4) a sample improved topic sentence. Essay: [PASTE ESSAY].

title: Etsy Listing Optimizer
author: PromptDeck
description: Optimizes an Etsy listing's title, tags, and description for Etsy search.
prompt:
Role: You are an Etsy SEO specialist.

Task: Optimize my Etsy listing for [PRODUCT] targeting shoppers searching [WHAT BUYERS TYPE]. Current listing: [PASTE CURRENT or 'new listing'].

Format: The title (keyword-front-loaded, natural, within limits); all 13 tags (multi-word, no single-word waste); the description (buyer-benefit first paragraph, details, personalization/shipping notes); attribute recommendations; photo order suggestion; and 2 occasion/audience angles I'm missing.

title: Evaluate & Improve an AI Output
author: PromptDeck
description: Critiques an AI-generated draft against your goal and produces a stronger version.
prompt:
Role: You are a rigorous editor evaluating AI-generated content.

Task: Evaluate the draft below against the goal [GOAL] for audience [AUDIENCE]. Check accuracy, completeness, tone, structure, and specificity; flag anything generic or unverifiable.

Format: (1) a score out of 10 per dimension with one-line reasons; (2) the top 5 concrete fixes; (3) the improved version. Draft: [PASTE OUTPUT].

title: Exam Cram Plan (Limited Time)
author: PromptDeck
description: Builds a triage study plan when the exam is close and time is short.
prompt:
Role: You are a study strategist who works with realities, not ideals.

Task: Build a cram plan: exam on [EXAM/TOPICS] in [DAYS/HOURS AVAILABLE], my current level per topic [SELF-ASSESSMENT], exam format [FORMAT/WEIGHTING]. Triage ruthlessly — points per hour of study.

Format: The topic triage (secure quickly / grind / strategically abandon) with reasoning; the hour-by-hour schedule with active-recall methods only (no rereading); the night-before and morning-of protocol; and the exam-time strategy for questions from abandoned topics.

title: Executive Decision Memo (One-Pager)
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a decision memo that gets leadership to a clear yes/no fast.
prompt:
Role: You are a strategy consultant who writes for executives.

Task: Write a decision memo asking leadership to decide [DECISION] by [DATE]. Context: [BACKGROUND]. Options considered: [OPTIONS]. My recommendation: [RECOMMENDATION].

Format: The decision needed (one sentence, top); context (≤5 lines); options with pros/cons/cost in a table; recommendation with reasoning; risks of the recommendation and mitigations; what happens if we don't decide; the specific approval being asked.

title: Executive Summary One-Pager
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a one-pager your champion can forward to their executive team.
prompt:
Role: You are a sales enablement writer.

Task: Write a one-pager about [PRODUCT] for the executives at [PROSPECT], sponsored by my champion [ROLE]. Their strategic priority: [PRIORITY]. Executives skim — lead with business outcome, not product.

Format: A headline tying us to their priority; the problem in their terms (2 lines); the outcome with numbers; how it works (3 bullets max); proof (customer + metric); investment & timeline; the specific decision being asked for.

title: Exit-Intent Popup Copy
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a non-annoying exit popup that actually earns the save.
prompt:
Role: You are a CRO copywriter.

Task: Write exit-intent popup copy for [PAGE TYPE] offering [OFFER/LEAD MAGNET] to visitors about to leave. Respect the reader — no guilt-trip 'No, I hate saving money' buttons.

Format: 3 popup variants — headline, 1–2 support lines, CTA button, decline link (neutral wording). Vary the angle: value-add, curiosity, and discount (if applicable). Note which fits cold vs warm traffic.

title: Explain & Review This Code
author: PromptDeck
description: Explains a code snippet and flags bugs, security issues, and improvements.
prompt:
Role: You are a senior software engineer doing a careful review.

Task: For the code below in [LANGUAGE/FRAMEWORK], explain it and improve it. Flag real bugs, edge cases, and security concerns.

Format: (1) 2–3 sentence summary; (2) step-by-step logic in plain language; (3) bugs/edge cases/security issues; (4) concrete improvements with short code examples; (5) quality rating out of 10 with a one-line justification. Code: [PASTE CODE].

title: Explain a Hard Topic to a Child
author: PromptDeck
description: Helps a parent explain difficult subjects age-appropriately and honestly.
prompt:
Role: You are a child-development-informed communication guide.

Task: Help me explain [TOPIC: death, divorce, money worries, news events, where babies come from] to my [AGE]-year-old, who asked/knows [WHAT PROMPTED THIS]. Honest but age-calibrated — kids fill silence with worse imagination.

Format: What a child this age can grasp about this; the explanation script in short simple sentences; the questions they'll likely ask next with answers; what NOT to say (over-detail, false reassurance); the feelings-check close; and the signal that they need more support.

title: Explain a Research Paper Simply
author: PromptDeck
description: Summarizes a dense paper or abstract into plain language with key takeaways.
prompt:
Role: You are a science communicator.

Task: Explain the paper/abstract below in plain language for a [LEVEL] reader, keeping the findings accurate and noting limitations.

Format: (1) the core question; (2) what they did (method) simply; (3) what they found; (4) why it matters; (5) caveats/limitations; (6) 3 terms defined. Paper/abstract: [PASTE HERE].

title: Explain Anything Simply (ELI5 → Expert)
author: PromptDeck
description: Explains any complex topic at three levels with an analogy and a self-check.
prompt:
Role: You are a gifted teacher who makes hard things simple.

Task: Explain [TOPIC] at three levels — ELI5 (to a curious 10-year-old with one everyday analogy), Intermediate (key mechanisms for a smart adult), and Expert (nuances, edge cases, common misconceptions).

Format: The three labeled levels, then a 3-question self-check quiz with answers hidden below a divider.

title: Explain It With a Diagram
author: PromptDeck
description: Chooses and structures the right diagram to explain a concept or system.
prompt:
Role: You are a visual-explanation specialist.

Task: Design a diagram explaining [CONCEPT/SYSTEM/PROCESS] to [AUDIENCE]. Choose the right form — flowchart, sequence, layers, quadrant, cycle, hierarchy — for the structure of the idea, not aesthetics.

Format: The diagram type + why it matches the idea's structure; the elements and their arrangement described precisely (or as Mermaid code if applicable); labels and annotations; what to leave out; and the 30-second verbal walkthrough that accompanies it.

title: Explain With the Perfect Analogy
author: PromptDeck
description: Explains a concept using tailored analogies from a field you already understand.
prompt:
Role: You are a master explainer.

Task: Explain [CONCEPT] to me using analogies drawn from [FIELD I KNOW, e.g. cooking, football, music]. Keep the analogy accurate and note where it breaks down.

Format: A one-line plain definition; the extended analogy; a mapping of each analogy part to the real concept; and where the analogy stops being true.

title: Extract Structured Data From Messy Text
author: PromptDeck
description: Pulls clean, structured fields out of unstructured text, ready for a spreadsheet.
prompt:
Role: You are a precise data-extraction engine.

Task: Extract these fields — [FIELDS, e.g. name, email, company, amount, date] — from the text below. Do not guess: mark missing values as null and ambiguous ones with a ? and a note.

Format: A markdown table with one row per record, exact field names as columns, followed by a JSON array of the same records. Text: [PASTE TEXT].

title: Fact-Check an AI Answer
author: PromptDeck
description: Systematically verifies an AI response for hallucinations and weak claims.
prompt:
Role: You are a skeptical fact-checking editor.

Task: Audit the AI-generated answer below for reliability. Classify each claim: verifiable fact (how to verify), plausible but unverified, opinion dressed as fact, or likely hallucination (specific numbers, citations, names are prime suspects).

Format: A claim-by-claim table — Claim | Classification | How to verify | Risk if wrong; the overall trust assessment; the 3 claims to verify before using this answer; and a rewritten version with unverifiable claims hedged or removed. Answer: [PASTE AI ANSWER].

title: Family History Interview Questions
author: PromptDeck
description: Captures a relative's life story before the stories are lost.
prompt:
Role: You are an oral-history interviewer.

Task: Prepare me to record [RELATIVE: grandmother, father, uncle]'s life story. Their life chapters I know of: [ROUGH OUTLINE: migration, war years, first job, the family business]. Stories live in specifics — ask about moments, not eras.

Format: The question flow by life chapter (childhood senses, turning points, the hard parts asked gently, love and loss, advice); the follow-up probes ('what did it smell like', 'what were you wearing'); recording logistics tips; the sensitive-topic navigation; and the closing questions that always produce gold.

title: Family Rituals & Quality Time Ideas
author: PromptDeck
description: Creates simple, repeatable family rituals that build connection.
prompt:
Role: You are a family connection researcher.

Task: Suggest rituals for my family: [FAMILY MAKEUP, AGES], time available [TIME], current challenge [EVERYONE ON SCREENS / TEENS WITHDRAWING / etc.]. Rituals beat grand gestures — small, repeatable, low-resistance.

Format: 8 ritual ideas grouped by frequency (daily micro / weekly / monthly / yearly) — each with what it is, why it works for these ages, and how to start it without it feeling forced; plus how to protect rituals when schedules get chaotic.

title: Fascination Bullet Points
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes curiosity-driven benefit bullets that make people need to know more.
prompt:
Role: You are a classic direct-response copywriter known for irresistible bullets.

Task: From the product details below for [PRODUCT], write 15 fascination bullets — specific, curiosity-loaded benefit teasers (e.g. 'The 3-word phrase that…'). Never reveal the full answer; never fabricate claims beyond the details given.

Format: 15 bullets grouped by angle (secret/mistake/shortcut/warning/how-to). Details: [PASTE FEATURES/CONTENT].

title: Feature / News Announcement Copy
author: PromptDeck
description: Announces a new feature or change with benefit-first framing across channels.
prompt:
Role: You are a product marketing copywriter.

Task: Announce [FEATURE/CHANGE] for [PRODUCT] to [USERS/AUDIENCE]. Lead with what they can now do, not what we shipped. Note any action users must take: [ACTION or none].

Format: (1) in-app banner line (≤90 chars); (2) email — subject + ≤120-word body; (3) changelog entry; (4) a social post; (5) if it's a removal/price change, a respectful explanation paragraph.

title: Feature → Benefit → Emotion Matrix
author: PromptDeck
description: Converts a feature list into benefits and emotional payoffs for copy anywhere.
prompt:
Role: You are a copy strategist.

Task: Convert the features of [PRODUCT] into a copy-ready matrix. Features: [LIST FEATURES]. For each: the functional benefit ('so you can…'), the emotional payoff ('which means you feel…'), and a proof idea.

Format: A table — Feature | Benefit | Emotional payoff | Proof idea | Sample copy line. Then the 3 strongest lines to lead with.

title: Feature Flag Strategy
author: PromptDeck
description: Sets up feature flags for safe rollouts without flag-debt chaos.
prompt:
Role: You are a release engineer.

Task: Design feature-flag usage for [TEAM/APP] on [STACK] to enable [GOALS: gradual rollout, kill switches, A/B]. Prevent the flag graveyard — every flag needs a death date.

Format: Flag types (release/ops/experiment) with lifecycle rules; naming convention; code pattern for clean flag checks; rollout playbook (1% → 10% → 50% → 100% with checks); the flag-removal ritual; and 3 places never to put a flag.

title: Feature Request & Feedback Triage
author: PromptDeck
description: Organizes raw user feedback into themes with product-team-ready summaries.
prompt:
Role: You are the bridge between support and product.

Task: Triage the raw feedback below from [SOURCE: tickets/reviews/NPS]. Cluster into themes, separate symptoms from requests, and translate the top themes into product-team-ready problem statements (not solution demands).

Format: Theme table — Theme | Count feel | Representative quote | Underlying problem statement; the 3 themes with the strongest signal; which requests are loud-minority vs broad; and the reply template to send users whose request we're not building.

title: Featured Snippet Optimizer
author: PromptDeck
description: Rewrites content to win paragraph, list, or table featured snippets.
prompt:
Role: You are an SEO specialist who wins featured snippets.

Task: Optimize my content below to win the featured snippet for [QUERY]. Identify whether Google likely shows a paragraph, list, or table snippet and structure accordingly.

Format: (1) the snippet-optimized block (40–58 words for paragraph, or a clean list/table) placed under a question-matching H2; (2) where to insert it in my content; (3) two supporting tweaks. Content: [PASTE CONTENT].

title: Few-Shot Example Builder
author: PromptDeck
description: Creates high-quality few-shot examples to make an LLM output consistent.
prompt:
Role: You are a prompt engineer specializing in in-context learning.

Task: Build few-shot examples for the task [TASK], where inputs look like [INPUT FORMAT] and outputs should look like [DESIRED OUTPUT]. Make examples diverse and cover edge cases.

Format: A short instruction line, then 4–6 input→output example pairs formatted consistently, then a note on how to add the user's real input. Keep formatting machine-friendly.

title: File Upload & Storage Design
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs secure, scalable file uploads with validation and storage strategy.
prompt:
Role: You are a backend engineer.

Task: Design file uploads for [APP] accepting [FILE TYPES, MAX SIZE, VOLUME] on [STACK]. Cover validation, storage ([S3-style/local]), and serving safely.

Format: The flow (direct-to-storage vs through-server, with reasoning); validation rules (type sniffing, not just extension); the upload endpoint/presign code; storage layout & naming; serving strategy (signed URLs/CDN); the security checklist (path traversal, SVG/XSS, size bombs).

title: Find Your Next Hobby
author: PromptDeck
description: Matches you with hobbies based on your energy, budget, and curiosity.
prompt:
Role: You are a curious generalist who has tried everything.

Task: Suggest hobbies for me: what I enjoy [ENJOYMENTS], energy after work [HIGH/LOW], budget [BUDGET], social preference [ALONE/WITH PEOPLE], space [SPACE]. Diagnose what I'm actually seeking (creation, mastery, movement, connection) from my answers.

Format: The diagnosis; 6 hobby matches — each with why it fits the diagnosis, the $-and-time cost to try it once, and the 'first session' plan; the one to try this week; and permission to quit any hobby that becomes a chore.

title: Finish Your Abandoned Projects
author: PromptDeck
description: Decides which half-finished projects to finish, and gets one done.
prompt:
Role: You are a completion coach for chronic starters.

Task: Sort my abandoned projects: [LIST HALF-DONE PROJECTS + rough % done + why each stalled]. Not everything deserves finishing — but something here does.

Format: The verdict per project (finish / officially kill with a clean conscience / shrink to a finishable version); for the ONE to finish — the redefined 'done' (smaller than original ambition), the remaining steps listed concretely, the first 30-minute session scheduled, and the anti-stall rule; plus the pattern across my stalls and its antidote.

title: Flash Sale Execution Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Runs a short flash sale that spikes revenue without cheapening the brand.
prompt:
Role: You are a promotions strategist.

Task: Plan a [DURATION: 24–72h] flash sale for [STORE/PRODUCTS] with discount room [MARGIN ROOM] and goal [CLEAR STOCK/REVENUE/LIST ACTIVATION]. Real deadline, honest scarcity, no fake timers.

Format: The offer + framing; the announcement email/SMS/social sequence with exact send times; site banner + urgency copy rules; the operations check (inventory, support coverage, site load); the post-sale price-integrity plan; and metrics to judge success.

title: Font Pairing Recommendations
author: PromptDeck
description: Recommends harmonious font pairings with usage and fallbacks.
prompt:
Role: You are a typographer.

Task: Recommend 3 font pairings for [BRAND/PROJECT] that should feel [ADJECTIVES], for use on [WEB/PRINT/APP]. Prefer accessible, widely available (e.g. Google Fonts) options.

Format: For each pairing — heading font + body font, why they work together, suggested weights/sizes, a web-safe fallback stack, and where it fits best. Recommend the strongest.

title: Form & Checkout Microcopy
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes labels, placeholders, helper text, and error copy that reduce form abandonment.
prompt:
Role: You are a UX copywriter specializing in forms.

Task: Write microcopy for the [SIGNUP/CHECKOUT/LEAD] form of [PRODUCT]. Fields: [LIST FIELDS]. Reduce anxiety at each field (why we ask, privacy, examples).

Format: Per field — label, placeholder, helper text (only where needed), and error message (specific + fixable). Plus the submit button copy (2 options), a privacy reassurance line, and a post-submit success message.

title: Free Trial → Paid Drip Sequence
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a behavior-aware email sequence that converts trial users to paid.
prompt:
Role: You are a lifecycle marketer for SaaS.

Task: Write a [TRIAL LENGTH]-day trial conversion sequence for [PRODUCT], whose aha moment is [AHA]. Emails should push toward activation actions, not just sell.

Format: For each email — day, trigger/segment note (active vs inactive), subject + preview, body ≤150 words with one CTA. Sequence: welcome/first win → activation nudge → use-case inspiration → social proof → trial-ending offer → last day.

title: Freelance Finance Setup
author: PromptDeck
description: Sets up the money side: separate accounts, tax buffer, and irregular income.
prompt:
Role: You are a finance coach for freelancers. (General guidance — a local accountant should confirm tax specifics.)

Task: Set up my freelance finances: income [MONTHLY RANGE — irregular], country [COUNTRY], current setup [CURRENT MESS/SETUP]. Smooth the irregularity; never get surprised by taxes.

Format: The account structure (income → tax buffer % → owner pay → business buffer) with the percentage logic; the monthly money routine (30 min); invoice hygiene (terms, late-fee line, follow-up cadence); the tax-buffer rule of thumb to confirm with an accountant; and the 3-month emergency-buffer target.

title: Freelance Niche Selection
author: PromptDeck
description: Chooses a profitable freelance niche from your skills and market demand.
prompt:
Role: You are a freelance positioning strategist.

Task: Help me pick a niche: my skills [SKILLS], experience/industries touched [BACKGROUND], what I enjoy [PREFERENCES]. Score niche candidates on demand, my credibility, willingness-to-pay, and enjoyment.

Format: 5 niche candidates in a scoring table; the winner with the full positioning line ('I help X do Y'); the credibility bridge (how my background maps); the first-3-clients plan in that niche; and the escape hatch (niching is a marketing choice, not a prison — when to widen).

title: Freelance Project Proposal
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a proposal that wins the project without racing to the bottom on price.
prompt:
Role: You are a freelance proposal expert.

Task: Write a proposal for [CLIENT] who needs [PROJECT]. From our conversation: their goal is [GOAL], their worry is [CONCERN], budget signal [BUDGET SIGNAL]. Lead with their outcome; price appears after value is established.

Format: Their situation & goal (their words); the approach (phases, what happens when); deliverables; timeline; investment (1–2 options); why me (1 short proof paragraph); next step with expiry date; and the 3 personalization spots that beat template-smell.

title: Friendship Maintenance System
author: PromptDeck
description: Keeps important friendships alive despite distance and busy seasons.
prompt:
Role: You are a relationships researcher who takes friendship seriously.

Task: Build a friendship maintenance system for me: the people who matter [FRIENDS + CONTEXT: where they live, how we drifted], my social energy [HIGH/LOW/SEASONAL]. Friendships die of logistics, not conflict — lower the logistics.

Format: The tier-honest sort (close/keep-warm/let-drift-guiltlessly); per close friend — the reconnection message (specific, no 'we should catch up sometime'), and the recurring low-effort ritual matched to our reality; the 15-minute weekly friendship habit; and the reframe for guilt about drifted friendships.

title: Frontend Accessibility Fixes
author: PromptDeck
description: Fixes concrete a11y issues in component code with correct semantics and ARIA.
prompt:
Role: You are a frontend engineer specializing in accessibility.

Task: Fix the accessibility of the component code below ([FRAMEWORK]). Check semantics, keyboard support, focus management, labels, and contrast-dependent logic. Prefer native elements over ARIA bolt-ons.

Format: (1) issues found with WCAG references; (2) the corrected code; (3) what changed and why; (4) how to test each fix (keyboard + screen reader steps). Code: [PASTE COMPONENT].

title: Frontend State Management Decision
author: PromptDeck
description: Chooses the right state approach for your app instead of defaulting to a library.
prompt:
Role: You are a frontend architect.

Task: Recommend state management for a [FRAMEWORK] app with characteristics [SERVER DATA/LOCAL UI STATE/REALTIME/FORMS COMPLEXITY]. Separate server-cache state from client state before picking tools.

Format: The state inventory (what state exists, categorized); the recommendation per category (with library only where warranted); code patterns for the 2 trickiest cases; migration path if we outgrow it; the over-engineering trap to avoid.

title: Full Landing Page Copy Framework
author: PromptDeck
description: Generates complete conversion-focused landing page copy, section by section.
prompt:
Role: You are a direct-response conversion copywriter.

Task: Write full landing page copy for [PRODUCT/SERVICE], aimed at [AUDIENCE], whose core desired outcome is [OUTCOME] and main objection is [OBJECTION]. Sell benefits, not features, in a [TONE] tone.

Format: Deliver labeled sections — (1) Hero: headline, subheadline, CTA button text; (2) Problem/agitation (3 bullets); (3) Solution & how it works (3 steps); (4) Benefits (4 bullets); (5) social-proof placeholder + 2 testimonial formats; (6) objection-handling FAQ (4 Qs); (7) final CTA with urgency.

title: Generate a Help-Center FAQ
author: PromptDeck
description: Creates a thorough, organized help-center FAQ that deflects common tickets.
prompt:
Role: You are a knowledge-base writer.

Task: Create a help-center FAQ for [PRODUCT/SERVICE] using details [WHAT IT DOES, PRICING, PLATFORMS, POLICIES]. Answers should be complete enough to deflect a ticket. Flag missing details with [PLACEHOLDER].

Format: 12–15 questions grouped into sections (Getting Started, Billing, Features, Troubleshooting, Account), each answered in 2–4 friendly sentences.

title: Generate Practice Problems + Solutions
author: PromptDeck
description: Creates graded practice problems with worked solutions for any subject.
prompt:
Role: You are a subject tutor.

Task: Create practice problems on [TOPIC] for [LEVEL], ranging easy to challenging, that reinforce [SPECIFIC SKILL].

Format: 8 problems ordered by difficulty. Below a divider, provide fully worked step-by-step solutions and a common mistake to avoid for each.

title: Generate Unit Tests for This Code
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a thorough unit-test suite covering happy paths, edge cases, and errors.
prompt:
Role: You are a test engineer.

Task: Write unit tests for the code below using [LANGUAGE / TEST FRAMEWORK]. Cover happy path, edge cases, invalid inputs, and error handling; mock external dependencies; use behavior-describing test names.

Format: The test file, then a short list of any ambiguous behaviors that need clarification. Code: [PASTE CODE].

title: Gift Guide Content & Collection
author: PromptDeck
description: Creates a shoppable gift guide that lifts seasonal sales and SEO.
prompt:
Role: You are an e-commerce content marketer.

Task: Create a gift guide for [STORE] for [OCCASION/SEASON], products available [PRODUCT RANGE], targeting gift-buyers for [RECIPIENT TYPES]. Guides sell by solving the buyer's anxiety: 'will they like it?'

Format: The guide structure — recipient-based sections ('for the X who Y') with 4–6 products each and a one-line why-they'll-love-it per product; the guide's intro copy; SEO title/meta targeting '[occasion] gifts for [recipient]'; email + social promo copy for the guide.

title: Git Command Helper
author: PromptDeck
description: Gives the exact git commands (and safe alternatives) for what you want to do.
prompt:
Role: You are a git expert who prevents footguns.

Task: I want to [DESCRIBE GIT GOAL]. My current situation: [CONTEXT/STATE]. Give me the safest way to do it.

Format: (1) the exact commands in order; (2) what each does in plain English; (3) how to undo if it goes wrong; (4) a warning if any step is destructive and a safer alternative.

title: Give Useful Design Feedback (Non-Designer)
author: PromptDeck
description: Helps non-designers give feedback designers can actually use.
prompt:
Role: You are a design collaboration coach.

Task: Help me give feedback on a design for [WHAT/GOAL]. My raw reactions: [WHAT I THINK: 'don't like the colors', 'feels crowded']. Translate reactions into problems, not prescriptions — designers solve problems, they don't take orders.

Format: My reactions translated (each: the underlying concern + the goal it threatens, phrased as a question or problem); what to say vs what I almost said; the feedback structure (what works first, concerns tied to goals, priorities clear); and 3 questions to ask the designer before judging.

title: Giveaway / Contest Campaign
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs a giveaway that grows the right audience, not prize hunters.
prompt:
Role: You are a promotions strategist.

Task: Design a giveaway for [BRAND] on [PLATFORM] to grow [FOLLOWERS/EMAIL LIST] with people who actually fit [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Pick a prize that filters for fit, not generic prize hunters.

Format: Prize recommendation + why it filters; entry mechanics; the announcement post copy; rules summary (with a compliance disclaimer to verify local laws/platform rules); follow-up plan to convert entrants; metrics.

title: Glossary / 'What Is X' Definition Page
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a definitive 'What is X' page optimized for definitions and snippets.
prompt:
Role: You are an SEO writer for definition-intent queries.

Task: Write a 'What is [TERM]?' page for [AUDIENCE]. Lead with a snippet-ready 40–55 word definition, then depth.

Format: Title + meta; the crisp definition; how it works; a concrete example; why it matters; related terms (linked); FAQ (3 Qs); a short CTA. Keep jargon minimal.

title: Grading Rubric Generator
author: PromptDeck
description: Creates a clear, fair grading rubric for an assignment with performance levels.
prompt:
Role: You are an assessment specialist.

Task: Create a grading rubric for the assignment [ASSIGNMENT] at [LEVEL], worth [POINTS/WEIGHT], measuring [KEY SKILLS/CRITERIA].

Format: A rubric table — Criterion | Excellent | Proficient | Developing | Beginning | Weight. Keep level descriptions concrete and observable. Add a one-line note on how to give useful feedback with it.

title: Grant / Program Application Writer
author: PromptDeck
description: Drafts compelling answers for a grant or accelerator application.
prompt:
Role: You are a grant-writing specialist.

Task: Draft answers for the [GRANT/ACCELERATOR] application for [ORGANIZATION/PROJECT]. Questions: [PASTE QUESTIONS]. Our story/impact/numbers: [DETAILS]. Match their evaluation criteria [CRITERIA if known]; be specific and measurable, never grandiose.

Format: Per question — a draft answer within word limits [LIMITS], leading with the strongest concrete fact. Then 3 overall weaknesses in our application and how to shore them up.

title: Group Project Coordination Kit
author: PromptDeck
description: Organizes a student group project so work is fair and deadlines hold.
prompt:
Role: You are a project coordinator for student teams.

Task: Organize our group project: [ASSIGNMENT/DELIVERABLE], due [DATE], team [N PEOPLE + STRENGTHS if known]. Prevent the classic failures: freeloading, last-minute merge chaos, one person doing everything.

Format: The task breakdown with fair allocation logic; the milestone schedule with an integration deadline well before the due date; the working agreement (communication channel, response time, what happens if someone ghosts); the merge/assembly plan; and the professor-contact criteria if someone truly bails.

title: Growth Experiment Backlog (ICE)
author: PromptDeck
description: Generates and prioritizes growth experiments using ICE scoring.
prompt:
Role: You are a growth lead.

Task: Generate 15 growth experiments for [PRODUCT] focused on [METRIC TO MOVE], given context [CURRENT CHANNELS/CONSTRAINTS]. Score with ICE (Impact, Confidence, Ease, 1–10).

Format: A table — Experiment | Hypothesis | ICE scores | Total | Effort (S/M/L); then the top 3 with a minimal test design each (what to ship, sample/duration gut-check, decision rule).

title: Growth Funnel (AARRR) Audit
author: PromptDeck
description: Audits your funnel across acquisition→revenue and finds the biggest leak.
prompt:
Role: You are a growth marketer.

Task: Audit my funnel for [PRODUCT] using AARRR. My current numbers/observations per stage: [ACQUISITION / ACTIVATION / RETENTION / REFERRAL / REVENUE DATA]. Find the biggest leak and what to test.

Format: A stage-by-stage table — Stage | Current state | Benchmark sense-check | Diagnosis; then the #1 leak, 3 experiments to fix it (with hypothesis + metric), and the order to run them.

title: Habit-Building Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Turns a goal into a realistic habit plan using proven behavior-change tactics.
prompt:
Role: You are a behavior-change coach.

Task: Help me build the habit [HABIT] toward goal [GOAL], given my obstacles [OBSTACLES] and daily routine [ROUTINE]. Use habit-stacking, tiny starts, and cue design.

Format: A specific implementation intention ('after [existing habit], I will [tiny version]'); an environment tweak; a weekly ramp; how to track it; a plan for missed days (never miss twice); and one reward.

title: Handling Discount Requests
author: PromptDeck
description: Responds to 'can you do better on price?' without instantly caving.
prompt:
Role: You are a sales negotiation coach.

Task: The prospect asked for a discount on [PRODUCT] at [PRICE]. Context: [DEAL CONTEXT]. Give me responses that protect value — trade, don't give; get something for anything.

Format: (1) the clarifying question to ask first; (2) 3 response scripts — hold with value re-anchor, trade (longer term/case study/timing) for discount, and the walk-away-warm version; (3) what never to say; (4) internal guardrail: max concession and why.

title: Hashtag Strategy & Sets
author: PromptDeck
description: Creates layered hashtag sets (broad/niche/branded) per content pillar.
prompt:
Role: You are a social media growth specialist.

Task: Build hashtag sets for [ACCOUNT/NICHE] on [PLATFORM] for content pillars [PILLARS]. Layer sizes: a few broad, mostly mid/niche, plus branded.

Format: Per pillar — a ready-to-paste set of 10–15 hashtags labeled (broad/niche/branded/community); rotation advice to avoid staleness; 3 hashtags to avoid and why.

title: Headline Generator (4U Formula)
author: PromptDeck
description: Creates headlines scored on Useful, Urgent, Unique, Ultra-specific.
prompt:
Role: You are a headline specialist.

Task: Write 12 headlines for [CONTENT/OFFER] aimed at [AUDIENCE], then score each on the 4 U's — Useful, Urgent, Unique, Ultra-specific (1–4 each).

Format: A table — Headline | U/U/U/U scores | Total. Rewrite the top 2 to push their weakest U higher, and note where each headline fits best (email, ad, blog).

title: High-Converting B2B Cold Email
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a concise, personalized cold email with one value prop and a low-friction CTA.
prompt:
Role: You are a top B2B cold-email copywriter.

Task: Write a cold email for [WHAT YOU SELL] to [ROLE] at [COMPANY TYPE], whose likely pain is [PAIN POINT], desired action [BOOK A CALL/REPLY]. Under 120 words, personalized first line [PLACEHOLDER], one value proposition, conversational, no 'I hope this finds you well'.

Format: The email plus 3 subject-line options under 6 words each. End with a single low-commitment CTA phrased as a question.

title: High-Converting CTA & Button Copy
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes tested call-to-action and button variations that lift clicks.
prompt:
Role: You are a conversion copywriter focused on microcopy.

Task: Write CTA and button copy for [CONTEXT, e.g. a pricing page for a SaaS], where the desired action is [ACTION] and the main hesitation is [HESITATION].

Format: 10 button variations (2–5 words) grouped by psychology (value, low-risk, urgency, curiosity, ownership), plus 3 supporting microcopy lines to reduce friction near the button. Mark the top 2 to test.

title: High-Stakes / VIP Customer Reply
author: PromptDeck
description: Crafts a careful reply to an angry, high-value, or escalation-risk customer.
prompt:
Role: You are a senior escalations specialist for [COMPANY].

Task: Write a reply to a high-value customer who is [ANGRY/THREATENING TO CHURN/ESCALATING] about [ISSUE]. History: [CONTEXT]. De-escalate, own the problem, and offer a concrete path forward without over-committing.

Format: A reply that validates their frustration specifically, states what happened without excuses, lays out exact next steps with owners and timing, and offers a direct line of contact. Under 180 words.

title: Home Declutter & Organize Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Builds a room-by-room decluttering plan you can actually finish.
prompt:
Role: You are a professional organizer.

Task: Create a decluttering plan for [SPACE/HOME], with time available [TIME], and challenge [SENTIMENTAL ITEMS / SMALL SPACE / etc.]. Make it doable in small sessions.

Format: A room-by-room order (easiest wins first); a per-session checklist; a simple keep/donate/toss decision rule; storage suggestions; and a maintenance habit to stop it rebuilding.

title: Homework Helper (Guide, Don't Solve)
author: PromptDeck
description: Helps a student work through a problem without doing it for them.
prompt:
Role: You are a tutor whose rule is: never give the answer; build the student's ability to find it.

Task: Help me with this problem: [PASTE PROBLEM]. My level: [LEVEL]. My attempt so far: [MY WORK or 'stuck at the start'].

Format: Diagnose where my understanding breaks; ask a guiding question or give the smallest useful hint; wait for my response before continuing; if I'm still stuck after 2 hints, show the next single step (not the whole solution) and check I understand why. End each session with me re-solving a similar problem alone.

title: Household Emergency Preparedness
author: PromptDeck
description: Builds a sensible household emergency kit and family plan.
prompt:
Role: You are an emergency-preparedness educator (practical, not doomsday).

Task: Build an emergency plan for my household: [PEOPLE/PETS], location risks [EARTHQUAKE/FLOOD/STORM/OUTAGE], home type [HOME TYPE]. Sensible 72-hour readiness, not bunker-building.

Format: The kit checklist (water/food/meds/docs/power) with quantities for my household; the family communication plan (meeting points, out-of-area contact); document backup steps; a grab-bag list; the twice-yearly refresh ritual; and the first 3 things to do this weekend.

title: HTML Email Template Design Spec
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs an email template that renders reliably across clients.
prompt:
Role: You are an email designer who knows client quirks.

Task: Design a reusable email template for [BRAND] used for [NEWSLETTER/TRANSACTIONAL/PROMO]. Brand: [COLORS/FONTS]. It must survive Outlook and dark mode.

Format: The layout structure (600px, single column logic); a section inventory (header, hero, content blocks, CTA, footer) with specs; dark-mode and image-off fallbacks; font stack; the CTA button approach (bulletproof); accessibility notes; testing checklist.

title: Human-Like Rewriter (Anti-AI-Detection)
author: PromptDeck
description: Rewrites robotic or AI text to sound natural and human, with varied rhythm.
prompt:
Role: You are a skilled human editor with a distinct, natural voice.

Task: Rewrite the text below so it reads as if written by a thoughtful human. Vary sentence length (burstiness), use natural transitions and occasional contractions, add a clear point of view, and remove filler and clichés. Keep the original meaning, facts, and structure. Match this tone: [TONE] and reading level: [READING LEVEL].

Format: Output only the rewritten text. Text: [PASTE TEXT].

title: Human-Written SEO Long-Form Article
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a 100% unique, plagiarism-free, SEO-optimized long-form article with outline, meta description, and FAQ.
prompt:
Role: You are an expert SEO copywriter and subject-matter authority.

Task: Write a comprehensive, 100% original, plagiarism-free article about [TOPIC], targeting the primary keyword [KEYWORD] for [informational/commercial] search intent. Naturally place the keyword in the H1, first paragraph, and 2–3 H2s without stuffing. Write in [LANGUAGE] with a [TONE] tone, at an 8th-grade reading level, minimum 1500 words.

Format: Output in this order — (1) SEO title (≤60 chars) and meta description (≤155 chars); (2) H1; (3) hooking intro; (4) H2/H3 sections with short paragraphs and bullets; (5) a 5-question FAQ formatted for FAQ schema; (6) a short conclusion with a CTA. Output only the article.

title: Icon Set Design Spec
author: PromptDeck
description: Specs a consistent icon set: grid, stroke, corners, and metaphors.
prompt:
Role: You are a UI icon designer.

Task: Spec an icon set for [PRODUCT] needing icons for [LIST CONCEPTS]. Style context: [EXISTING UI STYLE].

Format: The style spec (grid size, stroke weight, corner radius, filled vs outline, padding rules); per concept — 2 metaphor options with the clearer one starred; consistency rules; the 3 hardest concepts and how to keep them recognizable at 16px.

title: Illustration / Visual Asset Style Guide
author: PromptDeck
description: Defines a consistent illustration or visual asset style for a brand.
prompt:
Role: You are a brand illustrator.

Task: Define an illustration style for [BRAND] used across [WHERE: web, empty states, social]. Brand personality: [ADJECTIVES]. It must be reproducible by different artists/tools consistently.

Format: The style spec — shapes/geometry, line treatment, color usage rules, character style (if any), texture/shading, composition rules; 5 example scenes described; do/don't pairs; a prompt template for generating drafts with AI tools.

title: Image Alt Text & Filename Optimizer
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes accessible, keyword-aware alt text and SEO filenames for a set of images.
prompt:
Role: You are an SEO and accessibility specialist.

Task: For each image I describe in [IMAGE DESCRIPTIONS], targeting [KEYWORD] where relevant, write descriptive alt text and an SEO-friendly filename. Alt text must describe the image accurately for screen readers first, with keywords only where natural (never stuffed).

Format: A table — Image | Alt Text (≤125 chars) | Suggested Filename (hyphenated, lowercase).

title: Improve My Prompt (Meta-Prompt)
author: PromptDeck
description: Rewrites a rough prompt into a clear, structured, high-performing one with variables.
prompt:
Role: You are a prompt engineer.

Task: Improve the prompt below so it produces better, more reliable results. Point out what's vague, then rewrite it with a clear role, task, context, constraints, output format, and [FILL-IN VARIABLES].

Format: (1) what's weak; (2) the rewritten, copy-paste-ready prompt; (3) a short 'why this is better'; (4) one optional advanced version. Prompt: [PASTE PROMPT].

title: Inbox Zero Email System
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs a sustainable email triage system for your actual volume.
prompt:
Role: You are a productivity systems designer.

Task: Design an email system for me: volume [EMAILS/DAY], current pain [PAIN: buried requests, constant checking], tools [EMAIL CLIENT]. Sustainable beats perfect — no system that takes an hour a day to maintain.

Format: The triage rules (the 4 moves: do/defer/delegate/delete with time thresholds); folder/label structure (minimal); checking schedule with batching; templates for the 3 replies I send most; the unsubscribe/filter purge process; and the weekly 10-minute maintenance ritual.

title: Incident / Outage Status Updates
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes calm, clear status-page updates for the full lifecycle of an incident.
prompt:
Role: You are an incident communications lead.

Task: Write status-page updates for an incident where [WHAT'S AFFECTED] for [PRODUCT], current state [INVESTIGATING/IDENTIFIED/MONITORING/RESOLVED]. Be honest, specific about impact, and jargon-free; never over-promise timelines.

Format: Four short updates — Investigating, Identified, Monitoring, Resolved — each 2–4 sentences with impact, what we're doing, and when we'll update next.

title: Inclusive Job Description Writer
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a compelling, inclusive job description that attracts strong candidates.
prompt:
Role: You are an inclusive-hiring recruiter.

Task: Write a job description for a [ROLE] at [COMPANY] (which does [WHAT]). Use bias-free language, avoid gatekeeping requirements, and keep the must-have list realistic.

Format: An engaging company/mission intro; day-to-day responsibilities; must-have vs nice-to-have; what success looks like in 90 days; what we offer; a low-friction application step.

title: Influencer / Creator Outreach DM
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a personalized outreach message to partner with a creator.
prompt:
Role: You are an influencer marketing manager.

Task: Write outreach to [CREATOR/NICHE] to collaborate on [CAMPAIGN/PRODUCT], with a clear value exchange [WHAT THEY GET]. Personalized, warm, no spam; make the ask easy.

Format: A short DM (≤90 words) with a personalized opener [PLACEHOLDER], one line on why them, the offer, and a low-friction next step. Then a slightly longer email version and 3 subject lines.

title: Infographic Content & Structure
author: PromptDeck
description: Structures data and narrative for an effective infographic.
prompt:
Role: You are an information designer.

Task: Structure an infographic about [TOPIC/DATA] for [AUDIENCE/CHANNEL]. Data available: [PASTE DATA/POINTS]. One story, told visually — not a decorated data dump.

Format: The single takeaway (headline); the narrative flow (3–5 sections in reading order); per section — the data point, the right chart/visual form for it, and the caption line; the hierarchy spec (what's biggest); source citation placement; and what data to cut for focus.

title: Instagram Carousel Script
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a slide-by-slide carousel with a scroll-stopping cover and save-worthy payoff.
prompt:
Role: You are a short-form content designer.

Task: Create a [7–10]-slide carousel for [PLATFORM] on [TOPIC] for [AUDIENCE]. Slide 1 is a bold hook; middle slides carry one concrete idea each; second-to-last recaps; last is a CTA (follow/save/comment). Make it genuinely save-worthy.

Format: For each slide — on-slide text (≤2 lines) + a tiny design note. Then the post caption (hook + context + CTA) and 5 suggested hashtags.

title: Insurance Coverage Review Prep
author: PromptDeck
description: Prepares questions and checks before buying or renewing insurance.
prompt:
Role: You are a consumer insurance educator. (General guidance — verify specifics with a licensed agent in your region.)

Task: Prepare me to review [INSURANCE TYPE: health/auto/home/term life] for my situation: [SITUATION: family, assets, budget]. The goal is understanding what I'm buying — most people discover gaps at claim time.

Format: The coverage-needs logic for my situation; the questions to ask the agent (coverage limits, exclusions, claim process, waiting periods); the fine-print items to actually read; common gaps for this insurance type; the comparison table to fill across quotes; and the over-insurance traps too.

title: Internal Escalation / Handoff Note
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a crisp internal escalation note so the next team can act fast.
prompt:
Role: You are a support agent escalating a case to [ENGINEERING/BILLING/MANAGER].

Task: Summarize the issue below into an internal escalation note that lets the next team act without re-reading the whole thread.

Format: Customer & account; Issue in one line; Steps already taken; Impact & urgency; What we need from you; Relevant links/IDs [PLACEHOLDER]. Neutral and factual. Thread: [PASTE THREAD].

title: Internal Linking Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Builds an internal linking map to distribute authority and boost target pages.
prompt:
Role: You are an SEO architect.

Task: Create an internal linking plan for my site. Priority pages to boost: [TARGET PAGES]. Existing pages: [LIST PAGES/TOPICS]. Suggest contextual links with varied, natural anchor text.

Format: A table — From Page | To Page | Suggested Anchor Text | Placement hint. Then 3 orphan-page risks and a rule of thumb for future posts.

title: International SEO / hreflang Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Plans hreflang, URL structure, and localization for multi-country SEO.
prompt:
Role: You are an international SEO consultant.

Task: Plan international SEO for [SITE] expanding to markets [COUNTRIES/LANGUAGES]. Recommend URL structure (ccTLD/subfolder/subdomain) with reasoning, hreflang implementation, and what to localize beyond translation.

Format: (1) URL structure recommendation; (2) sample hreflang tags for [EXAMPLE PAGE]; (3) a localization checklist; (4) 3 common pitfalls.

title: Internationalization (i18n) Setup
author: PromptDeck
description: Sets up translations, formatting, and locale handling properly from the start.
prompt:
Role: You are an engineer who has shipped multilingual apps.

Task: Set up i18n for a [FRAMEWORK] app targeting languages [LANGUAGES]. Cover translations, plurals, dates/numbers/currency, and RTL if applicable.

Format: Library choice + setup code; translation file structure with naming conventions; the component usage pattern; plural/interpolation examples; date/number formatting approach; the 5 hardcoded-string traps to sweep for; workflow for translators.

title: Interview Thank-You & Follow-Up Notes
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes post-interview notes that reinforce your candidacy without groveling.
prompt:
Role: You are an interview strategist.

Task: Write my post-interview follow-up for [ROLE] at [COMPANY]. What we discussed: [KEY MOMENTS/TOPICS]. Any stumble to recover: [WEAK ANSWER if any]. Add value — don't just thank.

Format: The thank-you email (sent within 24h) — a specific conversation callback, one idea/thought extending a topic we discussed, the brief stumble-recovery line if needed, and enthusiasm without desperation; the variant per interviewer if panel; and the follow-up cadence if silence follows (when, what, when to stop).

title: Inventory & Reorder Planning
author: PromptDeck
description: Plans stock levels and reorder points to avoid stockouts and dead stock.
prompt:
Role: You are an inventory planning consultant.

Task: Help me plan inventory for [PRODUCTS] with sales velocity [UNITS/PERIOD], supplier lead time [LEAD TIME], and cash constraints [BUDGET]. Balance stockout risk against dead-stock risk.

Format: The reorder-point math for my numbers (with safety stock reasoning); a reorder calendar; seasonal adjustment logic; the slow-mover identification rule and clearance tactics; and a simple weekly stock-review ritual with the 3 numbers to check.

title: Investor / Board Update Memo
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a concise monthly investor update that builds trust.
prompt:
Role: You are a startup CEO's chief of staff.

Task: Write a monthly investor update for [COMPANY] covering: metrics [METRICS], wins [WINS], challenges [CHALLENGES], and asks [ASKS]. Be honest about the bad news — investors trust founders who share it early.

Format: TL;DR (3 lines); metrics vs last month table; highlights; lowlights (real ones); asks (specific + actionable); a personal closing note. Under 500 words.

title: Investor Pitch Deck Outline
author: PromptDeck
description: Builds a classic 10-slide investor pitch deck outline with talking points.
prompt:
Role: You are a pitch-deck consultant.

Task: Outline an investor pitch deck for [STARTUP], which does [WHAT] for [MARKET], raising [ROUND] to achieve [MILESTONES].

Format: A slide-by-slide outline — Problem; Solution; Product; Market size; Business model; Traction; Competition; Go-to-market; Team; The ask & use of funds. For each slide, give the one key message and a supporting talking point.

title: Job Search Strategy & Weekly Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Builds a structured job search plan beyond spray-and-pray applications.
prompt:
Role: You are a job search strategist.

Task: Build a search strategy for me targeting [TARGET ROLE] in [INDUSTRY/LOCATION/REMOTE], from my background [BACKGROUND], timeline [TIMELINE]. Prioritize referral paths over cold applications.

Format: The target company list criteria (with 3 tiers); weekly activity plan (networking touches, tailored applications, skill signals) with realistic numbers; the tracking system; how to convert connections to referrals; and 3 signs to adjust strategy after 4 weeks.

title: Job-Seeker Visibility Content Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Plans posts that make recruiters and hiring managers find you.
prompt:
Role: You are a career-brand strategist.

Task: Plan 4 weeks of LinkedIn content for a [ROLE] job-seeker targeting [TARGET ROLE/INDUSTRY], drawing from my experience [EXPERIENCE HIGHLIGHTS]. Show competence publicly; never sound desperate.

Format: 8 post outlines (2/week) — each with hook, the professional insight/story, and engagement question; which searchable keywords to weave in; commenting strategy on target-company posts; the one post type to avoid while job hunting.

title: Keyword Cannibalization Fixer
author: PromptDeck
description: Diagnoses pages competing for the same keyword and prescribes merge/redirect/differentiate.
prompt:
Role: You are an SEO consultant.

Task: These pages of mine may be cannibalizing each other for [KEYWORD(S)]: [LIST PAGES + THEIR FOCUS]. Decide for each conflict: merge, 301, differentiate intent, or canonicalize — and explain why.

Format: A table — Pages in conflict | Diagnosis | Action | New target keyword for each survivor. Then the execution order.

title: Keyword Cluster & Content Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Builds a topic-cluster keyword strategy: pillar page plus supporting articles with intent and funnel stage.
prompt:
Role: You are a senior SEO strategist.

Task: From the seed keyword [KEYWORD] in the [INDUSTRY] niche, build a topic-cluster content plan with 1 pillar page and 8–12 supporting articles that internally link to it.

Format: A markdown table with columns — Cluster | Page Type (Pillar/Supporting) | Working Title | Target Keyword | Search Intent | Funnel Stage (TOFU/MOFU/BOFU) | Suggested Word Count. After the table, list 5 internal-linking recommendations and 3 low-competition long-tail keywords to target first.

title: Keyword Search-Intent Classifier
author: PromptDeck
description: Classifies a list of keywords by intent and maps each to the right content type.
prompt:
Role: You are an SEO analyst specializing in search intent.

Task: Classify each keyword in [KEYWORD LIST] by intent (informational, navigational, commercial, transactional) and recommend the best content type and funnel stage to target it.

Format: A table — Keyword | Intent | Best Content Type | Funnel Stage | Priority (High/Med/Low). End with the 3 keywords to target first and why.

title: Knowledge Base Article Writer
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a clear, scannable help article that solves the issue on first read.
prompt:
Role: You are a support content writer.

Task: Write a knowledge base article for [PRODUCT] on [TOPIC/TASK/ISSUE]. Reader: a frustrated user mid-problem — front-load the fix. Details: [STEPS/DETAILS].

Format: A task-based title ('How to…'); a one-line answer-first summary; prerequisites; numbered steps with the exact UI labels in bold; a screenshot placeholder per tricky step; a troubleshooting section (if X, then Y); related articles; and metadata (search keywords users would type).

title: KPI Dashboard Spec
author: PromptDeck
description: Defines the right KPIs and dashboard layout for a team or goal.
prompt:
Role: You are an analytics lead.

Task: Define a KPI dashboard for [TEAM/FUNCTION] pursuing [GOAL]. Pick metrics that drive decisions, not vanity metrics.

Format: 5–8 KPIs — name, definition/formula, why it matters, target, and data source. Then a suggested dashboard layout (what's top-level vs drill-down) and 2 leading indicators to watch.

title: Landing Hero Concepts (Copy + Visual)
author: PromptDeck
description: Generates hero-section concepts pairing headline copy with a visual direction.
prompt:
Role: You are a creative director for web.

Task: Create 3 hero-section concepts for [PRODUCT] targeting [AUDIENCE] with goal [GOAL]. Each pairs copy with a visual direction.

Format: For each concept — headline, subheadline, CTA text, and a described visual/layout direction (imagery, style, mood). Note which converts best and why.

title: Language Conversation Practice Partner
author: PromptDeck
description: Turns the AI into a patient conversation partner in your target language.
prompt:
Role: You are a friendly native speaker of [TARGET LANGUAGE] and language tutor.

Task: Have a conversation with me in [TARGET LANGUAGE] at [LEVEL: A2/B1/etc.] about [TOPIC or everyday situations]. Correct my errors gently: recast my sentence correctly, note the error type briefly in [MY LANGUAGE], then continue the conversation naturally.

Format: Stay in the conversation (short turns, questions to keep me talking). After every 10 exchanges, give a mini-summary: my 3 recurring errors, 3 new words/phrases I should reuse, and one grammar pattern to practice. Start now.

title: Layoff Recovery & Restart Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Structures the practical and narrative recovery after a layoff.
prompt:
Role: You are a career transition coach.

Task: Help me recover from a layoff from [ROLE] at [COMPANY] after [TENURE]. Situation: [SEVERANCE/RUNWAY/MARKET]. Handle both logistics and the story I tell.

Format: Week-1 practical checklist (references, portfolio evidence, benefits, finances); the layoff narrative for interviews (factual, no bitterness — 2 versions: one-liner and full answer); the announcement post (optional) that attracts leads; the structured search plan; and permission to take a real week off first.

title: Lead Qualification (MEDDIC) Scorecard
author: PromptDeck
description: Qualifies a deal with MEDDIC and flags what's missing before you invest time.
prompt:
Role: You are a sales qualification coach.

Task: Qualify this opportunity using MEDDIC. What I know: [DEAL NOTES]. Score each element and tell me what to find out next.

Format: A table — Metrics | Economic buyer | Decision criteria | Decision process | Identify pain | Champion — each with Known info | Confidence (H/M/L) | Question to close the gap. End with a go/slow/no-go call and the single most important next step.

title: Learn by Teaching It Back (Feynman)
author: PromptDeck
description: Uses the Feynman technique — you explain, the AI finds your gaps.
prompt:
Role: You are a curious student who knows nothing about my topic (Feynman technique partner).

Task: I will explain [TOPIC] to you. Ask naive-but-sharp follow-up questions wherever my explanation is fuzzy, jumps steps, or hides behind jargon. Push until I either explain it simply or admit a gap.

Format: Conversational. After my explanation attempt, ask up to 3 probing questions, one at a time. When we finish, list: the gaps you exposed, the jargon I leaned on, and the 2 concepts I should restudy. Ready — here's my explanation: [EXPLAIN THE TOPIC].

title: Learning Support & Accommodation Ideas
author: PromptDeck
description: Suggests classroom accommodations for a student's learning needs.
prompt:
Role: You are a learning-support specialist. (Suggestions to discuss with the school's specialists — not a diagnosis.)

Task: Suggest accommodations for a student who [OBSERVED NEEDS: focus, reading pace, anxiety at tests, processing time] in a [GRADE/SUBJECT] class. Accommodations level the field without lowering the bar.

Format: Accommodation ideas grouped by moment (instruction, practice, assessment, environment) — each with how to implement it discreetly; which need documentation vs which any teacher can just do; how to frame it with the student non-stigmatizingly; and signs the plan needs a specialist review.

title: Legacy Code Refactor Strategy
author: PromptDeck
description: Plans a safe, incremental attack on a legacy module without a rewrite.
prompt:
Role: You are a legacy-code specialist (strangler-fig school, not big-rewrite school).

Task: Plan improving [LEGACY MODULE/SYSTEM]: [DESCRIBE STATE — no tests, tangled deps, etc.]. Constraint: it must keep working in production throughout.

Format: (1) characterization tests to write first; (2) the seams to cut at; (3) an incremental step plan where every step ships safely; (4) what to leave ugly on purpose; (5) how to stop the rot (boundaries for new code); (6) the argument against rewriting.

title: LinkedIn Connection & Outreach Message
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a non-salesy LinkedIn connection note and a value-first follow-up.
prompt:
Role: You are a social-selling specialist.

Task: Write LinkedIn outreach to [PROSPECT ROLE] at [COMPANY TYPE], whose pain is [PAIN], to eventually discuss [SOLUTION]. Sound human and curious, not salesy.

Format: (1) a connection note (≤300 chars) with a genuine, personalized reason; (2) a value-first follow-up after they accept (≤100 words) that gives before it asks; (3) a soft CTA. Include [PLACEHOLDERS] for personalization.

title: LinkedIn Headline & About Rewrite
author: PromptDeck
description: Rewrites your LinkedIn headline and About to be keyword-rich and magnetic to recruiters.
prompt:
Role: You are a personal-branding strategist.

Task: Rewrite my LinkedIn headline and About. My goal: [ROLE/WHAT I WANT TO BE HIRED FOR]; achievements: [LIST]; audience: [RECRUITERS/CLIENTS].

Format: (1) 3 headline options (≤220 chars, value-led, keyword-rich); (2) a first-person About — hook, what I do & for whom, proof with metrics, 3–4 searchable keywords, a soft CTA. Confident, not braggy.

title: Local SEO Google Business Profile Post
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes an optimized Google Business Profile post to boost local visibility and calls.
prompt:
Role: You are a local SEO specialist.

Task: Write a Google Business Profile post for [BUSINESS] in [CITY], promoting [OFFER/UPDATE/EVENT] to attract nearby customers. Include the primary local keyword [KEYWORD] naturally and a clear next step.

Format: A post of 150–300 words with a hook, the offer/value, local relevance, and a CTA. Suggest a button type (Call/Book/Learn more) and 3 relevant categories/keywords. Keep it warm and specific to the location.

title: Local Service Location Page
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a unique, non-doorway location landing page for a service business.
prompt:
Role: You are a local SEO copywriter.

Task: Write a location page for [SERVICE] in [CITY/AREA] for [BUSINESS]. Make it genuinely local (landmarks, neighborhoods, local pain points [LOCAL DETAILS]) — not a find-and-replace doorway page.

Format: H1 + meta; intro with local relevance; services offered; why locals choose us; local proof/testimonial placeholder; service-area list; FAQ (3 local Qs); CTA with NAP placeholder.

title: Logging & Observability Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Defines what to log, metric, and alert on so 3am debugging is possible.
prompt:
Role: You are an SRE.

Task: Design observability for [SERVICE/APP] on [STACK]. Critical user flows: [FLOWS]. Define structured logging, key metrics, and alerts that fire on symptoms (user pain) not causes.

Format: Log schema (fields + levels + what never to log); the 4 golden signals applied to my service; alert rules with thresholds and runbook one-liners; trace points for the critical flow; a 'debugging at 3am' checklist.

title: Logo Concept & Generation Prompt
author: PromptDeck
description: Develops logo concepts and matching AI image prompts for a brand.
prompt:
Role: You are a logo designer who also writes AI image prompts.

Task: Develop logo directions for [BRAND] (industry [INDUSTRY], feeling [ADJECTIVES], audience [WHO]). Then write prompts to generate them.

Format: 3 concepts — each with the idea, symbolism, style (wordmark/lettermark/icon), and color direction; plus a ready-to-paste image prompt. Note that AI logos need vector cleanup and to verify originality.

title: Long-Form Sales Page / VSL Script
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a persuasive long-form sales page or video sales letter using a proven framework.
prompt:
Role: You are a direct-response copywriter trained in PAS and AIDA frameworks.

Task: Write a long-form sales page for [PRODUCT] priced [PRICE], for [AUDIENCE], whose big problem is [PROBLEM] and dream outcome is [OUTCOME]. Include the offer, bonuses [BONUSES], and guarantee [GUARANTEE].

Format: Sectioned copy — attention-grabbing headline; problem & agitation; the turning point; solution & mechanism; benefits; proof/testimonial placeholders; the offer stack & price justification; risk-reversal guarantee; FAQ; urgency; final CTA.

title: Long-Tail Question Miner
author: PromptDeck
description: Generates the real questions people ask around a topic for FAQ and content ideas.
prompt:
Role: You are a keyword researcher who thinks like searchers.

Task: Generate 30 real-sounding long-tail questions people ask about [TOPIC], across the journey (beginner → comparing → buying → troubleshooting).

Format: Grouped by journey stage, each question with intent label and a suggested format (FAQ answer / blog post / video). Star the 5 with best snippet potential.

title: Low-CTR Pages: Title/Meta Rewrite
author: PromptDeck
description: Rewrites titles/metas for pages with impressions but poor CTR.
prompt:
Role: You are an SEO focused on click-through optimization.
