Task: These pages get impressions but low CTR (from Search Console): [LIST: page + query + position + CTR]. Rewrite each title and meta to better match intent and earn the click at that position.

Format: A table — Page | Query | New Title | New Meta | Angle used. Note any page where the real problem is ranking position or intent mismatch, not copy.

title: Make the Case for Tech Debt Work
author: PromptDeck
description: Argues for refactoring time in language product managers accept.
prompt:
Role: You are an engineering lead who wins tech-debt arguments.

Task: Build the case for addressing [TECH DEBT ITEM] which currently causes [SYMPTOMS: slow features, bugs, on-call pain]. Translate to product language — velocity, risk, and money, not code aesthetics.

Format: The business framing (what this debt costs per month in features-not-shipped/incidents); the proposal (scoped, time-boxed, incremental — not 'rewrite everything'); the risk of deferring another quarter; the metrics that will show it worked; and the compromise version if the full ask is rejected.

title: Market Research Survey Builder
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs an unbiased market-research survey that yields decision-useful answers.
prompt:
Role: You are a market researcher.

Task: Build a survey to learn [RESEARCH GOAL, e.g. willingness to pay, feature demand] from [AUDIENCE]. Avoid leading and double-barreled questions.

Format: A logical question flow (screener → core → demographics); a mix of question types with answer options; note which map to your goal; and 3 tips to boost completion. Keep it under ~10 questions.

title: Marketing Budget Allocation Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Allocates a budget across channels based on goal, stage, and payback.
prompt:
Role: You are a marketing lead who owns budgets.

Task: Allocate a [BUDGET]/month marketing budget for [BUSINESS] at stage [STAGE] with goal [GOAL]. Current channels & results: [WHAT'S WORKED]. Balance proven channels vs experiments (~70/20/10).

Format: An allocation table — Channel | % | Amount | Expected role | Success metric | Review cadence; the experiment slate for the 20/10; and kill criteria for underperformers.

title: Marketplace Review & Q&A Responses
author: PromptDeck
description: Responds to marketplace reviews and product questions to lift conversion.
prompt:
Role: You are a marketplace reputation manager.

Task: Write responses for my [MARKETPLACE] product [PRODUCT]: (a) this negative review: [PASTE REVIEW], (b) this product question: [PASTE QUESTION]. Future buyers read these — every reply is sales copy in disguise.

Format: The review reply (empathetic, factual, resolution-forward, no defensiveness, within platform rules); the Q&A answer (direct answer first, then the useful detail that pre-sells); and 3 patterns to mine from reviews for listing improvements.

title: Marketplace vs Own Store Strategy
author: PromptDeck
description: Decides where to sell: Amazon/Etsy/marketplaces, your own store, or both.
prompt:
Role: You are a multichannel e-commerce strategist.

Task: Recommend a channel strategy for [PRODUCT/BRAND] currently selling [CURRENT CHANNELS]. Consider: fees, ownership of customer data, discovery traffic, and fulfillment burden per channel.

Format: A channel comparison table for my product type; the recommended mix with sequencing (where to start, when to expand); how to use marketplaces for discovery while migrating buyers to owned channels (within marketplace rules); and the channel-conflict traps.

title: Meeting Icebreakers & Team Activities
author: PromptDeck
description: Generates inclusive icebreakers and activities for a class, workshop, or team.
prompt:
Role: You are a facilitator.

Task: Suggest icebreakers/activities for a [GROUP: size, context, virtual/in-person] with goal [BREAK ICE / BUILD TRUST / ENERGIZE], time [MINUTES]. Keep them inclusive and low-pressure (nothing that puts people on the spot uncomfortably).

Format: 6 activities — name, time, how to run it, and why it works. Mark which suit introverts and which work virtually.

title: Meeting Notes → Action Items
author: PromptDeck
description: Turns raw notes or a transcript into a summary, decisions, and owner-assigned actions.
prompt:
Role: You are an executive assistant.

Task: Convert the notes/transcript below into a structured summary. If owners or dates aren't stated, mark them [TBD]. Be concise and neutral.

Format: (1) TL;DR (3 bullets); (2) key decisions; (3) action items table (Task | Owner | Due | Priority); (4) open questions; (5) suggested next-meeting agenda points. Notes: [PASTE NOTES/TRANSCRIPT].

title: Memory Techniques for Hard Material
author: PromptDeck
description: Builds mnemonics and memory structures for material that resists memorization.
prompt:
Role: You are a memory-techniques coach.

Task: Help me memorize [MATERIAL: list, formulas, vocab, sequences, speech]. Match the technique to the material type — method of loci for sequences, vivid-absurd association for pairs, chunking for numbers.

Format: The technique choice with why; the actual memory aids built for my material (the mnemonics/loci/story — concrete, vivid, a bit ridiculous, since that's what sticks); the practice schedule (spaced retrieval); and the self-test to run in 24 hours. Material: [PASTE MATERIAL].

title: Menu & Food Description Copy
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes appetizing menu descriptions that lift order value.
prompt:
Role: You are a menu engineer and food writer.

Task: Write menu descriptions for [RESTAURANT TYPE/CUISINE]: items [LIST DISHES + KEY INGREDIENTS]. Sensory but tight — origin, texture, and preparation words sell; adjective soup doesn't.

Format: Per dish — a 1–2 line description (≤20 words) with one sensory hook and one provenance/preparation detail; flag the 3 high-margin dishes to visually feature; and 2 naming improvements for dishes with flat names.

title: Micro-Influencer Campaign Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Runs a seeding campaign with small creators for authentic reach.
prompt:
Role: You are an influencer-marketing manager who prefers micro over mega.

Task: Plan a micro-influencer campaign for [PRODUCT] targeting [AUDIENCE] with budget [BUDGET]. Micro creators (1–50k) convert on trust — optimize for genuine fit over follower count.

Format: The creator-fit criteria (engagement quality signals over follower math); the outreach sequence (gift-first vs paid-first logic); the brief-lite (guidance without scripting them into inauthenticity); compensation structures at this budget; tracking setup (codes/links); and the red flags of fake-engagement accounts.

title: Micro-Productivity for Dead Time
author: PromptDeck
description: Builds a menu of useful 5–15 minute tasks for gaps between meetings.
prompt:
Role: You are a time-management coach.

Task: Build me a dead-time menu — useful tasks for 5/10/15-minute gaps (between meetings, commuting, waiting). My roles/projects: [MY WORK/PROJECTS]. Match tasks to energy too: some gaps I'm sharp, some I'm fried.

Format: A menu — 5-min / 10-min / 15-min columns, each split by energy (sharp/fried); how to make these tasks grabbable (pre-staged lists, open loops); what NOT to attempt in gaps; and the rule for when a gap should just be rest.

title: Midjourney Prompt Generator
author: PromptDeck
description: Outputs four richly detailed, ready-to-paste Midjourney prompts with parameters.
prompt:
Role: You are a Midjourney prompt expert.

Task: For the concept [CONCEPT], write 4 distinct, ready-to-paste prompts, each specifying subject and action, environment, art style/medium, lighting, color palette, mood, camera/composition, and detail level. Vary the style across the four (photoreal, cinematic, illustrated, 3D).

Format: 4 numbered prompts, each ending with sensible parameters (e.g. --ar 16:9 --style raw --v 6). Output only the prompts.

title: Mine a Transcript for Insights
author: PromptDeck
description: Extracts decisions, commitments, tensions, and quotes from any transcript.
prompt:
Role: You are a conversation analyst.

Task: Mine the transcript below for what matters. Context: [WHAT MEETING/CALL THIS WAS]. Go beyond a summary — find commitments, disagreements (including polite ones), unanswered questions, and quotable moments.

Format: Decisions made; commitments (who owes what by when); tensions/disagreements (including the politely-buried ones); questions raised but never answered; notable quotes verbatim; and the one follow-up that matters most. Transcript: [PASTE TRANSCRIPT].

title: Mission, Vision & Values Statement
author: PromptDeck
description: Crafts a clear mission, vision, and core values that aren't generic filler.
prompt:
Role: You are a brand strategist.

Task: Write a mission, vision, and values for [COMPANY], which does [WHAT] for [WHO] and believes [BELIEFS]. Make them specific and true to this company, not interchangeable buzzwords.

Format: A mission (what we do & why, present tense); a vision (the future we're building); and 4–5 values, each with a one-line 'what this means in practice'.

title: Mobile App Architecture Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Chooses the stack and structure for a mobile app (native/cross-platform).
prompt:
Role: You are a mobile lead.

Task: Plan a mobile app for [PRODUCT/FEATURES] with team skills [TEAM SKILLS], budget/timeline [CONSTRAINTS]. Recommend native vs React Native vs Flutter for this case with honest tradeoffs.

Format: The stack recommendation + reasoning; project structure; state management choice; offline strategy; push/deep-link handling notes; the App Store/Play submission gotchas for this app type; what to prototype first.

title: Mock Interview & Answer Coach
author: PromptDeck
description: Runs a role-specific mock interview and coaches your answers using STAR.
prompt:
Role: You are a hiring manager interviewing me for a [ROLE].

Task: Run a mock interview. Ask one question at a time (behavioral + role-specific). After each answer, give feedback: what was strong, what to improve, and a tighter STAR-structured version.

Format: First list 8 likely questions for this role, then begin with question 1 and proceed one at a time. Job context: [PASTE JD/ROLE].

title: Moodboard & Visual Direction Brief
author: PromptDeck
description: Defines a visual direction with references, textures, and do/don't guidance.
prompt:
Role: You are an art director.

Task: Define a visual direction for [PROJECT/BRAND] that should feel [ADJECTIVES] for [AUDIENCE]. Industry context: [INDUSTRY].

Format: 3 distinct direction concepts — each with a name, mood description, color feel, typography feel, imagery/texture style, motion feel, real-world reference types to collect, and what to avoid. Recommend one and explain the choice.

title: Moving / Relocation Master Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Organizes a house move or relocation with timeline and checklists.
prompt:
Role: You are a relocation coordinator.

Task: Plan my move from [FROM] to [TO] on [DATE], household [SIZE/SPECIAL ITEMS], mode [MOVERS/DIY], plus [VISA/JOB/SCHOOL complexities if any].

Format: The countdown checklist (8 weeks out → moving day → first week after); the address-change/utilities/subscriptions list; packing strategy (what to pack when, the essentials box); mover-booking questions; budget estimate ranges; and the 5 most-forgotten tasks.

title: Multi-Step AI Workflow Design
author: PromptDeck
description: Chains multiple prompts into a repeatable workflow for a complex task.
prompt:
Role: You are an AI workflow architect.

Task: Design a repeatable prompt chain for: [COMPLEX TASK, e.g. 'weekly competitor report' or 'blog from research to publish']. Break it into steps where each prompt's output feeds the next; note where a human must review.

Format: The workflow diagram in text (Step 1 → 2 → …); per step — the full prompt with [VARIABLES], the expected output shape, and the quality check; the human review gates; and how to store outputs so the chain is re-runnable.

title: Multi-Threading Outreach (New Stakeholders)
author: PromptDeck
description: Reaches new stakeholders in an active deal without going over your champion's head.
prompt:
Role: You are an enterprise sales strategist.

Task: Help me multi-thread into [STAKEHOLDER ROLE] at [PROSPECT] where my champion is [CHAMPION ROLE] and the deal is at [STAGE]. Do it transparently — champion informed, not bypassed.

Format: The heads-up message to my champion (asking to loop them in); the outreach to the new stakeholder (referencing champion + their role-specific value angle); and a fallback if the champion resists, addressing why gatekeeping risks the deal.

title: Mutual Action Plan (Close Plan)
author: PromptDeck
description: Creates a shared buyer-seller timeline from today to go-live.
prompt:
Role: You are an enterprise sales manager.

Task: Create a mutual action plan for the deal with [PROSPECT], target signature [DATE], steps known so far [KNOWN STEPS: security review, legal, etc.]. Frame it around their go-live goal, not our quota.

Format: A table — Milestone | Owner (us/them) | Target date | Status; the email that introduces the MAP collaboratively; and 3 signals the plan is slipping with the recovery move for each.

title: Natural Anchor Text Variations
author: PromptDeck
description: Generates a safe, natural anchor-text distribution for link building.
prompt:
Role: You are a link-building strategist who avoids over-optimization.

Task: Generate anchor text variations for links pointing to [URL] targeting [KEYWORD]. Create a natural distribution: branded, naked URL, generic, partial-match, exact-match (sparingly), and long-tail.

Format: A table — Anchor | Type | Suggested share of links. Add a one-line warning on what distribution looks spammy.

title: Negotiation Prep & Scripts
author: PromptDeck
description: Prepares leverage, targets, BATNA, and word-for-word negotiation scripts.
prompt:
Role: You are a negotiation coach.

Task: Prepare me to negotiate [WHAT I'M NEGOTIATING] with goal [TARGET] against [WHO & WHAT THEY WANT]. Keep it collaborative, not combative.

Format: My likely leverage; target / walk-away / opening anchor; my BATNA; 3 cheap concessions to trade; likely objections with responses; word-for-word scripts for the opening ask and for holding firm politely.

title: Networking / Referral Outreach Message
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a genuine networking message to ask for advice, a chat, or a referral.
prompt:
Role: You are a networking coach.

Task: Write a message to [PERSON/ROLE] at [COMPANY] to [ASK ADVICE / REQUEST A CHAT / ASK FOR A REFERRAL], given my connection to them [CONTEXT]. Warm, specific, low-pressure — give a reason it's them.

Format: A short message (≤110 words) with a personalized opener, a clear and easy ask, and gratitude. Then a shorter LinkedIn-DM version and a follow-up if no reply.

title: New Hire 30-60-90 Onboarding Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Creates a structured onboarding plan for a specific role.
prompt:
Role: You are an HR onboarding specialist.

Task: Create a 30-60-90 day plan for a new [ROLE] at [COMPANY], reporting to [MANAGER], whose success looks like [OUTCOMES].

Format: Week 1 checklist (access, intros, context docs); 30 days (learn — with specific milestones); 60 days (contribute — first owned deliverable); 90 days (own — full responsibility + review criteria); check-in cadence; and 3 signs onboarding is off-track.

title: New Job First 90 Days Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Plans your first 90 days to build trust and early wins in a new role.
prompt:
Role: You are an executive onboarding coach.

Task: Plan my first 90 days as [ROLE] at [COMPANY/TEAM CONTEXT]. Balance learning vs proving — early overreach burns trust, but so does invisibility.

Format: 0–30 (listen: the stakeholder map to build, questions to ask everyone, what to resist changing); 31–60 (contribute: picking the right first win); 61–90 (own: the visible deliverable); relationship-building cadence; the 3 classic new-joiner mistakes for this kind of role.

title: New Market Entry Analysis
author: PromptDeck
description: Evaluates whether and how to enter a new market or segment.
prompt:
Role: You are a strategy consultant.

Task: Evaluate [COMPANY] entering [NEW MARKET/SEGMENT/GEOGRAPHY] with [PRODUCT/ADAPTATION]. Current position: [CURRENT STATE]. Label assumptions clearly and pressure-test the upside story.

Format: Market attractiveness (demand signals, competition, barriers); our right-to-win; entry-mode options (direct, partner, acquire) compared; resource requirements; a staged entry plan with kill criteria; go/no-go recommendation.

title: New Pet Care & Training Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Prepares you for a new pet with setup, routine, and training basics.
prompt:
Role: You are an experienced pet care advisor. (General guidance — a vet should confirm health specifics.)

Task: Prepare me for a [ANIMAL/BREED/AGE] arriving [WHEN]. My situation: [HOME/WORK SCHEDULE/KIDS/OTHER PETS]. Cover setup, the first week, and foundational training.

Format: The shopping/setup checklist; first-72-hours plan (settling, routines, sleep); the daily routine matched to my schedule; foundational training steps (positive reinforcement) for the first month; early warning signs that need a vet; and realistic cost expectations.

title: New Product Drop Campaign
author: PromptDeck
description: Builds hype and sales for a new product drop across email and social.
prompt:
Role: You are a DTC launch marketer.

Task: Plan a product drop for [NEW PRODUCT] at [STORE] to list [LIST SIZE] and socials [FOLLOWING]. Angle: [WHAT'S SPECIAL]. Build anticipation before, urgency during.

Format: The teaser phase (2 emails + 3 social posts); launch-day email + posts; the 48-hour follow-up (social proof, low-stock if true); subject lines throughout; an early-access mechanic for VIPs; and honest-urgency rules (no fake scarcity).

title: Nonprofit Donation Appeal
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a donation appeal that moves people to give.
prompt:
Role: You are a nonprofit fundraising copywriter.

Task: Write a donation appeal for [ORGANIZATION/CAUSE] for campaign [CAMPAIGN/GOAL]. Story assets: [BENEFICIARY STORY/IMPACT DATA]. One story beats statistics — but pair the story with the concrete impact of a specific gift amount.

Format: The appeal email — a story-first opening (one person, dignified framing), the problem's stakes, the donation-impact ladder ('X provides Y'), the direct ask, and a PS with urgency; plus 3 subject lines and a social post version.

title: Note-Taking / Second Brain System
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs a simple note system you'll actually maintain and retrieve from.
prompt:
Role: You are a personal knowledge management coach (simplicity school).

Task: Design a note system for me: what I capture [MEETING NOTES/IDEAS/RESEARCH], tool [TOOL or 'recommend'], current failure [WHY PAST SYSTEMS DIED]. Retrieval beats organization — design for finding, not filing.

Format: The capture rule (one inbox, friction-free); the minimal structure (max 4 top-level areas); the processing ritual (when/how, 10 min); naming/tagging conventions that aid search; what NOT to save; and the monthly review that keeps it alive.

title: Objection-Crusher Page Section
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a page section that dissolves the top objections before checkout.
prompt:
Role: You are a conversion copywriter.

Task: Write an objection-handling section for the [PRODUCT] page. Top objections: [OBJECTION 1/2/3, e.g. price, time, 'will it work for me']. Answer each honestly with reframe + proof, no strawmen.

Format: A section headline ('Still on the fence?' alternatives ×3); per objection — the objection voiced in the customer's words, a 2–4 sentence answer, a proof element. Close with a reassurance line + CTA.

title: Offer / Value Ladder Design
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs a value ladder from free lead magnet to premium offer.
prompt:
Role: You are an offer strategist.

Task: Design a value ladder for [BUSINESS] serving [AUDIENCE], from a free entry point up to a premium offer, each step naturally leading to the next.

Format: A ladder (Lead magnet → Tripwire → Core offer → Premium/continuity) — for each: what it is, price, the value delivered, and the transition to the next step. Note the one metric that shows the ladder is working.

title: On-Page SEO Audit of a Page
author: PromptDeck
description: Audits a page's on-page SEO from its content and gives prioritized fixes.
prompt:
Role: You are a technical SEO auditor.

Task: Audit the on-page SEO of the content below, targeting [KEYWORD]. Check title, meta, headings, keyword usage, internal links, readability, and content depth versus intent.

Format: A table — Element | Status (OK/Warning/Fail) | Finding | Fix. Then a prioritized top-5 action list. Content: [PASTE PAGE CONTENT].

title: One-Page Lean Business Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Turns a rough idea into a structured one-page lean plan and pressure-tests assumptions.
prompt:
Role: You are a startup advisor.

Task: Turn my idea [DESCRIBE IDEA] for customer [WHO] into a one-page lean plan, and challenge weak assumptions.

Format: Concise bullet sections — Problem (top 3); Solution & unique value; Target market & size (with reasoning); Revenue model & pricing; Key channels; Main costs; Key risks & unfair advantage; First 3 milestones.

title: Online Course Curriculum & Sales Outline
author: PromptDeck
description: Structures a sellable online course from your expertise.
prompt:
Role: You are an online course strategist.

Task: Structure an online course teaching [SKILL/TRANSFORMATION] to [AUDIENCE] at price point [PRICE]. Design for completion — most courses die at module 2.

Format: The promised transformation (one sentence); module-by-module outline (module → lessons → the win each delivers); the quick-win placed in module 1; worksheets/actions per module; completion mechanics (milestones, community, accountability); the course-name options ×5.

title: Online Store Launch Checklist
author: PromptDeck
description: Complete pre-launch checklist for a new online store.
prompt:
Role: You are an e-commerce launch consultant.

Task: Build a launch checklist for my store on [PLATFORM: Shopify/Woo/custom] selling [PRODUCTS] to [MARKET]. Current state: [WHAT'S DONE].

Format: A phased checklist — store setup (pages, policies, payments, shipping zones, taxes note); product readiness (photos, descriptions, variants, inventory); trust elements; analytics/pixels; test-order protocol; launch-day plan; and the 5 things stores most often forget.

title: Operations Runbook Writer
author: PromptDeck
description: Documents an operational process as a runbook anyone can execute under pressure.
prompt:
Role: You are an operations engineer who writes for 3am brains.

Task: Write a runbook for [PROCESS/INCIDENT TYPE: payment failure, deploy rollback, vendor outage] at [COMPANY/SYSTEM CONTEXT]. The reader is stressed and may not be the expert — zero ambiguity.

Format: When to use this runbook (trigger conditions); pre-checks; the numbered steps with exact commands/clicks and the expected result of each; the decision points as if-then; the escalation criteria with contacts placeholder; the post-incident checklist; and the last-reviewed date field.

title: Outrank a Competitor Article
author: PromptDeck
description: Analyzes a competitor's ranking page and writes a more complete article designed to outrank it.
prompt:
Role: You are an SEO content strategist.

Task: I want to outrank the competitor page [COMPETITOR URL], which targets [KEYWORD]. Infer the topics and questions that page likely covers, identify 5 content gaps they miss, then write a more comprehensive, better-structured article covering everything they do plus the gaps.

Format: Start with an SEO title, meta description, and an outline, then the full draft. Be genuinely more useful, not just longer.

title: Overwhelm Triage (Too Much, Right Now)
author: PromptDeck
description: Sorts an overwhelming pile of commitments into a survivable plan today.
prompt:
Role: You are a calm triage partner for overwhelmed moments.

Task: I'm overwhelmed. Everything on my plate: [BRAIN-DUMP EVERYTHING]. Deadlines/stakes where known: [DEADLINES]. First lower the temperature, then sort.

Format: The immediate sort — actually urgent today (usually fewer than it feels) / this week / later / never (with permission to drop); what to renegotiate or cancel today with the exact message to send; the next 2 hours planned concretely; and the one recurring cause of this overwhelm worth fixing later — not today.

title: Packaging & Unboxing Experience
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs a memorable unboxing that drives shares and repeat purchases.
prompt:
Role: You are a brand-experience designer.

Task: Design the unboxing for [PRODUCT/BRAND] with per-unit packaging budget [BUDGET]. The unboxing is the one moment we fully control — make it feel [BRAND ADJECTIVES].

Format: The packaging structure (outer, inner, protection) within budget; the insert card copy (thank-you that doesn't beg, care/usage tips, the review/share nudge done tastefully); a low-cost delight element; the share-worthy moment engineered in; and sustainability notes customers increasingly expect.

title: Page Wireframe & Layout Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Outlines a page's sections and layout in a logical, conversion-aware order.
prompt:
Role: You are a product designer.

Task: Plan a wireframe for a [PAGE TYPE, e.g. SaaS landing page] whose goal is [GOAL] for [AUDIENCE]. Order sections for flow and conversion.

Format: A top-to-bottom section list — for each: purpose, key content, primary element/CTA, and a note on hierarchy. End with mobile considerations and the single most important above-the-fold element.

title: Parent Communication (Sensitive Topics)
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes tactful messages to parents about academic or behavior concerns.
prompt:
Role: You are a teacher skilled at parent partnerships.

Task: Write a message to the parents of [STUDENT] about [CONCERN: academic slide, behavior, missed work]. What I've observed: [SPECIFICS]. What I've tried: [INTERVENTIONS]. Frame parents as partners, not recipients of bad news.

Format: The message — a genuine positive first, the concern described as observable behavior (not character), what we're doing in class, the specific partnership ask, and an invitation to talk. Plus a phone-call opening script version.

title: Partnership Terms Outline (Pre-Legal)
author: PromptDeck
description: Structures the business terms of a partnership before lawyers get involved.
prompt:
Role: You are a business development advisor. (This is a business outline, not legal advice — have a lawyer draft the actual agreement.)

Task: Outline the terms for a partnership between [MY COMPANY] and [PARTNER] to [PURPOSE]. Cover the deal shape before legal drafting.

Format: Scope & responsibilities of each side; revenue/cost split logic; term & exit conditions; IP and brand usage; exclusivity (or not) with reasoning; success metrics & review cadence; the 5 points most likely to be contested.

title: PAS (Problem–Agitate–Solve) Copy
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes tight Problem–Agitate–Solve copy for ads, emails, or intros.
prompt:
Role: You are a conversion copywriter.

Task: Write PAS copy for [PRODUCT] targeting [AUDIENCE] whose core problem is [PROBLEM]. Agitate with the real costs of inaction (specific, not melodramatic), then present the solution with one proof point.

Format: 3 versions — short (≤50 words, for ads), medium (≤120, for emails), long (≤250, for a page intro). Label each stage in the long version only.

title: Peak Season Support Readiness
author: PromptDeck
description: Prepares a support team for holiday or launch traffic spikes.
prompt:
Role: You are a support operations planner.

Task: Prepare support for [PEAK EVENT: holidays/launch/sale] at [COMPANY], expecting [X]× volume. Team: [SIZE]. Historical peak issues: [KNOWN SPIKES: shipping-where-is-it, returns, stock].

Format: The readiness plan — macro pack to pre-write (list with triggers), self-service deflection to ship beforehand (FAQ/status page), staffing/hours adjustments, the triage rules when queues overflow (what waits, what never waits), the daily peak-metrics check, and the post-peak recovery plan.

title: Performance Bottleneck Analysis
author: PromptDeck
description: Diagnoses slow code or queries and prescribes measured optimizations.
prompt:
Role: You are a performance engineer who measures before optimizing.

Task: This [CODE/QUERY/ENDPOINT] is slow: [PASTE CODE + TIMING/PROFILE DATA if any]. Environment: [STACK/SCALE]. Find the likely bottlenecks and fixes, in order of expected impact.

Format: (1) how to measure/confirm the bottleneck first (exact profiling steps); (2) ranked causes with reasoning; (3) the fix for each with code; (4) expected gain (order of magnitude); (5) what NOT to optimize.

title: Personal Automation Opportunities
author: PromptDeck
description: Finds repetitive tasks in your week worth automating with simple tools.
prompt:
Role: You are an automation consultant for non-programmers.

Task: Find automation opportunities in my week: recurring tasks [LIST], apps I use [APPS], technical comfort [NONE/SOME/DEV]. Only suggest automations whose setup cost pays back within a month.

Format: A table — Task | Automation approach (tool/feature) | Setup effort | Payback; step-by-step setup for the top 2; the maintenance reality check; and 2 tasks that look automatable but aren't worth it.

title: Personal Budget & Savings Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Builds a simple monthly budget and savings plan (general educational guidance).
prompt:
Role: You are a budgeting coach. (General educational guidance, not professional financial advice.)

Task: Build a monthly budget from income (after tax) [AMOUNT], fixed costs [RENT, BILLS], and goals [SAVE FOR X / PAY OFF DEBT].

Format: A suggested budget as a table using a clear framework (e.g. 50/30/20) adapted to my numbers; a realistic monthly savings target; 3 likely areas to cut; and a priority order (emergency fund, high-interest debt, etc.).

title: Personal Custom Instructions Writer
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes custom instructions/preferences that make every AI chat better.
prompt:
Role: You are an AI personalization expert.

Task: Write custom instructions for my AI assistant. About me: [ROLE/WORK/PROJECTS]. How I like answers: [STYLE: concise/detailed, examples, tone]. My recurring uses: [COMMON TASKS]. Pet peeves: [WHAT ANNOYS ME IN AI ANSWERS].

Format: (1) the 'about me' block (≤120 words, only what changes answers); (2) the 'how to respond' block (≤120 words, specific and testable); (3) 3 test questions to verify the instructions work; (4) what I left out and why.

title: Personal Energy Audit
author: PromptDeck
description: Maps what fills and drains your energy, then redesigns your week around it.
prompt:
Role: You are a performance coach who manages energy, not just time.

Task: Run my energy audit. My typical week: [DESCRIBE WEEK/ACTIVITIES]. What leaves me drained vs energized: [OBSERVATIONS]. Time management fails when the hours exist but the energy doesn't.

Format: The drain/gain analysis (activities sorted, with the hidden drains flagged — often people and meetings, not tasks); my apparent energy pattern (when I'm sharp); the week redesign (hard tasks moved to sharp hours, drains batched/shrunk/delegated); one drain to eliminate entirely; and a 2-week experiment to verify.

title: Personal Event Planning (Party/Wedding)
author: PromptDeck
description: Plans a personal event with timeline, budget, and day-of logistics.
prompt:
Role: You are an event planner.

Task: Plan a [EVENT: birthday/wedding/anniversary/reunion] for [GUESTS] guests, budget [BUDGET], date [DATE], venue status [BOOKED/NEEDED]. Vibe: [VIBE].

Format: The countdown timeline (what to book/do by when, from today); budget allocation table with the usual overspend traps; the day-of run sheet; a delegation list (what to hand off); contingency plans for the top 3 things that go wrong; and 3 touches that make it memorable without cost.

title: Personal Prompt Library Organizer
author: PromptDeck
description: Audits your saved prompts and reorganizes them into a reusable, tagged system.
prompt:
Role: You are a knowledge-management specialist for AI workflows.

Task: Review my saved prompts below and reorganize them into a clean library. Merge near-duplicates, standardize each into Role–Task–Format structure, and tag them.

Format: (1) a category/tag taxonomy; (2) each prompt rewritten in RTF form with tags; (3) a list of merged duplicates; (4) 3 gaps where I'm missing a useful prompt. Prompts: [PASTE PROMPTS].

title: Personal Website / Bio Page Content
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes the content for a personal site that wins opportunities.
prompt:
Role: You are a personal-brand copywriter.

Task: Write content for the personal site of [NAME], a [ROLE] who wants [OPPORTUNITIES: job offers, clients, speaking]. Background: [HIGHLIGHTS]. Sound like a person, not a resume.

Format: Homepage — headline, subhead, a 3-line intro, proof strip; About page — first-person story connecting past to what I do now; Work/Projects — the framing template per project; Contact CTA; plus title-tag and meta for the homepage.

title: Personalized Journaling Prompts
author: PromptDeck
description: Generates reflective journaling prompts tailored to what you're working through.
prompt:
Role: You are a reflective journaling guide. (Supportive, not a substitute for professional mental-health care.)

Task: Create journaling prompts to help me explore [THEME, e.g. stress, clarity, gratitude, a decision], at depth [GENTLE/DEEP].

Format: 10 prompts grouped as Reflect / Understand / Move forward, each open-ended and non-judgmental. Add one 2-minute quick prompt for busy days.

title: Personalized Reading List
author: PromptDeck
description: Curates a reading path matched to your goal, taste, and available time.
prompt:
Role: You are a well-read librarian who matches books to people.

Task: Curate a reading list for me. Goal: [WHY I'M READING: grow in X, escape, understand Y]. Taste so far: [BOOKS I LOVED/HATED]. Pace: [BOOKS/MONTH]. Sequence matters — order them so each book builds appetite for the next.

Format: 8 books in reading order — each with why this one for me specifically (not a blurb), the ideal reading mood, and a 'skip if' warning; plus one wildcard outside my taste and the reason to risk it.

title: Personalized Study / Learning Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs a realistic week-by-week roadmap to learn a skill, with resources and milestones.
prompt:
Role: You are a learning coach.

Task: Build a study plan to learn [SKILL/SUBJECT] from [CURRENT LEVEL] to [GOAL LEVEL] in [TIMEFRAME] at [HOURS]/week. Front-load fundamentals.

Format: A week-by-week roadmap (focus topic, outcomes, resource types, a small applied project, a checkpoint). End with the 3 beginner mistakes to avoid in this area.

title: Pillar Page / Ultimate Guide Outline
author: PromptDeck
description: Structures a comprehensive pillar page that anchors a topic cluster.
prompt:
Role: You are a content strategist.

Task: Outline an 'Ultimate Guide to [TOPIC]' pillar page targeting [KEYWORD], designed to link out to cluster posts [CLUSTER TOPICS] and earn backlinks.

Format: Title + meta; a chaptered outline (H2/H3) with a one-line brief per section; where each cluster link goes; 3 linkable-asset ideas (stat roundup, template, checklist); suggested word count per chapter.

title: Pinterest Marketing Strategy
author: PromptDeck
description: Builds a Pinterest strategy with boards, pin ideas, and SEO descriptions.
prompt:
Role: You are a Pinterest marketing specialist.

Task: Build a Pinterest strategy for [BRAND/NICHE] to drive [TRAFFIC/SALES]. Pinterest is search-driven — optimize for keywords [KEYWORDS].

Format: 8 board ideas with keyword-rich names/descriptions; 15 pin content ideas mapped to boards; a pin title + description template with keyword placement; fresh-pin cadence advice; how to convert clicks.

title: Pipeline Review Prep
author: PromptDeck
description: Prepares crisp pipeline review answers your manager will actually respect.
prompt:
Role: You are a sales operations coach.

Task: Prepare me for pipeline review. My deals: [LIST: name, stage, amount, close date, last activity, next step]. Flag stale deals, missing next steps, and unrealistic close dates before my manager does.

Format: Per deal — a 2-line status (where it stands, next step + date); an honesty check (keep date / push / disqualify); then my quarter summary: commit vs best-case, gaps, and my plan to close the gap.

title: Podcast Episode Outline & Questions
author: PromptDeck
description: Plans a podcast episode with a hook, segment flow, and sharp interview questions.
prompt:
Role: You are a podcast producer and interviewer.

Task: Plan an episode about [TOPIC] for [AUDIENCE], format [SOLO/INTERVIEW with GUEST ROLE]. Make it conversational, not a lecture.

Format: (1) 3 title options + a 2-sentence hook; (2) a segment-by-segment outline with rough timings; (3) if interview, 10 non-obvious questions ordered to build a narrative plus 3 follow-ups; (4) 3 quotable teasers for social; (5) a closing CTA.

title: Portfolio Project Case Study
author: PromptDeck
description: Turns a project into a portfolio case study that shows your thinking and impact.
prompt:
Role: You are a portfolio coach.

Task: Write a case study for my project [PROJECT], my role [ROLE], the problem [PROBLEM], what I did [ACTIONS], and the outcome [RESULT]. Show my thinking, not just the output.

Format: Overview & my role; the problem & constraints; my process and key decisions; the solution; the result/impact (with metrics); what I learned. Keep it skimmable with clear headings.

title: Positioning Against a Big Incumbent
author: PromptDeck
description: Positions a small product against a dominant competitor without competing head-on.
prompt:
Role: You are a positioning strategist for challengers.

Task: Position [MY PRODUCT] against the incumbent [BIG COMPETITOR]. My real advantages: [ADVANTAGES]. Find the segment and use-case where their size is a weakness.

Format: The wedge segment to own; the reframe ('they're built for X, we're built for Y'); 3 messaging pillars with proof; what NOT to claim; a homepage hero headline + subhead using the wedge.

title: Post-Demo Follow-Up Sequence
author: PromptDeck
description: Creates a 4-touch follow-up sequence after a sales call or demo.
prompt:
Role: You are a sales development expert.

Task: Write a 4-message follow-up sequence after a demo of [PRODUCT] to [PROSPECT ROLE] who liked [KEY BENEFIT] but hasn't committed. Helpful and confident, never desperate.

Format: For each message — subject line, body under 120 words, one clear ask. Arc: (1) recap value + resource (same day); (2) address hesitation (+3d); (3) social proof/case study (+5d); (4) polite break-up with an easy yes (+10d).

title: Post-Purchase Upsell Offer Copy
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a one-time-offer upsell that feels like a natural next step.
prompt:
Role: You are a funnel copywriter.

Task: Write upsell copy shown right after someone buys [MAIN PRODUCT], offering [UPSELL] at [PRICE/DISCOUNT]. Frame it as completing the result they just bought, not a second sale.

Format: Congratulation line; the 'this makes it work even better' bridge; 3 benefit bullets; the one-time framing (honest); accept + decline button copy (respectful); a PS line.

title: PR Review — Do It Like a Senior
author: PromptDeck
description: Reviews a pull request diff with prioritized, kind, actionable comments.
prompt:
Role: You are a senior engineer reviewing a teammate's PR.

Task: Review the diff below for [CONTEXT: what the PR does]. Separate blocking issues from nitpicks; assume good intent; explain the why behind each comment.

Format: (1) what the PR does well; (2) blocking issues (bug/security/data-loss risks) with file:line and suggested fix; (3) non-blocking suggestions; (4) nitpicks (batched, take-or-leave); (5) verdict: approve / request changes. Diff: [PASTE DIFF].

title: Prepare for a Lawyer Consultation
author: PromptDeck
description: Organizes your situation and questions before paid legal time starts.
prompt:
Role: You are a legal-consultation prep assistant. (Organization help only — the lawyer provides the legal advice.)

Task: Prepare me for a consultation about [MATTER: tenancy dispute, contract, family matter, small claim]. My situation: [FACTS AS I KNOW THEM]. Lawyer time is expensive — arrive organized.

Format: The situation summary written neutrally (facts and dates, feelings removed); the document checklist to bring; my questions ranked (rights, options, costs, timeline, what NOT to do meanwhile); the note-taking template for during; and the questions that assess THIS lawyer's fit.

title: Prepare Your Job References
author: PromptDeck
description: Chooses and briefs references so they advocate effectively.
prompt:
Role: You are a career coach.

Task: Prepare my references for [ROLE] I'm finalist for. Candidates for referees: [PEOPLE + RELATIONSHIP]. A briefed reference tells YOUR story; an unbriefed one improvises.

Format: The referee selection (who covers which competency the role needs); the ask message (permission + easy out); the briefing note per referee (the role, the 2–3 stories to emphasize, the competency the employer will probe); the heads-up timing; and the thank-you after regardless of outcome.

title: Presentation Prep & Speaker Notes
author: PromptDeck
description: Prepares a talk: structure, speaker notes, and Q&A anticipation.
prompt:
Role: You are a public speaking coach.

Task: Prepare me to present [TOPIC] to [AUDIENCE] for [DURATION] with goal [INFORM/PERSUADE/DECIDE]. My material: [PASTE CONTENT/POINTS]. Structure for the audience's attention curve, not my content's completeness.

Format: The talk structure (hook → 3 points max → memorable close) with timings; speaker notes in spoken language (not slide text); transitions between sections; the 5 likely Q&A questions with answers; delivery notes (pace, pauses, where to slow down); and the 60-second version if time gets cut.

title: Press / Media Kit Builder
author: PromptDeck
description: Assembles a press kit that makes journalists' jobs effortless.
prompt:
Role: You are a PR professional.

Task: Build a press kit for [COMPANY/PRODUCT/PERSON]. The facts: [KEY FACTS: founding, numbers, milestones]. Journalists use kits under deadline — findable facts beat beautiful prose.

Format: The boilerplate (25, 50, and 100-word versions); the fact sheet (founded, team, funding, users, milestones as scannable rows); founder bios (2 lengths); the story angles list (3 pitches a journalist could run with); asset checklist (logos, headshots, product shots with specs); and the press contact block.

title: Price Increase Announcement
author: PromptDeck
description: Announces a price increase to customers without triggering churn.
prompt:
Role: You are a customer communications specialist.

Task: Announce a price increase for [PRODUCT] from [OLD] to [NEW] effective [DATE], for [EXISTING CUSTOMERS]. Reasons: [VALUE ADDED/COSTS]. Be direct — no burying the number; give notice and options [GRANDFATHERING/LOCK-IN OFFER if any].

Format: The email — clear subject, the change and date up front, the honest why, value delivered since they joined, their options, and a human sign-off. Plus an FAQ (3 Qs) and a response template for upset replies.

title: Price Presentation & Psychology
author: PromptDeck
description: Optimizes how prices are framed and displayed on your store.
prompt:
Role: You are a pricing-psychology consultant (honest-tactics only).

Task: Optimize price presentation for [STORE/PRODUCT] at [PRICE POINTS]. Apply legitimate framing — anchoring with real compare-at prices, charm pricing fit for my positioning, installment display — no fake discounts.

Format: The recommendations per product/tier (display format, anchor, installment/currency notes); when NOT to use charm pricing (premium positioning); the compare-at-price integrity rule; sale-price display standards; and one framing test to run.

title: Pricing Page Copy & FAQ
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes pricing page copy that frames value and pre-empts objections.
prompt:
Role: You are a SaaS conversion copywriter.

Task: Write pricing page copy for [PRODUCT] with tiers [TIERS + PRICES], where most users should pick [TARGET TIER]. Frame value before price; name tiers by who they're for.

Format: Page headline + subhead; per-tier — name, one-line 'for who', 5 feature bullets ordered by desirability, CTA text; a highlighted 'most popular' justification; billing toggle microcopy; a 5-question objection-focused FAQ.

title: Pricing Strategy & Tier Design
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs value-based pricing tiers with differentiation, anchoring, and upsell logic.
prompt:
Role: You are a pricing strategist.

Task: Design pricing for [PRODUCT/SERVICE] aimed at [TARGET CUSTOMER] with the goal to [MAXIMIZE REVENUE/ADOPTION].

Format: (1) 3 tiers — name, price, who it's for, features, one reason to upgrade; (2) the value metric pricing should scale on and why; (3) an anchor/decoy recommendation; (4) 2 add-ons; (5) a one-line positioning per tier; (6) 3 bullets on the pricing psychology.

title: Prioritize My Task List
author: PromptDeck
description: Turns a messy to-do list into a prioritized plan using impact and urgency.
prompt:
Role: You are a productivity coach.

Task: Prioritize my tasks below toward goal [GOAL] with time available [TIME]. Use an impact/effort and urgency lens; flag anything to delegate or drop.

Format: An Eisenhower-style grouping (Do now / Schedule / Delegate / Drop); a ranked 'today' list with time estimates; and the single most important task to do first. Tasks: [PASTE LIST].

title: Proactive Customer Education Email
author: PromptDeck
description: Preempts common support tickets with a well-timed educational email.
prompt:
Role: You are a proactive support strategist.

Task: Write an educational email preempting our most common ticket: [COMMON ISSUE/CONFUSION] for [PRODUCT] users at moment [TRIGGER: day 3, after first X, before renewal]. Teach the fix before they hit the wall.

Format: Subject (helpful, not salesy) + preview; the email — name the moment they're likely in, the 3-step guidance with one screenshot placeholder, the link to self-serve depth, and an easy reply-for-help line. ≤150 words.

title: Product / Feature Naming
author: PromptDeck
description: Generates name candidates with rationale, checks, and shortlist.
prompt:
Role: You are a naming consultant.

Task: Name a [PRODUCT/FEATURE/COMPANY] that does [WHAT] for [AUDIENCE], brand feel [ADJECTIVES]. Generate across styles: descriptive, suggestive, invented, metaphor, compound.

Format: 20 candidates in a table — Name | Style | Rationale | Risk (pronunciation/meaning/trademark-ish). Shortlist 5 with a domain-style handle idea each, and remind me to run real trademark/domain checks.

title: Product Bundle & AOV Strategy
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs bundles and add-ons that raise average order value naturally.
prompt:
Role: You are a merchandising strategist.

Task: Design AOV-lifting offers for [STORE] with products [PRODUCT RANGE], current AOV [AOV]. Bundles must make usage sense — 'frequently bought together' logic, not random pairings.

Format: 3 bundle concepts (name, contents, the usage story, price vs separate, margin note); the cart add-on strategy (threshold nudges, impulse add-ons under [PRICE POINT]); placement recommendations; and how to test without cannibalizing full-price sales.

title: Product Demo Script & Flow
author: PromptDeck
description: Structures a demo around the prospect's pains instead of a feature tour.
prompt:
Role: You are a demo coach.

Task: Build a demo flow for [PRODUCT] to [BUYER ROLE] whose stated pains are [PAINS]. Show the 'after' state early; tie every feature shown to a stated pain; skip everything else.

Format: Opening recap of their pains (talk track); the 'money shot' moment to show in the first 5 minutes; 3–4 pain→feature→benefit demo beats with transition lines; check-in questions between beats; closing summary + next-step ask.

title: Product Hunt Launch Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Prepares a complete Product Hunt launch: assets, copy, and day-of playbook.
prompt:
Role: You are a launch strategist who has run Product Hunt launches.

Task: Prepare a Product Hunt launch for [PRODUCT] — a [ONE-LINE DESCRIPTION] for [AUDIENCE].

Format: Tagline options (3, ≤60 chars); the maker's first comment (story + ask); gallery asset checklist; a pre-launch supporter outreach message; day-of hour-by-hour playbook; 3 mistakes to avoid; post-launch follow-up plan.

title: Product Launch / Go-To-Market Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Builds a phased launch plan with channels, messaging, and a timeline.
prompt:
Role: You are a go-to-market strategist.

Task: Create a launch plan for [PRODUCT] aimed at [AUDIENCE] with goal [GOAL] and budget level [BUDGET]. Cover pre-launch, launch, and post-launch.

Format: (1) core positioning & message; (2) target channels with why; (3) a phased timeline table (Phase | Actions | Owner | KPI); (4) a launch-day checklist; (5) 3 risks and mitigations. Keep it actionable.

title: Product Onboarding Flow Design
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs a first-run onboarding flow that gets users to their aha moment fast.
prompt:
Role: You are a product designer focused on activation.

Task: Design an onboarding flow for [PRODUCT] whose 'aha moment' is [AHA], for new user [PERSONA]. Minimize steps to first value.

Format: The target activation event; a step-by-step flow (screen → goal → key action → microcopy); what to defer until later; an empty-state plan; and one metric to measure activation.

title: Product Page Conversion Audit
author: PromptDeck
description: Audits a product page against conversion best practices with prioritized fixes.
prompt:
Role: You are a CRO specialist for e-commerce.

Task: Audit my product page: [PASTE PAGE CONTENT/DESCRIBE LAYOUT]. Price point: [PRICE]. Traffic source mix: [SOURCES]. Check: above-fold clarity, image quality signals, price framing, trust elements, shipping visibility, mobile experience, and CTA.

Format: A findings table — Element | Issue | Impact guess (H/M/L) | Fix; the 3 highest-impact changes with implementation detail; what's already good (don't touch); and one A/B test worth running first.

title: Product Photo Shot List & Brief
author: PromptDeck
description: Creates a complete shot list and style brief for product photography.
prompt:
Role: You are an e-commerce creative director.

Task: Create a photography brief for [PRODUCT] sold on [CHANNELS: site/Amazon/Instagram]. Brand feel: [ADJECTIVES]. Budget context: [PRO SHOOT/DIY].

Format: The shot list — hero on white, angles, detail/texture, scale reference, in-use lifestyle, group/variant shots — each with purpose and channel; styling and lighting notes; a DIY setup alternative if budget is tight; and the 3 shots that most affect conversion.

title: Product Requirements Doc (PRD)
author: PromptDeck
description: Drafts a concise PRD that aligns a team on what to build and why.
prompt:
Role: You are a senior product manager.

Task: Write a PRD for [FEATURE/PRODUCT] solving [PROBLEM] for [USER], with goal/metric [SUCCESS METRIC]. Keep it crisp and decision-oriented.

Format: Problem & context; goals & non-goals; target user & use cases; requirements (must/should/could); success metrics; open questions; and a rough milestone plan. Avoid over-specifying the solution.

title: Product Review Generation System
author: PromptDeck
description: Builds an automated post-purchase flow that generates authentic reviews.
prompt:
Role: You are a retention marketer who builds review engines.

Task: Design a review-generation flow for [STORE/PRODUCT TYPE] with delivery time [SHIPPING DAYS]. Time the ask for peak satisfaction; make leaving a review effortless. Never incentivize positive reviews specifically (platform rules + ethics).

Format: The flow timeline (delivery + X days); the review request email (+1 reminder) with copy; photo-review encouragement; how to route unhappy customers to support before they review publicly (ethically — support first, not review-gating); and handling negative reviews.

title: Productize Your Freelance Services
author: PromptDeck
description: Turns hourly freelancing into clear, valuable fixed packages.
prompt:
Role: You are a freelance business strategist.

Task: Productize my services: I do [SERVICE] for [CLIENT TYPE], currently charging [CURRENT PRICING]. Package by outcome, not hours — clients buy results.

Format: 3 packages — name, the outcome promised, what's included (scoped tightly), what's explicitly excluded, price with reasoning, delivery timeline; the anchor logic across the three; the custom-work escape hatch; and how to migrate existing hourly clients.

title: Professional Email Reply Drafter
author: PromptDeck
description: Drafts a clear, appropriately-toned reply to any email with two length options.
prompt:
Role: You are a communications assistant.

Task: Draft a reply to the email below with intent [AGREE/DECLINE/NEGOTIATE/ASK], key points [BULLETS], tone [FRIENDLY/FORMAL/FIRM]. Lead with the main point and end with a clear next step.

Format: A short version and a slightly warmer longer version. Email: [PASTE EMAIL].

title: Professional Press Release
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a newsworthy, properly formatted press release with quotes and boilerplate.
prompt:
Role: You are a PR writer for a communications agency.

Task: Write a press release announcing [WHAT'S HAPPENING] for [COMPANY] in [CITY] on [DATE], with key facts [FACTS] and news angle [ANGLE]. Keep it factual and news-style, not salesy.

Format: Standard format — compelling headline + subhead; dateline; a lead answering who/what/when/where/why; supporting details; 1–2 labeled quotes; an 'About' boilerplate; a media-contact placeholder; end with ###.

title: Professional Resignation Letter
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a gracious, bridge-preserving resignation letter.
prompt:
Role: You are a professional communication advisor.

Task: Write a resignation letter for my role [ROLE] at [COMPANY], last day [DATE], reason [OPTIONAL/BRIEF]. Keep it gracious and burn no bridges.

Format: A concise letter — clear statement of resignation and last day, brief thanks and a positive note, an offer to help with transition, and a warm close. Keep it professional regardless of my true feelings.

title: Professional Self-Introduction
author: PromptDeck
description: Crafts a confident self-introduction for interviews, networking, or events.
prompt:
Role: You are a communication coach.

Task: Write a self-introduction for me for context [INTERVIEW / NETWORKING EVENT / NEW TEAM], given my background [BACKGROUND] and goal [GOAL]. Natural and confident, not a résumé readout.

Format: A 30-second spoken version and a 10-second version. Include who I am, what I do/value, a proof point, and a hook that invites conversation.

title: Programmatic SEO Page Template
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs a scalable template for programmatic SEO pages from a data pattern.
prompt:
Role: You are a programmatic SEO strategist.

Task: Design a page template to scale content for the pattern [PATTERN, e.g. 'best [X] in [CITY]'] using the dataset fields [FIELDS]. Ensure each page is genuinely useful and not thin/duplicate.

Format: Provide the URL pattern, title & meta templates (with {variables}), an H1 template, a recommended page section outline, dynamic vs static content notes, and 3 rules to avoid thin-content penalties.

title: Project Wrap-Up & Client Offboarding
author: PromptDeck
description: Ends projects cleanly: handoff, final invoice, testimonial, and referral.
prompt:
Role: You are a client-experience designer for service businesses.

Task: Build my project offboarding for [SERVICE]. The ending is what clients remember — engineer it for testimonials, referrals, and return work.

Format: The wrap-up sequence — final delivery email (what's included, how to use it, support window); the handoff document template; final invoice timing; the results-recap touchpoint (2–4 weeks later, showing their outcome); the testimonial + referral ask placement; and the stay-in-touch cadence that isn't spammy.

title: Proofread & Line-Edit
author: PromptDeck
description: Fixes grammar and clarity, tightens prose, and shows what changed and why.
prompt:
Role: You are a meticulous copy editor.

Task: Proofread and line-edit the text below — fix grammar, spelling, and punctuation; tighten wordy sentences; improve clarity and flow while keeping my voice and meaning. Target reading level [LEVEL], variant [US/UK] English.

Format: (1) the clean edited version; (2) a short bullet list of the most important changes and why. Text: [PASTE TEXT].

title: Quarter Retrospective & Lessons
author: PromptDeck
description: Runs a structured quarter retro that produces real changes, not vibes.
prompt:
Role: You are a retrospective facilitator.

Task: Run a retro on [QUARTER/PROJECT]. Results vs goals: [RESULTS]. What happened: [EVENTS/CONTEXT]. Separate outcome luck from process quality — good process can lose, bad process can win.

Format: What we set out to do vs what happened; what worked (keep); what didn't (change); process-vs-luck analysis of the biggest hit and miss; 3 specific process changes with owners; one thing to stop doing entirely.

title: Quarterly Business Review (QBR) Deck Outline
author: PromptDeck
description: Structures a QBR that proves value and expands the account.
prompt:
Role: You are a customer success / account executive.

Task: Outline a QBR deck for [CUSTOMER] using [PRODUCT], covering results to date [METRICS], goals [GOALS], and an expansion opportunity [OPPORTUNITY].

Format: A slide-by-slide outline — Agenda; Wins & ROI to date; Progress vs goals; Product usage & adoption; Roadmap relevant to them; Recommendations & expansion; Action items & owners. Add a talk-track line for each slide.

title: Quarterly OKRs Generator
author: PromptDeck
description: Turns a goal into well-formed Objectives and measurable Key Results.
prompt:
Role: You are an operations lead who runs OKRs.

Task: Create quarterly OKRs for [TEAM/COMPANY] whose main goal is [GOAL]. Make KRs numeric and outcome-based, not tasks; ambitious but realistic.

Format: 2–3 Objectives (qualitative, time-bound), each with 3 measurable Key Results. Then 3 initiatives that would drive them and one anti-pattern to avoid.

title: Questions YOU Ask the Interviewer
author: PromptDeck
description: Prepares sharp questions that impress interviewers and surface red flags.
prompt:
Role: You are an interview coach.

Task: Prepare questions for me to ask when interviewing for [ROLE] at [COMPANY/TYPE], stage [SCREEN/HIRING MANAGER/FINAL]. Questions should show strategic thinking AND extract decision-useful info.

Format: Per stage — 5 questions with what a good vs concerning answer sounds like; 3 red-flag-detection questions phrased diplomatically; the 2 questions never to ask at this stage; and the closing question that leaves a strong impression.

title: Quota Gap Recovery Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Builds a concrete plan to close a mid-quarter quota gap.
prompt:
Role: You are a pragmatic sales coach.

Task: I'm at [X]% of a [QUOTA] quota with [WEEKS] weeks left. Pipeline: [PIPELINE SUMMARY]. Build a recovery plan across: accelerate existing deals, resurrect recent losses/stalls, and net-new only where it can close this quarter.

Format: The math (what must close); per bucket — specific plays with this-week actions; a daily activity plan; what to stop doing; and the honest call on whether the gap is closable + what to tell my manager.

title: Raise Your Rates With Existing Clients
author: PromptDeck
description: Announces a rate increase to current clients while keeping the relationship.
prompt:
Role: You are a freelance pricing coach.

Task: Help me raise rates from [CURRENT] to [NEW] with existing clients. Relationship: [YEARS/VALUE DELIVERED]. Notice period: [NOTICE]. No apologizing, no over-explaining — confidence sets the frame.

Format: The email — the value recap (brief, factual), the new rate with effective date, the honored-rate transition window, and a warm continuation line; responses to 'that's too much for us' (hold, adjust scope, or graceful exit); and which clients to inform first.

title: Rate Limiting & Abuse Protection
author: PromptDeck
description: Adds rate limiting and abuse protection to an API or app.
prompt:
Role: You are a backend engineer protecting a public API.

Task: Design rate limiting for [API/APP] on [STACK] with endpoints [ENDPOINTS] and abuse concerns [CONCERNS: scraping, brute force, cost]. Choose algorithms (fixed window/sliding/token bucket) per endpoint class.

Format: Limits table — Endpoint class | Algorithm | Limit | Key (IP/user/API-key); implementation approach with code sketch; the 429 response shape + headers; bypass for trusted clients; monitoring; what to do when limits are hit legitimately.

title: Real Estate Listing Description
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a property listing that creates desire while staying accurate.
prompt:
Role: You are a real estate copywriter.

Task: Write a listing for [PROPERTY: type, beds/baths, size, standout features, area perks], price [PRICE], target buyer [BUYER TYPE]. Sell the life lived there — accurately, no fair-housing-violating language, no puffery that backfires at viewing.

Format: A headline; the opening that sells the lifestyle moment; the feature walk-through in logical order; the neighborhood paragraph; the CTA to view; plus a 3-line portal/short version and 5 photo-caption lines.

title: Realistic Morning Routine Design
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs a morning routine that fits your actual life, not a fantasy.
prompt:
Role: You are a pragmatic habits coach (anti-5am-guru school).

Task: Design a morning routine for me: wake time [TIME], available minutes [MINUTES], constraints [KIDS/COMMUTE/etc.], goal [ENERGY/FOCUS/CALM]. Build around my actual constraint, not an influencer's fantasy.

Format: The routine minute-by-minute (with a 'minimum viable version' for bad days); the night-before setup that makes it automatic; the one keystone element if I only do one thing; and how to restart after falling off without self-punishment.

title: Recipe From What's in My Fridge
author: PromptDeck
description: Creates a doable recipe from the ingredients you already have.
prompt:
Role: You are a resourceful home cook.

Task: Create a recipe using mainly these ingredients [INGREDIENTS], for [SERVINGS], with skill level [BEGINNER/INTERMEDIATE], time [MINUTES], and restrictions [ANY]. Assume basic pantry staples.

Format: Dish name; ingredient list (flag any small additions needed); step-by-step instructions; time; and 2 easy variations or substitutions.

title: Refactor & Clean Up Code
author: PromptDeck
description: Refactors code for readability, performance, and best practices without changing behavior.
prompt:
Role: You are a staff engineer who values clean, maintainable code.

Task: Refactor the code below in [LANGUAGE/FRAMEWORK] to improve readability, structure, and performance while preserving behavior. Follow idiomatic conventions.

Format: (1) the refactored code; (2) a bullet list of what changed and why; (3) any behavior you kept identical to confirm; (4) optional further improvements. Code: [PASTE CODE].

title: Referral Program Design
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs a referral program with the right incentive structure and copy.
prompt:
Role: You are a growth marketer who has launched referral programs.

Task: Design a referral program for [PRODUCT] (price [PRICE], audience [AUDIENCE]). Choose an incentive structure (two-sided/one-sided, credit/cash/feature) with reasoning and anti-abuse rules.

Format: (1) the incentive structure + why; (2) the referral ask copy (in-app + email); (3) the invite message users send; (4) 3 placement moments; (5) abuse guardrails; (6) the one metric that proves it works.

title: Refresh & Update an Old Post
author: PromptDeck
description: Updates a stale post to recover rankings with new sections and freshness signals.
prompt:
Role: You are an SEO content editor specializing in content decay recovery.

Task: Refresh the old post below targeting [KEYWORD]. Identify outdated facts, thin sections, and missing subtopics competitors now cover; then rewrite/expand where needed.

Format: (1) a change log table (Section | Problem | Fix); (2) the rewritten/new sections ready to paste; (3) an updated title + meta; (4) 3 freshness signals to add. Post: [PASTE POST].

title: Refund & Cancellation Policy Writer
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a clear, fair refund policy that prevents disputes.
prompt:
Role: You are a customer-experience policy writer. (Have a lawyer review before publishing.)

Task: Write a refund/cancellation policy for [PRODUCT/SERVICE] with terms [REFUND WINDOW/CONDITIONS/EXCEPTIONS]. Plain language — a policy customers can't understand creates the disputes it should prevent.

Format: The policy in plain language (what's refundable, the window, how to request, processing time, exceptions); a short version for the checkout page; the FAQ pair ('can I get a refund if…'); and the support macro for granting and for declining per this policy.

title: Regex Builder & Explainer
author: PromptDeck
description: Builds a tested regular expression from a description and explains each part.
prompt:
Role: You are a regex expert.

Task: Create a regex for [DESCRIBE THE PATTERN, with should-match and should-not-match examples] in flavor [JS/PYTHON/PCRE]. Keep it as simple as possible while correct.

Format: (1) the regex; (2) a token-by-token breakdown; (3) 3 matching and 3 non-matching test strings; (4) a note on edge cases or backtracking risk.

title: Renewal Conversation Prep
author: PromptDeck
description: Prepares a renewal call that secures the contract and finds expansion.
prompt:
Role: You are a customer success manager who owns renewals.

Task: Prep my renewal conversation with [CUSTOMER], contract [VALUE] ending [DATE]. Usage/health: [USAGE DATA/SENTIMENT]. Anchor on value delivered before discussing terms.

Format: A value recap with their numbers; renewal-risk assessment with mitigations; the conversation flow (value → their goals → renewal ask → expansion probe); responses to 'we're evaluating alternatives' and 'we need a discount'; the ideal and fallback outcomes.

title: Repurpose One Piece Into Ten
author: PromptDeck
description: Turns a single blog, video, or transcript into a full multi-channel content package.
prompt:
Role: You are a content repurposing specialist.

Task: Repurpose the source content below into a multi-channel package for [BRAND/AUDIENCE], adapting each asset to its platform (not copy-paste), in voice [VOICE].

Format: Deliver — 1 LinkedIn post; 1 X thread (6–8 tweets); 3 short-form video hooks + a 20s script for one; 1 Instagram carousel outline; 1 newsletter blurb; 5 pull-quote graphics; 3 SEO title ideas for a spin-off article. Source: [PASTE CONTENT].

title: Respond to Online Reviews
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes professional, on-brand replies to positive and negative public reviews.
prompt:
Role: You are a reputation manager for [BUSINESS], voice [VOICE].

Task: Write a public reply to the [X]/5 review below, mindful that future customers are reading. If positive: thank specifically, reinforce what they loved, invite them back. If negative: stay calm, acknowledge, no excuses or public arguing, move resolution offline.

Format: A short, sincere reply. Review: [PASTE REVIEW].

title: Responsive Behavior Spec
author: PromptDeck
description: Specifies how a design adapts across mobile, tablet, and desktop.
prompt:
Role: You are a UI engineer-designer hybrid.

Task: Spec responsive behavior for [PAGE/COMPONENT] with desktop design [DESCRIBE LAYOUT]. Mobile-first: decide what collapses, stacks, hides, or transforms.

Format: A breakpoint table — Element | Mobile | Tablet | Desktop behavior; the touch-target and spacing adjustments; navigation transformation; what content is truly hidden vs reflowed (and why hiding is a last resort); test cases for the awkward middle widths.

title: REST/JSON API Endpoint Design
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs clean API endpoints with methods, schemas, status codes, and errors.
prompt:
Role: You are an API architect.

Task: Design API endpoints for the feature [FEATURE] with resources [RESOURCES]. Follow REST conventions, sensible status codes, and consistent error shapes.

Format: For each endpoint — method + path; purpose; request params/body schema; success response schema; error responses; auth notes. Then 3 design decisions and any pagination/versioning recommendation.

title: Returns Reduction Audit
author: PromptDeck
description: Diagnoses why customers return your product and fixes the causes.
prompt:
Role: You are a returns-reduction consultant.

Task: Reduce returns for [PRODUCT] with return rate [RATE] and stated reasons [RETURN REASONS DATA]. Fix causes, not just policy — most returns are expectation gaps.

Format: The reason-by-reason diagnosis (expectation gap vs product flaw vs buyer's remorse); the fix per cause (page copy, photos, sizing, packaging insert); the post-purchase expectation-setting email; the exchange-first flow; and which returns to accept gracefully vs which signal a product problem.

title: Reverse-Engineer a Prompt from a Description
author: PromptDeck
description: Turns a described image/style into a reusable generation prompt.
prompt:
Role: You are an expert at deconstructing visual style into prompts.

Task: From the image or style I describe [DESCRIBE THE LOOK], write a reusable generation prompt that would recreate it, isolating the style elements from the subject so I can swap subjects.

Format: (1) a full prompt; (2) a 'style-only' snippet I can append to any subject; (3) key parameters; (4) 2 variations for different subjects.

title: Rewrite for a Specific Audience
author: PromptDeck
description: Rewrites complex text for a specific audience and reading level without losing accuracy.
prompt:
Role: You are a communication specialist who adapts content for any audience.

Task: Rewrite the text below for [AUDIENCE, e.g. executives, 5th graders, developers] at reading level [LEVEL], length [same/shorter]. Keep it accurate but adjust vocabulary, examples, and detail; add an analogy if it aids understanding.

Format: The rewritten text, then a one-line note flagging anything simplified that loses nuance. Text: [PASTE TEXT].

title: RFP Response Section Writer
author: PromptDeck
description: Drafts compelling, compliant answers to RFP questions.
prompt:
Role: You are a proposal manager.

Task: Draft responses to these RFP questions from [ISSUER] for [PRODUCT/SERVICE]: [PASTE QUESTIONS]. Our relevant strengths: [STRENGTHS/PROOF]. Answer the question asked first, then differentiate; comply with any word limits [LIMITS].

Format: Per question — a direct first-sentence answer, supporting specifics, a differentiator woven in, and a proof point. Flag any question we can't honestly answer well with a mitigation approach.

title: Risk-Reversal Guarantee Copy
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a guarantee that removes purchase fear and feels credible.
prompt:
Role: You are a conversion copywriter.

Task: Write guarantee copy for [PRODUCT] at [PRICE] with terms [REFUND WINDOW/CONDITIONS]. Make the buyer feel the risk is on us, name the guarantee memorably, and keep the terms honest.

Format: 3 versions — a named guarantee headline + paragraph; a short badge/box version; a one-line version near the buy button. Plus one FAQ entry answering 'what's the catch?'.

title: ROI / Business Case Builder
author: PromptDeck
description: Builds a credible ROI narrative the champion can defend internally.
prompt:
Role: You are a value engineer.

Task: Build a business case for [PROSPECT] buying [PRODUCT] at [PRICE]. Their relevant numbers: [THEIR METRICS: team size, hours spent, current costs]. Use conservative assumptions and show the math.

Format: The cost of the status quo (calculated); expected gains (time/money, conservative); payback period; a one-paragraph executive summary the champion can paste; and the 2 assumptions a CFO will challenge with defenses.

title: Roleplay Difficult Scenarios (Practice Partner)
author: PromptDeck
description: Sets up the AI as a realistic practice partner for hard conversations.
prompt:
Role: You are a realistic roleplay partner and coach.

Task: Roleplay [SCENARIO: salary negotiation, sales objection, difficult customer, interview] with me. Play [THE OTHER PARTY] realistically — push back like they actually would ([DIFFICULTY: reasonable/tough]), don't fold easily.

Format: Stay in character for the conversation; I speak as myself. After every 6–8 exchanges or when I say 'feedback', break character: what I did well, my 2 weakest moments with better alternatives, and one technique for the next round. Then resume. Start the scenario now.

title: Salary Negotiation Coach & Scripts
author: PromptDeck
description: Prepares your case and gives scripts to negotiate a job offer confidently.
prompt:
Role: You are a compensation negotiation coach.

Task: Help me negotiate an offer for [ROLE] at [COMPANY]. Current offer: [OFFER]; my target: [TARGET]; my leverage: [OTHER OFFERS/SKILLS]. Keep it collaborative and professional.

Format: My value-based justification; a target/anchor/walk-away; word-for-word scripts to counter on base, and to negotiate non-salary items (equity, PTO, remote, start date); and how to respond if they say no.

title: Sales Compensation Plan Design
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs a sales comp plan that drives the right behavior without gaming.
prompt:
Role: You are a sales-compensation consultant.

Task: Design comp for [ROLE: AE/SDR/CSM] at [COMPANY], selling [PRODUCT at ACV], with company priority [PRIORITY: new logos, expansion, retention]. Reps optimize whatever you pay for — including the loopholes.

Format: The structure (base/variable split with market logic); the commission mechanics (rate, accelerators, thresholds) tied to the priority; the gaming scenarios this plan invites and the guardrails; SPIF ideas used sparingly; the payment timing/clawback fairness rules; and a worked example of a rep's monthly math.

title: Sales Objection Handling Scripts
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes empathetic responses to your most common sales objections.
prompt:
Role: You are a top sales closer.

Task: Write response scripts for my offer [PRODUCT/SERVICE] at [PRICE] handling these objections: 'too expensive', 'need to think about it', 'we already use [competitor]', 'no budget now', 'send me info'. Acknowledge, reframe, and move to a next step without pushiness.

Format: For each objection — a response under 90 words plus one clarifying question to continue the dialogue.

title: Sales Voicemail Scripts
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes short voicemails that actually earn callbacks or prime the follow-up email.
prompt:
Role: You are an SDR coach.

Task: Write voicemail scripts for prospect [ROLE] about [PRODUCT/VALUE]. Each ≤20 seconds spoken, one idea, name + number said slowly at the end. The goal is curiosity for my follow-up email, not a full pitch.

Format: 4 voicemails — first touch, value-drop, referral/social-proof, and break-up — each with the exact words and a matching email subject line to send right after.

title: Say No Kindly (Policy / Refund Reply)
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a firm-but-kind reply that declines a request while preserving goodwill.
prompt:
Role: You are a support specialist skilled at difficult conversations.

Task: Decline the request [WHAT THEY WANT] because [POLICY/REASON], offering alternative [ALTERNATIVE or 'none']. Be clear and kind, no wall of text, under 130 words.

Format: A reply that empathizes, explains the 'why' honestly and briefly, offers the alternative or a goodwill gesture, and ends warmly.

title: Saying No Gracefully (Templates)
author: PromptDeck
description: Declines requests, invitations, and asks without burning goodwill.
prompt:
Role: You are a boundaries coach.

Task: Help me say no to: [THE REQUEST] from [WHO/RELATIONSHIP]. Why I want to decline: [REAL REASON]. What I fear: [FEAR: disappointing them, missing out, retaliation]. A clean no beats a resentful yes or a slow fade.

Format: 3 versions — the warm brief no (no over-explaining), the no-with-alternative (only if I genuinely want to offer one), and the firm no for repeat askers; what NOT to include (fake excuses that invite negotiation); and the follow-through script if they push back.

title: Scheduled Jobs & Cron Reliability
author: PromptDeck
description: Makes scheduled jobs observable, idempotent, and failure-tolerant.
prompt:
Role: You are a backend reliability engineer.

Task: Harden these scheduled jobs: [JOBS + WHAT THEY DO] on [STACK/SCHEDULER]. Address: silent failures, overlapping runs, partial completion, and missed schedules.

Format: Per job — idempotency approach, locking/overlap guard, timeout, and failure alert; a shared job-wrapper code pattern; heartbeat/dead-man's-switch monitoring; the runbook entry template; and which job should become event-driven instead.

title: Schema Markup (JSON-LD) Generator
author: PromptDeck
description: Generates valid JSON-LD schema for any page type.
prompt:
Role: You are a technical SEO specialist.

Task: Generate JSON-LD schema markup for a [PAGE TYPE: Article/Product/LocalBusiness/Recipe/Event/HowTo] page with these details: [PAGE DETAILS]. Use only properties supported by Google's rich results.

Format: (1) the complete, valid JSON-LD in a code block; (2) a note on which rich result it targets; (3) any recommended optional properties I should add data for.

title: Scholarship / Admission Essay Coach
author: PromptDeck
description: Coaches an authentic, memorable application essay from your real experiences.
prompt:
Role: You are an admissions essay coach who forbids generic essays. (Coaching your authentic story — not writing fiction for you.)

Task: Help me write my essay for [SCHOLARSHIP/PROGRAM], prompt: [ESSAY PROMPT], length [WORDS]. My raw material: [EXPERIENCES/BACKGROUND/MOTIVATION]. Committees read thousands — specificity and reflection win, not thesaurus words.

Format: The angle recommendation (which of my experiences answers the prompt best and why); the structure (scene-first opening, the turn, the reflection); a draft built ONLY from my provided material with [EXPAND: …] markers where I must add real detail; and the clichés-to-cut list.

title: Scope Creep: Say No Gracefully
author: PromptDeck
description: Handles 'just one more thing' requests without losing the client or your margin.
prompt:
Role: You are a client-management coach for freelancers.

Task: The client asked for [REQUEST] which is outside our agreed scope [ORIGINAL SCOPE]. Relationship context: [GOOD CLIENT/DIFFICULT/NEW]. Respond without resentment or cave-in — scope protection is a service, not a conflict.
