Format: The reply — acknowledge the idea positively, name it as new scope matter-of-factly, offer the paths (add-on quote / swap for something in scope / phase 2); the change-order mini-template; and the prevention clause to add to future contracts.

title: Script a Difficult Conversation
author: PromptDeck
description: Helps you prepare and phrase a hard personal or work conversation with empathy.
prompt:
Role: You are a communication coach skilled in nonviolent communication.

Task: Help me approach a difficult conversation with [WHO] about [ISSUE], where I want [DESIRED OUTCOME] and I'm worried about [FEAR]. Keep it respectful and non-blaming.

Format: How to open; observation-feeling-need-request phrasing; 2–3 things to actually say; how to handle a defensive reaction; and what to avoid saying. Keep it calm and human.

title: SDR → AE Handoff Brief
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a clean handoff so the AE walks in knowing everything.
prompt:
Role: You are a sales operations designer.

Task: Turn my discovery notes into an SDR→AE handoff brief for the meeting with [PROSPECT]. Notes: [PASTE NOTES]. The AE should never re-ask what the prospect already told us.

Format: Company & contact snapshot; why they took the meeting (their words); pain + impact; current solution; decision process/timeline hints; landmines (competitors, politics, sensitivities); the promised agenda; suggested opening line for the AE.

title: Search Feature Implementation Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Implements search (DB full-text vs dedicated engine) sized to your needs.
prompt:
Role: You are a search engineer.

Task: Add search to [APP] over data [WHAT'S SEARCHED, VOLUME] with needs [FUZZY/FACETS/RANKING/MULTILINGUAL]. Recommend the simplest adequate tier: LIKE → DB full-text → dedicated engine; justify against my actual needs.

Format: The recommendation + why not the tier above/below; schema/index setup code; the query implementation with ranking; handling typos/synonyms at this tier; when I'd know it's time to upgrade tiers.

title: Seasonal / Holiday Campaign Ideas
author: PromptDeck
description: Generates timely campaign concepts tied to a season, holiday, or trend.
prompt:
Role: You are a campaign planner.

Task: Generate campaign ideas for [BRAND/PRODUCT] around [SEASON/HOLIDAY/EVENT] targeting [AUDIENCE] with goal [GOAL]. Avoid clichés every competitor will run.

Format: 6 concepts — each with a hook/angle, the offer or content idea, best channel, and effort level (S/M/L). Flag the one with the best effort-to-impact ratio and a 2-week execution sketch for it.

title: Seasonal SEO Content Calendar
author: PromptDeck
description: Plans content ahead of seasonal search spikes in your niche.
prompt:
Role: You are an SEO planner.

Task: Build a 12-month seasonal content calendar for [NICHE/BUSINESS], anticipating search spikes (publish 6–8 weeks before each peak).

Format: A table — Month to Publish | Target Season/Event | Keyword Idea | Content Type | Notes. Include evergreen-vs-seasonal balance advice and 3 quick-win seasonal updates to existing pages.

title: Security-Focused Code Review
author: PromptDeck
description: Reviews code specifically for security vulnerabilities with severity and fixes.
prompt:
Role: You are an application security engineer.

Task: Review the code below in [LANGUAGE/FRAMEWORK] for security issues — injection, auth/authz flaws, secrets exposure, unsafe deserialization, SSRF, XSS, race conditions, and dependency risks. This is my own code being reviewed defensively.

Format: A table — Issue | Location | Severity (Critical/High/Med/Low) | Exploit scenario in one line | Fix with code snippet. Then the top 2 to fix immediately. Code: [PASTE CODE].

title: Self-Review / Performance Write-Up
author: PromptDeck
description: Turns your accomplishments into a strong, evidence-based performance self-review.
prompt:
Role: You are a career coach who writes compelling self-reviews.

Task: Write my performance self-review for the period [PERIOD] in role [ROLE], based on accomplishments [LIST] and goals [GOALS]. Be confident and specific, backed by impact.

Format: Key accomplishments (with metrics/impact); how I lived the company values/competencies; growth areas framed constructively; goals for next period. Keep it honest and evidence-based.

title: Sell Internationally: Readiness Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Prepares a store for international orders: currency, duties, logistics.
prompt:
Role: You are a cross-border e-commerce consultant.

Task: Prepare [STORE] to sell from [HOME COUNTRY] to [TARGET MARKETS]. Products: [PRODUCTS]. Assess demand signals first, then operational readiness.

Format: Market prioritization (which first, why); the operational checklist — currency display, duties/taxes transparency (DDP vs DDU explained simply), shipping options and realistic costs, returns handling, translated content priority; the compliance items to verify per market; and a small-scale test approach before committing.

title: Seller's Personal Brand Post Plan
author: PromptDeck
description: Plans LinkedIn content that makes prospects recognize and trust a seller.
prompt:
Role: You are a social-selling coach.

Task: Plan 2 weeks of LinkedIn posts for a [ROLE] selling [PRODUCT] to [ICP]. Goal: familiarity and trust with prospects — not going viral. Draw on their daily conversations: [COMMON CUSTOMER QUESTIONS/OBSERVATIONS].

Format: 6 post outlines — hook, the insight from real customer conversations, and an engagement question; a 15-minute daily engagement routine; and 2 comment templates for prospects' posts that add value without pitching.

title: SEO Content Brief for Writers
author: PromptDeck
description: Creates a complete SEO brief a writer can follow to produce a rank-ready article.
prompt:
Role: You are an SEO editor briefing a freelance writer.

Task: Build a content brief for an article targeting [KEYWORD] with [SEARCH INTENT] for [AUDIENCE]. Include the angle, target word count, and the questions the piece must answer.

Format: Deliver — Working title options (3); Primary + 5 secondary keywords; Search intent; Suggested H2/H3 outline; Key questions to answer; Internal & external link suggestions; Tone & word count; A 'do not' list. Keep it skimmable.

title: SEO FAQ Schema Generator
author: PromptDeck
description: Generates a keyword-rich FAQ answering real 'People Also Ask' questions, ready for FAQ schema.
prompt:
Role: You are an SEO content specialist who writes for featured snippets.

Task: Generate an FAQ section for a page about [TOPIC] targeting [KEYWORD]. Produce 8 questions real users search (People Also Ask style), mixing informational and commercial intent, each answered in 2–4 useful sentences with related keywords used naturally.

Format: Output twice — (1) human-readable Q&A; (2) valid FAQPage JSON-LD schema ready to paste.

title: SEO Title Tags & Meta Descriptions (Bulk)
author: PromptDeck
description: Generates optimized title tags and meta descriptions for a list of pages or keywords.
prompt:
Role: You are an on-page SEO specialist.

Task: Write title tags and meta descriptions for these pages/keywords: [LIST PAGES OR KEYWORDS] for the brand [BRAND]. Titles ≤60 chars with the keyword near the front; meta descriptions ≤155 chars, benefit-led, with a soft CTA and the keyword used naturally. Avoid duplicate phrasing across rows.

Format: A table — Page/Keyword | Title Tag | Meta Description.

title: SEO/CTR Title A/B Variations
author: PromptDeck
description: Generates title-tag variations to A/B test for higher click-through rate.
prompt:
Role: You are a CTR optimization expert.

Task: For the page targeting [KEYWORD] currently titled [CURRENT TITLE], write 8 alternative title tags designed to lift click-through rate while keeping the keyword and staying under 60 characters. Vary the emotional angle (curiosity, benefit, number, urgency, authority).

Format: A table — Title | Angle | Why it may lift CTR. Recommend the top 2 to test first.

title: SERP Intent & Format Analyzer
author: PromptDeck
description: Deduces what Google rewards for a keyword and how to match it.
prompt:
Role: You are an SEO analyst.

Task: Based on these current top results for [KEYWORD] — [PASTE TITLES/TYPES OF TOP 10] — deduce the dominant intent and content format Google rewards, and how to fit while standing out.

Format: The dominant intent; the winning format & angle; table-stakes elements every top page has; 2 differentiation opportunities; recommended structure for my page.

title: Service Business Lead Magnet
author: PromptDeck
description: Creates a lead magnet that attracts your ideal service clients.
prompt:
Role: You are a service-business marketer.

Task: Design a lead magnet for my [SERVICE] business attracting [IDEAL CLIENT]. It must demonstrate my expertise on the exact problem I get paid to solve — a free sample of my thinking, not generic tips.

Format: 3 lead-magnet concepts (audit checklist / template / mini-diagnostic) each with title, what's inside, and why it pre-sells my service; the landing-page copy for the best one; the 3-email follow-up that bridges to a call; and the delivery format recommendation.

title: Sharp Meeting Agenda Builder
author: PromptDeck
description: Builds a tight, outcome-driven meeting agenda with time boxes and a decision list.
prompt:
Role: You are a chief of staff who runs efficient meetings.

Task: Create an agenda for a [DURATION] meeting about [TOPIC] with [ATTENDEES/ROLES], where success looks like [OUTCOMES]. Cut anything that isn't a decision or a discussion needing the group.

Format: A stated objective; a time-boxed table (Item | Owner | Minutes | Desired outcome); a list of decisions to make; pre-reads to send; a 2-line follow-up template.

title: Shipping Strategy & Policy
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs shipping options, pricing, and policy copy that converts.
prompt:
Role: You are an e-commerce operations consultant.

Task: Design shipping for my store: products [PRODUCTS/WEIGHT], margins [MARGIN CONTEXT], markets [DOMESTIC/INTERNATIONAL]. Decide free-shipping threshold vs flat vs calculated — shipping cost is the #1 cart killer.

Format: The recommended structure with the math (threshold set just above AOV); the policy page copy; the checkout messaging; international considerations (duties transparency); and how to test a threshold change safely.

title: Short Story / Narrative Generator
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes an engaging short story with structure, character, and a satisfying end.
prompt:
Role: You are an award-winning short-fiction writer.

Task: Write a [GENRE] short story of about [WORDS] words from the premise [PREMISE], featuring [MAIN CHARACTER] in [SETTING], with a [TONE] tone. Give the protagonist a clear want and obstacle; use show-don't-tell and natural dialogue; avoid clichés and purple prose.

Format: A complete story with a clear arc (hook, rising tension, turn, resolution) and a final line that lands.

title: Short-Form Video Hooks (Reels/TikTok)
author: PromptDeck
description: Generates scroll-stopping first-3-second hooks plus a short script skeleton.
prompt:
Role: You are a short-form video strategist.

Task: Generate 15 scroll-stopping hooks for a video about [TOPIC] for [AUDIENCE], mixing formats (bold claim, question, mistake, 'stop doing X', curiosity gap, result tease), each under 12 words. Then script the best one.

Format: The 15 hooks as a list, then a 20–35 second script skeleton for the best hook (hook → 3 value beats → payoff → CTA) with [B-ROLL] and [ON-SCREEN TEXT] notes.

title: Sincere Apology / Service Recovery Email
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a genuine apology email after a mistake or outage that rebuilds trust.
prompt:
Role: You are a communications lead handling service recovery, voice [VOICE].

Task: Write an apology to customers after [WHAT WENT WRONG], optionally offering [GOODWILL GESTURE]. Under 180 words, accountable, no 'we take this seriously' clichés or deflection.

Format: A clear non-defensive acknowledgement + impact; a sincere apology; the honest cause (briefly); the fix and prevention; the goodwill gesture (if any); a warm close.

title: Sincere Personal Apology
author: PromptDeck
description: Helps you apologize to someone you hurt — fully, without excuses.
prompt:
Role: You are a relationships counselor who knows real apologies have no 'but'.

Task: Help me apologize to [WHO] for [WHAT I DID]. Context: [WHAT HAPPENED]. What I'm tempted to defend: [MY EXCUSES — you'll help me drop these].

Format: The apology — naming exactly what I did, its impact on them, no justification, what I'll do differently, and the repair offer; delivery advice (medium, timing, their space to respond); what NOT to say (the excuse-shaped phrases); and how to handle it if they're not ready to forgive.

title: Site Migration SEO Checklist
author: PromptDeck
description: Creates a redirect and QA plan to migrate a site without losing rankings.
prompt:
Role: You are a technical SEO who has run many migrations.

Task: Create a migration plan for moving [FROM: domain/platform/structure] to [TO], covering redirects, canonical handling, and post-launch monitoring.

Format: Phased checklist — Pre-launch (crawl, mapping, benchmarks); Launch day (301 map rules, sitemaps, GSC); Post-launch (monitoring, common breakages). Include a URL mapping table template and the 5 most common migration mistakes.

title: Size Guide & Fit Content
author: PromptDeck
description: Creates size/fit content that cuts returns and sizing tickets.
prompt:
Role: You are an e-commerce content specialist fighting return rates.

Task: Create size/fit content for [PRODUCT TYPE] with measurements [SIZE DATA]. Common complaint: [RUNS SMALL/LARGE/INCONSISTENT]. Help shoppers self-select the right size confidently.

Format: The size chart with how-to-measure instructions (body vs garment measurements clarified); the fit-note copy for product pages ('runs X, size up if…'); a fit-finder question flow (3 questions → recommendation); and the review prompt that collects fit feedback.

title: Skill Gap Analysis for a Target Role
author: PromptDeck
description: Maps your skills against a target role and plans the fastest gap-closing path.
prompt:
Role: You are a career development analyst.

Task: Compare my current skills [MY SKILLS/EXPERIENCE] against what [TARGET ROLE] requires (infer from typical JDs + this one if provided: [PASTE JD]). Distinguish must-close gaps from nice-to-haves.

Format: A table — Skill | My level | Required level | Gap priority; the 3 highest-leverage gaps with the fastest credible way to close each (project > course); how to signal each skill on my resume/LinkedIn before I'm 'fully ready'; the gap I can reframe as a strength.

title: Slide Deck Design Makeover
author: PromptDeck
description: Redesigns cluttered slides into clear, presentable visuals.
prompt:
Role: You are a presentation designer.

Task: Redesign my slides for [PRESENTATION/AUDIENCE]. Current content per slide: [PASTE SLIDE CONTENTS]. One idea per slide; the slide supports the speaker, it isn't the script.

Format: Per slide — the single message (headline rewrite), what to cut, the visual treatment (chart/diagram/photo/big number), and layout note; a consistent style spec (fonts, colors, spacing); which slides to split or merge.

title: Social Bio Optimizer
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a punchy, positioning-rich social bio that attracts the right followers.
prompt:
Role: You are a personal-brand copywriter.

Task: Write a bio for [PLATFORM] for [PERSON/BRAND], who helps [AUDIENCE] achieve [OUTCOME], with proof [CREDIBILITY] and vibe [TONE].

Format: 5 bio options within the platform's character limit — each with a clear who/what/why and, where it fits, a CTA or link line. Recommend the strongest and why.

title: Social Listening → Insights Report
author: PromptDeck
description: Turns raw social mentions/comments into themes, sentiment, and actions.
prompt:
Role: You are a consumer-insights analyst.

Task: Analyze these social mentions/comments/reviews about [BRAND/TOPIC]: [PASTE RAW MENTIONS]. Cluster into themes with sentiment and surface what marketing should do about each.

Format: Theme table — Theme | Sentiment | Representative quote | Frequency feel | Recommended action. Then top 3 opportunities, top 2 risks, and 3 content ideas straight from customer language.

title: Socratic Tutor (Learn by Questions)
author: PromptDeck
description: Tutors you on a topic by asking guiding questions instead of just giving answers.
prompt:
Role: You are a patient Socratic tutor.

Task: Teach me [TOPIC] at [LEVEL] by asking one guiding question at a time and adapting to my answers, only explaining directly when I'm stuck. Correct misconceptions gently.

Format: Start with a quick diagnostic question, then proceed one question at a time. After I answer, acknowledge, correct if needed, and ask the next. Begin now with question 1.

title: Solo Travel Plan & Safety Kit
author: PromptDeck
description: Plans a solo trip with confidence: safety, logistics, and social moments.
prompt:
Role: You are an experienced solo traveler and safety educator.

Task: Plan my solo trip to [DESTINATION] for [DAYS], my profile [FIRST-TIME SOLO/EXPERIENCED, any specific safety concerns], style [BUDGET/COMFORT]. Confidence comes from preparation, not paranoia.

Format: The safety layer (check-in system with someone home, document copies, arrival-day rules, area research notes to verify); the itinerary with solo-friendly choices (counter dining, walking tours, hostels/social stays if wanted); the eating-alone and meeting-people playbook; the scam patterns for this region type to verify locally; and the 'trust your gut' permission slip.

title: Solve & Explain an Algorithm Problem
author: PromptDeck
description: Solves a coding/algorithm problem with approach, complexity, and clean code.
prompt:
Role: You are a competitive programmer and interview coach.

Task: Solve the problem below in [LANGUAGE]. Explain your reasoning before the code and analyze complexity.

Format: (1) restate the problem & constraints; (2) approach and why; (3) time & space complexity; (4) clean, commented solution; (5) edge cases handled; (6) one optimization if possible. Problem: [PASTE PROBLEM].

title: Spaced-Repetition Flashcards
author: PromptDeck
description: Turns material into clean question/answer flashcards for Anki-style review.
prompt:
Role: You are a memory coach who builds effective flashcards.

Task: Create flashcards from the material below on [TOPIC] for [LEVEL]. Follow good card design — one fact per card, clear cue, no ambiguity.

Format: 15 cards as 'Q | A' rows suitable for import (pipe-separated), ordered from foundational to advanced. Material: [PASTE MATERIAL].

title: Speech / Toast Writer
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a heartfelt, well-paced speech or toast for a personal occasion.
prompt:
Role: You are a speechwriter for personal occasions.

Task: Write a [LENGTH] speech/toast for [OCCASION] about [PERSON/RELATIONSHIP], including these memories/qualities [DETAILS], with tone [HEARTFELT/FUNNY/BOTH]. Written to be spoken aloud.

Format: An opening that grabs attention; 1–2 stories or qualities; a heartfelt turn; a memorable closing line/toast. Add a note on pacing and where to pause.

title: Speed-to-Lead Inbound Response
author: PromptDeck
description: Responds to a hot inbound lead fast with qualification built in.
prompt:
Role: You are an inbound SDR who converts demo requests.

Task: Write responses for an inbound lead who just [DEMO REQUEST/PRICING PAGE/DOWNLOAD] for [PRODUCT]. Respond within minutes, make booking effortless, and qualify lightly without interrogating.

Format: (1) instant email — acknowledge, one qualifying question, booking link, ≤80 words; (2) a same-day follow-up if no booking; (3) a phone opener if I call first; (4) the 3 light qualification questions woven in naturally.

title: SPIN Sales Discovery Questions
author: PromptDeck
description: Generates tailored SPIN discovery questions for a consultative sales call.
prompt:
Role: You are an enterprise sales coach.

Task: Build a discovery-call question set using the SPIN framework for someone selling [PRODUCT/SERVICE] to [BUYER ROLE] at [COMPANY TYPE]. Make questions specific, open-ended, and non-leading.

Format: 3–4 questions each for Situation, Problem, Implication, and Need-payoff. End with 2 questions to uncover budget and decision process without being pushy.

title: Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) Writer
author: PromptDeck
description: Documents a repeatable process as a clear, delegable SOP.
prompt:
Role: You are an operations manager who documents processes.

Task: Write an SOP for the process [PROCESS] performed by [ROLE], so a new hire could do it without asking questions.

Format: Purpose; when to use it; roles & tools needed; numbered step-by-step instructions (clear, imperative); quality checks; common mistakes to avoid; a definition of 'done'.

title: Startup Financial Model Outline
author: PromptDeck
description: Structures the assumptions and drivers for a simple startup financial model.
prompt:
Role: You are a startup finance advisor. (Educational guidance, not professional financial advice.)

Task: Outline a simple 12-month financial model for [BUSINESS] with revenue model [MODEL], price [PRICE], and key costs [COSTS].

Format: List the input assumptions; the revenue drivers and formulas; the cost categories; a month-by-month structure to build (rows/columns); key metrics to track (burn, runway, CAC, LTV, gross margin); and 3 assumptions to sanity-check.

title: Statistics Roundup (Link Magnet)
author: PromptDeck
description: Structures a statistics roundup page journalists love to cite.
prompt:
Role: You are a content strategist who builds linkable assets.

Task: Structure a '[TOPIC] Statistics [YEAR]' page targeting [KEYWORD]. I'll supply/verify numbers: [STATS or 'suggest the stat categories to research']. Make every stat citable with a clear takeaway.

Format: Title + meta; a key-stats highlight box (top 7); themed sections with stat + one-line insight each; methodology/sources note; embed/cite-us blurb; update cadence advice.

title: Stay Visible While Remote
author: PromptDeck
description: Builds visibility and influence when you're not in the office.
prompt:
Role: You are a remote-career strategist.

Task: Build my visibility plan as a remote [ROLE] at [COMPANY SETUP: hybrid office culture/fully remote]. My worry: [PASSED OVER/FORGOTTEN/PROXIMITY BIAS]. Visibility is deliberate communication of impact — not performative busyness.

Format: The impact-communication rhythm (the weekly written update format that leadership actually reads); meeting presence tactics (camera, speaking early, the follow-up note); the relationship map to maintain deliberately (skip-levels included); async artifacts that outlive meetings; and the office-visit strategy if hybrid.

title: Stock Photo Selection Art Direction
author: PromptDeck
description: Defines rules for choosing stock imagery that doesn't look like stock.
prompt:
Role: You are an art director who hates obvious stock photos.

Task: Define image-selection rules for [BRAND] used on [WEB/SOCIAL/DECKS]. Brand feel: [ADJECTIVES]. Audience: [WHO]. Kill the clichés (handshakes, pointing-at-whiteboard, too-perfect smiles).

Format: The selection criteria (lighting, candidness, diversity done authentically, composition, color grade); search-term recipes that surface non-cliché results; the banned-image list for our industry; treatment rules (crop, overlay, duotone) that unify mixed sources; and 5 example searches with what to pick.

title: Structured Brainstorm & Idea Filter
author: PromptDeck
description: Generates a wide range of ideas then filters them to the most promising few.
prompt:
Role: You are an innovation facilitator.

Task: Brainstorm ideas for [CHALLENGE/GOAL] under constraints [CONSTRAINTS]. Push for range — safe, bold, and unconventional.

Format: (1) 15 ideas grouped as Quick wins / Big bets / Wildcards; (2) score the top 6 on impact and effort in a table; (3) recommend 3 to pursue with a first small step each.

title: Student Report / Feedback Comments
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes personalized, constructive report-card comments efficiently.
prompt:
Role: You are an experienced teacher writing report comments.

Task: Write report comments for students based on my notes: [STUDENT: strengths / growth areas / effort / specifics]. Each comment: specific, constructive, growth-framed — no generic filler that any student could receive.

Format: Per student — a 3–4 sentence comment (strength with example → growth area with strategy → forward-looking encouragement). Keep tone warm and professional; vary the sentence structures across students.

title: Subcontractor / White-Label Brief
author: PromptDeck
description: Briefs a subcontractor so the work comes back right the first time.
prompt:
Role: You are an agency operations lead.

Task: Write a brief for a subcontractor doing [TASK/DELIVERABLE] for my client project [PROJECT CONTEXT — share only what they need]. Quality bar and voice must match mine; the client relationship stays with me.

Format: The brief — deliverable spec with an example of 'good', deadline with buffer (before my client deadline), the style/voice guide extract, what to ask vs decide alone, confidentiality basics, revision expectations, and payment terms; plus my QA checklist before it goes to the client.

title: Subscription / Replenishment Offer Design
author: PromptDeck
description: Adds a subscription option to products with the right incentives and retention.
prompt:
Role: You are a subscription commerce strategist.

Task: Design a subscribe-and-save offer for [PRODUCT] with natural replenishment cycle [USAGE CYCLE]. Margin room: [DISCOUNT ROOM]. Retention beats acquisition — design for month 6, not month 1.

Format: The offer structure (discount %, flexibility: skip/pause/swap); the product-page subscription module copy; the churn-moment playbook (before charge reminder? pause offers); the month-3 surprise-and-delight idea; and the metrics that matter (not just sub count).

title: Summarize & Extract Key Points
author: PromptDeck
description: Condenses a long document into a layered summary with action items.
prompt:
Role: You are a research analyst.

Task: Summarize the text below for a busy reader, faithfully and neutrally, without adding information.

Format: (1) one-sentence TL;DR; (2) 5 key points; (3) any decisions, numbers, or deadlines; (4) action items or open questions; (5) a one-line 'so what'. Text: [PASTE TEXT].

title: Sunset / Kill-a-Project Analysis
author: PromptDeck
description: Objectively evaluates whether to kill, fix, or continue a struggling initiative.
prompt:
Role: You are an unsentimental portfolio strategist.

Task: Evaluate whether to kill, fix, or continue [PROJECT/PRODUCT]. Invested so far: [SUNK COSTS — to be explicitly ignored]. Current signals: [METRICS/FEEDBACK]. Opportunity cost: [WHAT THE TEAM COULD DO INSTEAD].

Format: The sunk-cost trap called out; forward-looking case for each option (kill/fix/continue) with required belief for each; decision criteria and a deadline; if kill — a respectful wind-down checklist; a recommendation.

title: Supplier Vetting Checklist
author: PromptDeck
description: Vets a product supplier before you stake your brand on them.
prompt:
Role: You are a sourcing consultant.

Task: Help me vet suppliers for [PRODUCT] from [SOURCE: Alibaba/local/etc.]. Order size: [QUANTITY]. My brand risk: their quality becomes my reviews.

Format: The vetting checklist (business verification, sample protocol, defect-rate questions, lead-time consistency, communication test); the sample-evaluation scorecard; questions that reveal problem suppliers; payment-term safety (escrow, never full upfront); and the backup-supplier rule.

title: Support → Engineering Tooling Request
author: PromptDeck
description: Makes the case to engineering for tools/fixes that would cut ticket volume.
prompt:
Role: You are a support lead who speaks engineering's language.

Task: Write a request to engineering for [TOOL/FIX: admin capability, self-serve feature, bug fix] that would reduce [TICKET TYPE] tickets. Volume data: [TICKETS/WEEK, TIME PER TICKET]. Frame in their currency: recurring cost vs one-time build.

Format: The request — the problem with volume math (hours/month burned), the customer impact, the proposed capability (outcome, not implementation), the effort-guess ask, and the cost-of-doing-nothing line; sized for a ticket/issue tracker.

title: Support Chatbot Conversation Flow
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs a helpful chatbot flow with intents, responses, and human-handoff points.
prompt:
Role: You are a conversation designer.

Task: Design a support chatbot flow for [PRODUCT] covering the top intents [INTENTS]. Keep it helpful, avoid dead-ends, and hand off to a human when stuck.

Format: For each intent — trigger phrases; bot response; follow-up options; and the human-handoff condition. Add a friendly greeting, a fallback message, and a closing/CSAT prompt.

title: Support Macro / Canned Response Set
author: PromptDeck
description: Generates a reusable set of on-brand canned responses for common tickets.
prompt:
Role: You are a support operations lead, brand voice [VOICE].

Task: Create 8 reusable macros for [PRODUCT/SERVICE] covering: greeting/acknowledgement, password reset, billing question, refund request, bug received, feature request received, how-to, and closing/CSAT ask. Warm, clear, skimmable.

Format: A labeled list — each macro with a short label and a message body using [PLACEHOLDERS].

title: Support Reply Tone Rewriter
author: PromptDeck
description: Rewrites a technically-correct support reply into a warm, human one.
prompt:
Role: You are a support communication coach.

Task: Rewrite my draft reply to be warmer and clearer without losing accuracy. Context: customer is [CUSTOMER STATE: frustrated/confused/calm]. Draft: [PASTE DRAFT]. Kill the corporate phrases ('as per our policy', 'kindly note').

Format: The rewritten reply; a before/after phrase table showing the worst offenders fixed; and the one-line empathy opener matched to their emotional state.

title: Support Shift Handoff Note
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a clean handoff so the next shift never drops an ongoing issue.
prompt:
Role: You are a support team lead.

Task: Turn my shift notes into a handoff for the next shift. Notes: [PASTE NOTES: ongoing tickets, angry customers, incidents, promises made]. Nothing ongoing gets dropped; promises made to customers are sacred.

Format: Ongoing critical items (ticket, customer, state, the promise made, next action + deadline); watch-list (may escalate); incident status if any; unusual patterns noticed; and the one thing to do first when the shift starts.

title: Support SLA & Response Policy
author: PromptDeck
description: Defines realistic response/resolution targets and the policy language.
prompt:
Role: You are a support operations consultant.

Task: Define SLAs for [COMPANY/PRODUCT] with team size [TEAM], channels [CHANNELS], and volume [VOLUME]. Set targets we can actually hit — a missed SLA is worse than a modest one.

Format: The severity matrix (P1–P4 with examples from our product); response + resolution targets per severity and channel; business-hours definition; the customer-facing policy wording; the internal escalation triggers; and what to measure weekly.

title: Support Someone Who Is Grieving
author: PromptDeck
description: Helps you show up for a grieving friend without empty clichés.
prompt:
Role: You are a grief-support educator. (Human presence matters most — this helps you offer it, not outsource it.)

Task: Help me support [WHO] after [LOSS]. Our relationship: [CLOSENESS]. My worry: [SAYING THE WRONG THING/BEING INTRUSIVE]. Showing up imperfectly beats staying away politely.

Format: The first message (short, no fixing, no 'at least…'); what to say and the cliché list to avoid; the concrete help offer (specific, not 'let me know if you need anything'); the follow-up rhythm (grief outlasts the casseroles — week 6 matters more than day 3); and what to do on the hard dates ahead.

title: System Architecture Review
author: PromptDeck
description: Reviews a proposed architecture for scalability, failure modes, and simpler alternatives.
prompt:
Role: You are a principal engineer reviewing designs.

Task: Review this architecture for [SYSTEM/FEATURE]: [DESCRIBE ARCHITECTURE]. Expected scale: [USERS/RPS/DATA]. Check for bottlenecks, single points of failure, unnecessary complexity, and operational burden.

Format: Strengths; concerns ranked by severity (each with the failure scenario); a simpler alternative if one exists; questions the design must answer before building; overall verdict (ship / revise / rethink).

title: Tagline & Slogan Generator
author: PromptDeck
description: Generates memorable taglines and slogans with different strategic angles.
prompt:
Role: You are a brand copywriter known for sticky one-liners.

Task: Create taglines for [BRAND], which offers [WHAT] to [AUDIENCE] and wants to feel [ADJECTIVES]. Explore different angles: benefit, emotion, wordplay, bold claim, and simplicity.

Format: 12 taglines grouped by angle (under 8 words each), each with a one-line rationale. Then recommend the top 3 and where each works best (logo lockup, ads, hero).

title: Tailor My Resume to a Job
author: PromptDeck
description: Rewrites resume bullets to match a job description, optimized for ATS and impact.
prompt:
Role: You are a professional resume writer and ATS expert.

Task: Tailor my resume to the job below. Keep everything truthful to my input.

Format: (1) the top 10 keywords/skills the job emphasizes; (2) rewritten experience bullets in 'action verb + task + quantified result' form mirroring that language; (3) gaps between my resume and the job with how to address them. Job: [PASTE JD]. Resume: [PASTE RESUME].

title: Tailored Cover Letter
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a concise, specific cover letter matched to a job and your background.
prompt:
Role: You are a career writer.

Task: Write a cover letter for the job below using my background. Under 250 words, 3 short paragraphs, no clichés.

Format: Open with a specific hook (not 'I am writing to apply'); connect 2–3 of my achievements to their needs with a metric; match their tone; close confidently and low-pressure. Job: [PASTE JD]. Background: [PASTE HIGHLIGHTS].

title: Talking With Aging Parents (Care & Plans)
author: PromptDeck
description: Navigates sensitive conversations with parents about care, driving, and plans.
prompt:
Role: You are a family-caregiving counselor.

Task: Help me talk with my parent(s) about [TOPIC: needing help at home, driving safety, finances, care plans, wills]. What prompted this: [SITUATION]. Their likely reaction: [PRIDE/DENIAL/OPENNESS]. Their autonomy stays central — adult children advising, not managing.

Format: The framing that preserves dignity (collaborating on THEIR wishes, not taking over); conversation starters for this topic; what to ask vs what to wait on; handling 'I'm fine, stop fussing'; the sibling-alignment note if relevant; and the small first step that opens the door without overwhelming.

title: Team AI Usage Policy
author: PromptDeck
description: Drafts a practical AI usage policy for a team or company.
prompt:
Role: You are an AI governance consultant (practical, not fear-based).

Task: Draft an AI usage policy for [COMPANY/TEAM] of [SIZE] doing [WORK]. Concerns: [CONFIDENTIALITY/QUALITY/CLIENT DISCLOSURE]. Enable safe use — a ban just drives usage underground.

Format: The policy — approved use cases, prohibited data (client info, PII, secrets) with examples, the human-review rule for external outputs, client-disclosure guidance, tool-approval process, and who to ask; a one-page quick-reference version; and the 3 scenarios to train people on.

title: Team Hiring Plan & Sequence
author: PromptDeck
description: Plans which roles to hire, in what order, and why.
prompt:
Role: You are a startup talent advisor.

Task: Plan hiring for [COMPANY] at stage [STAGE] with budget [BUDGET], current team [TEAM], and 12-month goals [GOALS]. Sequence hires by what unblocks growth, not by org-chart symmetry.

Format: A prioritized hire list — Role | Why now | Impact if delayed | Rough comp band | FT/contractor call; the first 90-day outcome per hire; and 2 roles people usually hire too early.

title: Team Working Agreement / Culture Doc
author: PromptDeck
description: Drafts a practical team working agreement covering how you actually collaborate.
prompt:
Role: You are an organizational coach.

Task: Draft a working agreement for [TEAM] ([SIZE], [REMOTE/HYBRID/OFFICE]). Known friction points: [FRICTIONS: meetings, response times, decision rights]. Make it behavioral — things you can observe, not vague values.

Format: Sections — communication norms (channels + response expectations); meeting rules; decision-making (who decides what, disagree-and-commit); feedback norms; focus-time protection; how we change this doc. Each norm: one line + concrete example.

title: Technical Documentation Writer
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes clear developer docs (README, usage, examples) for code or a library.
prompt:
Role: You are a developer-experience writer.

Task: Write documentation for [PROJECT/MODULE] that does [WHAT], for developers who want to [GOAL]. Base it on the code/details below.

Format: A README with — one-line description; installation; quick-start example; core usage with code snippets; API/options reference table; common pitfalls; and a troubleshooting section. Details: [PASTE CODE/DETAILS].

title: Templatize Your Repeated Work
author: PromptDeck
description: Turns work you keep redoing from scratch into reusable templates.
prompt:
Role: You are a systems-thinking productivity consultant.

Task: Find my templatization opportunities. Things I create repeatedly: [RECURRING OUTPUTS: emails, reports, proposals, briefs]. Anything created 3+ times deserves a template with the thinking pre-done.

Format: The template-worthy list ranked by time saved; for the top 2 — the actual template built (structure + boilerplate + [VARIABLES] + the embedded checklist so quality survives autopilot); where to store them for actual reuse; and the template-decay warning (review quarterly or they rot).

title: Testimonial / Review Request Message
author: PromptDeck
description: Asks happy customers for testimonials in a way that gets specific, usable quotes.
prompt:
Role: You are a marketer who collects great social proof.

Task: Write a testimonial request for customers of [PRODUCT/SERVICE] who achieved [RESULT]. Use guided questions that produce specific, outcome-rich quotes (not 'great service!').

Format: (1) a short request email; (2) 4 guided questions (before-state, result, favorite part, who they'd recommend it to); (3) a one-click review-site version; (4) a thank-you + usage-permission line.

title: Testimonial Editing & Pull-Quotes
author: PromptDeck
description: Tightens raw testimonials into punchy quotes without changing their meaning.
prompt:
Role: You are an editor who handles customer quotes ethically.

Task: Edit the raw testimonials below for [PRODUCT] — tighten rambling, fix grammar, surface the specific result — while preserving the customer's authentic voice and meaning. Never add claims they didn't make; mark any suggested trim with brackets for approval.

Format: Per testimonial — the polished version, a ≤12-word pull-quote, and where to use it (homepage/pricing/ads). Raw: [PASTE TESTIMONIALS].

title: Thank-You Page That Keeps Momentum
author: PromptDeck
description: Turns the thank-you page into onboarding, sharing, or a next action.
prompt:
Role: You are a lifecycle copywriter.

Task: Write thank-you page copy after someone [BOUGHT/SIGNED UP] for [PRODUCT/LEAD MAGNET]. Confirm success clearly, set expectations (what arrives when), and use the high-motivation moment for one next action [NEXT ACTION].

Format: Confirmation headline; expectation-setting lines; the next-step block with CTA; an optional share/referral ask; support contact line.

title: The Budget Conversation Script
author: PromptDeck
description: Navigates 'what's your rate?' and budget talks without underpricing yourself.
prompt:
Role: You are a pricing-confidence coach for freelancers.

Task: Script my budget conversations for [SERVICE] with rates [MY RATES]. My weakness: [WHAT I DO WRONG: quote too fast, discount preemptively, apologize]. Whoever names a number first anchors — usually get their budget range before quoting.

Format: The deflect-and-discover script for early 'what do you charge?'; the budget-range elicitation questions (3 phrasings); presenting the price (pause discipline, no trailing justification); the silence rule; responses to 'that's more than we expected'; and the walk-away number math.

title: Thesis Statement & Argument Builder
author: PromptDeck
description: Sharpens a vague essay idea into a defensible thesis with argument structure.
prompt:
Role: You are an academic writing tutor.

Task: Sharpen my essay idea into a real thesis. Assignment: [ASSIGNMENT]. My rough idea: [MY IDEA]. A thesis must be arguable — someone informed could disagree.

Format: 3 thesis candidates (increasingly bold) with the strongest starred; the argument skeleton for the starred one (3 supporting claims + the evidence type each needs); the strongest counterargument and my rebuttal angle; and the 'so what?' test answer.

title: Thought-Leadership LinkedIn Article
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a credible long-form LinkedIn article that builds authority.
prompt:
Role: You are a ghostwriter for executives on LinkedIn.

Task: Write a thought-leadership article for a [ROLE] on [TOPIC/POV] for [AUDIENCE]. Take a clear, slightly contrarian stance backed by reasoning and a concrete example; avoid buzzwords and generic advice.

Format: A scroll-stopping title, a 1-line hook, short skimmable sections with subheads, one story/example, a clear takeaway, and a question to invite discussion. Around 600–900 words.

title: Thoughtful Gift Idea Finder
author: PromptDeck
description: Suggests personalized gift ideas across budgets based on the person and occasion.
prompt:
Role: You are a thoughtful gift concierge.

Task: Suggest gifts for [PERSON: relationship, age, interests, personality] for occasion [OCCASION], budget [BUDGET]. Avoid generic clichés; match their actual interests.

Format: 10 ideas grouped by budget (under/around/splurge), each with a one-line why-it-fits. Then 2 experience-based (non-object) options and 1 DIY/personal idea.

title: Topical Authority Roadmap
author: PromptDeck
description: Maps every subtopic needed to become the authority on a subject.
prompt:
Role: You are an SEO strategist who builds topical authority.

Task: Map full topical coverage for [TOPIC] in [NICHE]. Break it into subtopic silos and the articles each needs, ordered so foundational pieces come first.

Format: Silo → article list (working titles + target keyword + intent), a publishing order for the first 15, and internal-linking rules between silos.

title: Translate & Localize (Not Just Literal)
author: PromptDeck
description: Translates text while adapting tone, idioms, and cultural nuance for a natural result.
prompt:
Role: You are a professional translator and localizer.

Task: Translate the text below from [SOURCE LANGUAGE] to [TARGET LANGUAGE] for purpose [MARKETING/LEGAL/CASUAL] and audience [WHO]. Localize idioms, tone, and formality so it reads natively; preserve meaning and any [PLACEHOLDERS].

Format: The localized text, then a short note on any phrase with no clean equivalent. Text: [PASTE TEXT].

title: Travel Itinerary Planner
author: PromptDeck
description: Plans a day-by-day trip itinerary tailored to your interests, budget, and pace.
prompt:
Role: You are a well-traveled trip planner.

Task: Plan a [DAYS]-day trip to [DESTINATION] for [TRAVELERS], with interests [INTERESTS], budget [BUDGET], and pace [RELAXED/PACKED]. Group activities by area to reduce backtracking.

Format: A day-by-day itinerary (morning/afternoon/evening) with activities, rough costs, and food suggestions; a packing note; and 3 local tips. Flag that opening hours/prices should be verified.

title: Troubleshooting Decision Tree Builder
author: PromptDeck
description: Converts tribal debugging knowledge into a step-by-step triage tree.
prompt:
Role: You are a support-process engineer.

Task: Build a troubleshooting decision tree for the issue [COMMON ISSUE] in [PRODUCT]. Known causes and fixes: [WHAT USUALLY FIXES IT]. Order checks by frequency × ease — cheapest checks first.

Format: The tree in indented text (Check → if yes → / if no →) down to resolution or escalation; the customer-facing questions phrased simply per step; what info to collect before escalating; and the 2 steps agents most often skip.

title: Turn a Lecture Into Active Learning
author: PromptDeck
description: Converts passive lecture content into engaging activities.
prompt:
Role: You are an instructional designer who fights passive lectures.

Task: Convert my lecture on [TOPIC] for [AUDIENCE/LEVEL] into active learning. Lecture points: [PASTE OUTLINE/POINTS]. Keep total time [DURATION].

Format: A restructured session — for each lecture chunk: the 5-minute framing, then the activity (think-pair-share, problem, prediction, debate) with instructions and timing; checks for understanding; the closing synthesis activity; what to cut entirely.

title: Turn a Side Project Into Career Proof
author: PromptDeck
description: Positions a side project as compelling evidence for the role you want.
prompt:
Role: You are a career-narrative strategist.

Task: Position my side project as career evidence: the project [WHAT IT IS/DOES], its honest state [USERS/STATE], the role I'm targeting [TARGET ROLE]. Employers care about the decisions and follow-through it proves, not the project's fame.

Format: The skills-evidence extraction (what this project proves that my job can't); the resume bullet versions (with honest metrics); the interview story arc (decision → tradeoff → outcome → lesson); the 'is it dead though?' answer if I've stopped working on it; and what to build/finish next ONLY if it strengthens the target-role case.

title: Turn Commute Into a Learning System
author: PromptDeck
description: Converts daily commute or chore time into structured audio learning.
prompt:
Role: You are a learning designer for busy people.

Task: Build an audio-learning system for my [COMMUTE/CHORE TIME]: [MINUTES/DAY], goal [WHAT I WANT TO LEARN], attention reality [FULL FOCUS/HALF-DISTRACTED]. Passive listening without retrieval teaches almost nothing — build in recall.

Format: The content plan (what to listen to, in sequence, matched to my half-attention reality); the retrieval ritual (the 2-minute voice-note summary after each session); the weekly consolidation step; and what NOT to attempt while commuting (deep technical material that needs notes).

title: Turn Rambling Notes Into Clear Writing
author: PromptDeck
description: Converts messy voice notes or brain-dumps into clean, structured writing.
prompt:
Role: You are an editor who turns chaos into clarity.

Task: Take my rambling notes below and turn them into clear, organized writing for purpose [EMAIL/DOC/POST] and audience [WHO], keeping my intent and all key facts.

Format: A clean version with a logical structure and headings/bullets where useful, then a 2-line TL;DR at the top. Notes: [PASTE NOTES].

title: UGC Creator Brief (Paid Content)
author: PromptDeck
description: Briefs a UGC creator so the content converts and needs no reshoots.
prompt:
Role: You are a DTC creative strategist.

Task: Write a brief for a UGC creator making [N] videos for [PRODUCT] to run as [ORGANIC/PAID] on [PLATFORM]. The angle that converts for us: [KNOWN ANGLE or 'suggest 3']. Ads must be honest — no scripted false claims.

Format: The brief — concept per video (hook, beats, CTA), the do/don't list (claims allowed vs not), b-roll shot list, technical specs, usage rights summary, delivery format; plus 3 hook variations per concept for testing.

title: UI Motion & Animation Spec
author: PromptDeck
description: Specs purposeful UI motion: what moves, when, and how fast.
prompt:
Role: You are a motion designer for product UIs.

Task: Spec motion for [PRODUCT/FEATURE] with brand feel [ADJECTIVES]. Motion must explain (orientation, causality, feedback), not decorate. Respect reduced-motion preferences.

Format: The motion principles (3 rules); the spec table — Trigger | What moves | Duration | Easing | Purpose; the standard durations/easings palette; which transitions get NO animation and why; the reduced-motion fallbacks; and the 2 moments worth a signature animation.

title: UI States Pack (Empty/Loading/Error)
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs the forgotten states that make an app feel finished.
prompt:
Role: You are a detail-obsessed product designer.

Task: Design all non-happy-path states for [FEATURE/SCREEN] in [PRODUCT]: first-use empty, cleared/zero-results empty, loading, partial failure, full error, and offline.

Format: Per state — what the user sees (layout + copy), the action offered, and tone; skeleton vs spinner decisions; which states can share patterns; the one state most products get wrong here and how we won't.

title: Unstick a Procrastinated Task
author: PromptDeck
description: Diagnoses why you're avoiding a task and gets you started in 10 minutes.
prompt:
Role: You are a compassionate productivity coach who understands avoidance.

Task: I've been avoiding [TASK] for [HOW LONG]. When I think about starting, I feel [FEELING]. Diagnose the real blocker — unclear next step, fear of judgment, perfectionism, boredom, or wrong task entirely.

Format: The likely diagnosis (from my answers) with a check question; the 10-minute starter version of the task (absurdly small); the environment setup; the script for the inner critic; and the honest question of whether this task should exist at all.

title: Upsell & Cross-Sell Message
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes tactful upsell/cross-sell messaging that adds value for existing customers.
prompt:
Role: You are an account manager focused on expansion.

Task: Write an upsell/cross-sell message to an existing customer of [PRODUCT] who currently has [CURRENT PLAN/USE], recommending [UPGRADE/ADD-ON] because [RELEVANT BENEFIT]. Center it on their success, not our revenue.

Format: A short email (≤130 words) — acknowledge their progress, tie the upgrade to a goal they have, state the concrete benefit, and offer a low-friction next step. Include 2 subject lines.

title: Upwork / Platform Proposal Writer
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes platform proposals that stand out in a stack of 50 bids.
prompt:
Role: You are a freelance platform coach.

Task: Write a proposal for this job post: [PASTE JOB POST]. My relevant experience: [RELEVANT WORK]. The first 2 lines decide everything — address their specific problem, not my life story.

Format: The proposal — a first line proving I read the post (reference a specific detail), the mini-plan for their exact task (3 steps), one relevant proof point with a number, a clarifying question that shows expertise, and a clean close; under 150 words; no 'Dear Sir', no generic openers.

title: User-Friendly Error Message Writer
author: PromptDeck
description: Rewrites technical errors into clear, helpful, non-scary user messages.
prompt:
Role: You are an engineer who cares about UX writing.

Task: Rewrite the technical error [ERROR / SITUATION] into a user-friendly message for [PRODUCT], voice [VOICE]. Say what happened, why (simply), and what to do next — without blame or jargon.

Format: A primary message + a shorter toast version + optional 'details' text for advanced users. Give 2 options for the main message.

title: User-Generated Content Campaign
author: PromptDeck
description: Plans a UGC campaign with hashtag, prompts, incentives, and rights handling.
prompt:
Role: You are a social media strategist.

Task: Plan a UGC campaign for [BRAND/PRODUCT] to get customers creating content on [PLATFORM]. Goal: [GOAL].

Format: Campaign concept + branded hashtag options (3); the participation prompt/mechanic; incentive structure; 3 seed-content examples to model; a usage-rights request template; moderation notes; success metrics.

title: UX Microcopy & Empty States
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes clear, on-brand microcopy for buttons, errors, empty states, and tooltips.
prompt:
Role: You are a UX writer.

Task: Write microcopy for [FEATURE/SCREEN] in [PRODUCT], brand voice [VOICE]. Keep it concise, human, action-oriented; no jargon or dead-ends.

Format: Copy for — primary button (2 options); secondary/cancel button; success message; error message (helpful, not blaming); empty state with a nudge (2 options); tooltip.

title: Value Proposition Statement
author: PromptDeck
description: Crafts a crisp, differentiated value proposition and tagline options.
prompt:
Role: You are a positioning strategist.

Task: Craft a value proposition for [PRODUCT/SERVICE] for [WHO], whose main pain is [PAIN], key benefit [BENEFIT], differentiator [WHAT MAKES YOU DIFFERENT]. Avoid buzzwords; make the difference obvious.

Format: (1) a 1–2 sentence value proposition; (2) a '[For X who Y, we do Z, unlike W]' positioning statement; (3) 5 short taglines; (4) a one-line elevator pitch.

title: Vendor / Tool Selection Brief
author: PromptDeck
description: Structures a vendor or tool evaluation with criteria, questions, and a scorecard.
prompt:
Role: You are a procurement advisor.

Task: Help me choose a [TOOL/VENDOR CATEGORY] for [USE CASE] with budget [BUDGET] and must-haves [REQUIREMENTS].

Format: (1) 6–8 evaluation criteria weighted by importance; (2) a scorecard table template; (3) 10 questions to ask each vendor (including pricing traps and exit terms); (4) red flags to watch for; (5) a recommended shortlist process with timeline.

title: Video / Podcast → Content Package
author: PromptDeck
description: Turns a transcript into summaries, clips, quotes, and posts.
prompt:
Role: You are a content repurposing specialist.

Task: Process this [VIDEO/PODCAST] transcript into a content package for [AUDIENCE/CHANNELS]. Find the moments with clip potential — strong claims, stories, useful frameworks.

Format: A tight summary (5 bullets); 5 clip-worthy moments with timestamps and why they'd perform; 8 quote pulls; a newsletter section draft; 3 social posts from different moments; and the episode's single strongest idea for the title/thumbnail. Transcript: [PASTE TRANSCRIPT].

title: Viral LinkedIn Post
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes an engagement-optimized LinkedIn post with a strong hook and discussion CTA.
prompt:
Role: You are a personal-branding ghostwriter for LinkedIn.

Task: Write a post for a [ROLE] about [TOPIC/IDEA] with goal [AUTHORITY/LEADS/DISCUSSION]. Use a scroll-stopping 1-line hook, short mobile-friendly lines, a real story or example, one takeaway, and a comment-inviting question. Avoid buzzwords and 'Agree?' endings.

Format: The post with generous whitespace, no hashtags in the body, and 3 suggested hashtags at the end.

title: Web Scraper Design (Respectful)
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs a polite, robust scraper for permitted data collection.
prompt:
Role: You are a data engineer who builds respectful scrapers. (For sites/data you're permitted to collect — check the site's terms and robots.txt.)

Task: Design a scraper for [TARGET DATA] from [SITE/SOURCE TYPE] in [LANGUAGE]. Handle pagination, retries, and layout changes gracefully; throttle politely.

Format: Architecture (fetch → parse → store); the code skeleton with rate limiting and error handling; selector strategy that survives minor redesigns; data schema; monitoring for silent breakage; the compliance checklist.

title: Webhook System Design
author: PromptDeck
description: Designs reliable webhook delivery or consumption with retries and signatures.
prompt:
Role: You are an integrations engineer.

Task: Design the [SENDING/RECEIVING] side of webhooks for [SYSTEM/EVENTS]. Requirements: reliability, ordering tolerance, and security.

Format: Event payload schema (with idempotency key); signature verification scheme; retry policy with backoff + dead-letter handling; endpoint code skeleton (verify → enqueue → 200 fast → process async); the failure modes table; a testing approach with replay.

title: Webinar / Event Promotion Kit
author: PromptDeck
description: Creates the full promo kit for a webinar: landing copy, emails, and social posts.
prompt:
Role: You are an event marketer.

Task: Create a promotion kit for the webinar/event [TITLE/TOPIC] on [DATE] for [AUDIENCE], whose big takeaway is [PAYOFF].

Format: (1) landing-page copy — headline, 3 bullet promises, speaker blurb placeholder, CTA; (2) a 3-email invite sequence (invite, value reminder, last chance) with subject lines; (3) 3 social posts; (4) a post-event replay email. Keep every piece benefit-led.

title: Webinar Registration Page Copy
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a registration page that maximizes signups for a webinar or workshop.
prompt:
Role: You are a webinar-funnel copywriter.

Task: Write registration page copy for [WEBINAR TITLE/TOPIC] on [DATE] for [AUDIENCE]. Sell the transformation of attending, tease 3 specific takeaways, and reduce no-show intent from the start.

Format: Headline + subhead; 'what you'll learn' (3 curiosity bullets); who it's for / not for; host credibility block; date/time + CTA; 2 FAQ entries; a PS creating honest urgency.

title: Webinar Replay & Follow-Up Sequence
author: PromptDeck
description: Converts webinar attendees and no-shows with a smart follow-up sequence.
prompt:
Role: You are a webinar-funnel marketer.

Task: Write the post-webinar sequence for [WEBINAR TOPIC] pitching [OFFER with DEADLINE]. Branch for attendees vs no-shows.

Format: Attendees — (1) replay + recap + offer, (2) objection Q&A, (3) deadline close. No-shows — (1) 'you missed it' + replay, (2) best-moment clip + offer, (3) deadline. Subjects + bodies ≤140 words each.

title: Wedding Vows & Personal Promises
author: PromptDeck
description: Helps you write vows that sound like you, not a greeting card.
prompt:
Role: You are a vow-writing guide who extracts YOUR words. (The vows must be yours — I shape, you supply the truth.)

Task: Help me write vows for [PARTNER]. My raw material: how we met [STORY], what I love specifically [SPECIFICS — the weirder the better], the hard time we passed [MOMENT], my promises [WHAT I ACTUALLY COMMIT TO]. Length: [DURATION].

Format: The vows drafted only from my material — a specific-memory opening, the 'what you are to me' middle, 3–4 promises (mix of profound and delightfully small), and a landing line; [ADD DETAIL] markers where generic; and a read-aloud pacing note.

title: Weekly Client Status Update
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes proactive client updates that build trust and prevent panic check-ins.
prompt:
Role: You are a client-communication coach.

Task: Turn my raw progress into a client update for [PROJECT] at [STAGE]. This week: [WHAT HAPPENED — including any delays]. Proactive updates prevent the 'any update?' email that means trust is slipping.

Format: The update — progress in their terms (outcomes, not tasks), what's next with dates, anything I need from them (with deadline), and honest flagging of any risk before they discover it; friendly-professional tone; under 150 words; same day/time every week.

title: Weekly Meal Plan & Grocery List
author: PromptDeck
description: Creates a balanced weekly meal plan and grocery list (general wellness guidance).
prompt:
Role: You are a meal-planning assistant. (General wellness guidance, not medical/dietary advice.)

Task: Create a 7-day meal plan for goal [WEIGHT LOSS/MUSCLE/MAINTENANCE], preferences/restrictions [VEGETARIAN/ALLERGIES], ~[CALORIES] per day, cooking time [MINUTES]. Keep ingredients affordable and reused across days.

Format: Breakfast, lunch, dinner, and a snack per day; then a consolidated grocery list grouped by aisle. Note that anyone with medical conditions should consult a professional.

title: Weekly Plan & Time Blocks
author: PromptDeck
description: Builds a realistic weekly plan with time blocks around your priorities and energy.
prompt:
Role: You are a time-management coach.

Task: Build a weekly plan for me given my priorities [PRIORITIES], fixed commitments [COMMITMENTS], and working hours [HOURS]. Protect deep-work time and leave buffer.

Format: A day-by-day time-blocked schedule (table); the week's top 3 outcomes; deep-work vs shallow-work split; and one tip to protect focus. Note where to add buffer for the unexpected.

title: What Should I Delegate to AI?
author: PromptDeck
description: Audits your weekly tasks and identifies what AI can take over.
prompt:
Role: You are an AI workflow consultant.

Task: Audit my recurring tasks and identify what to delegate to AI tools. My weekly tasks: [LIST TASKS WITH ROUGH HOURS]. My tools: [TOOLS I USE].

Format: A table — Task | Hours/week | AI-delegable? (fully/partly/no) | How exactly (the workflow or prompt approach) | Time saved estimate; the top 3 to automate first with a starter prompt for each; and the tasks that should stay human with why.

title: WhatsApp / DM Commerce Setup
author: PromptDeck
description: Sets up selling and support via WhatsApp/DMs with saved replies.
prompt:
Role: You are a conversational-commerce consultant.

Task: Set up DM-based selling for [BUSINESS] on [WHATSAPP/INSTAGRAM DM] where customers ask about [COMMON QUESTIONS] before buying. Speed and warmth win DMs.

Format: The greeting + catalog-sharing flow; saved replies for the top 8 questions (price, delivery, COD, sizes, authenticity…); the order-taking script with payment options; the follow-up for 'I'll think about it'; broadcast etiquette (opt-in, frequency); and the response-time standard.

title: Which AI Tool for This Job?
author: PromptDeck
description: Recommends the right AI tool/model type for a specific task honestly.
prompt:
Role: You are an AI tooling advisor with no vendor loyalty.

Task: Recommend the right kind of AI tool for: [TASK/WORKFLOW]. My constraints: [BUDGET/PRIVACY/VOLUME/QUALITY BAR]. Cover chat LLMs, image tools, transcription, and automation platforms as relevant — and say when a non-AI tool is simply better.

Format: The recommendation with reasoning; the runner-up and when it wins instead; privacy/cost notes for my constraints; the workflow (how the tool fits my process); and the capability gap to watch for.

title: Workplace Conflict Navigation
author: PromptDeck
description: Helps you handle a conflict with a colleague or manager professionally.
prompt:
Role: You are a workplace mediator.

Task: Help me navigate a conflict with [WHO: peer/manager/report] about [SITUATION]. Their likely perspective: [THEIR VIEW if known]. My goal: [OUTCOME]. Assume positive intent until proven otherwise; protect the working relationship.

Format: A reframe of the situation from their side; the direct conversation script (opening, naming the issue without blame, the ask); if that fails — the escalation path done professionally; what to document; and the line between conflict and a genuinely toxic situation.

title: Write & Explain a SQL Query
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes an optimized SQL query from plain English and explains it with index tips.
prompt:
Role: You are a senior data engineer.

Task: Write a [POSTGRES/MYSQL] query for [DESCRIBE WHAT YOU WANT] against tables/columns [SCHEMA]. Use CTEs for readability and note any assumptions.

Format: (1) the formatted query; (2) a plain-English explanation; (3) performance notes and a helpful index; (4) one alternative approach if it exists.

title: Write a LinkedIn Recommendation
author: PromptDeck
description: Writes a specific, credible recommendation for a colleague or report.
prompt:
Role: You are a thoughtful manager/colleague.

Task: Write a LinkedIn recommendation for [NAME], who was my [RELATIONSHIP], highlighting strengths [STRENGTHS] and a specific example [EXAMPLE]. Make it credible and warm, not generic.

Format: A short recommendation — how I know them, 2–3 specific strengths with a concrete example, their impact, and a confident closing endorsement.

title: Write in MY Voice (Style Transfer)
author: PromptDeck
description: Teaches the AI your writing voice from samples so drafts sound like you.
prompt:
Role: You are a stylometric analyst and ghostwriter.

Task: Learn my writing voice from the samples below, describe it precisely, then write [NEW PIECE: topic/format] in that voice.

Format: (1) my voice profile — sentence rhythm, vocabulary register, punctuation quirks, humor, openings/closings, what I never do; (2) the new piece in my voice; (3) a self-check: 3 spots where it most sounds like me and 1 where it might not. Samples: [PASTE 2–3 WRITING SAMPLES].

title: X / Twitter Thread Writer
author: PromptDeck
description: Turns one idea into a tight, high-engagement thread with a strong hook and CTA close.
prompt:
Role: You are a viral X/Twitter ghostwriter.

Task: Write a thread about [TOPIC/IDEA] for [AUDIENCE] in a [TONE] tone. Tweet 1 promises a specific payoff and creates curiosity (no clickbait lies); each tweet is self-contained, under 280 characters, one idea, skimmable; include one example or mini-story; final tweet summarizes + CTA (follow/bookmark/reply).

Format: 6–10 numbered tweets.

title: Yoast SEO Green-Light Writer
author: PromptDeck
description: Produces an article engineered to pass every Yoast SEO and readability check.
prompt:
Role: You are a WordPress SEO specialist who optimizes for the Yoast SEO plugin.

Task: Write an article on [TOPIC] for the focus keyphrase [KEYWORD]. Hit every Yoast green bullet: keyphrase in the SEO title, slug, meta description, first paragraph, one subheading and image alt text; keyphrase density 0.5–2.5%; 30%+ sentences with transition words; under 25% of sentences over 20 words; a subheading at least every 300 words; Flesch reading ease above 60.

Format: Deliver SEO title, slug, meta description, suggested featured-image alt text, then the full article with H2/H3 markup.

title: YouTube Video Script Creator
author: PromptDeck
description: Creates a retention-optimized YouTube script with hook, chapters, B-roll notes, and CTA.
prompt:
Role: You are a YouTube growth strategist and scriptwriter.

Task: Write a [MINUTES]-minute script about [VIDEO TOPIC] for [AUDIENCE], using retention tactics: open loops, pattern interrupts, callbacks, and mini-cliffhangers. Spoken, energetic tone with no filler.

Format: (1) 3 title options + a thumbnail text idea; (2) a 10-second hook; (3) a chaptered script with timestamps and inline [B-ROLL] and [ON-SCREEN TEXT] notes; (4) an end-screen CTA and pinned-comment suggestion.

title: YouTube Video SEO Optimizer
author: PromptDeck
description: Optimizes a video's title, description, tags, and chapters for search.
prompt:
Role: You are a YouTube SEO specialist.

Task: Optimize my video about [VIDEO TOPIC] targeting search term [KEYWORD]. Balance clickability with search relevance.

Format: (1) 3 title options ≤60 chars; (2) a 150–200 word description with keyword in the first 2 lines, timestamps/chapters, and links placeholders; (3) 15 tags; (4) a pinned comment idea; (5) thumbnail text suggestion.
